topics.title: CMOS Amplifiers: Common Source Amplifier with Resistive and Active Loads, Common Source Amplifier with Source Degeneration, Common Gate and Common Drain Amplifier (only voltage gain and input and output impedances of the circuits)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT755 - MIXED SIGNAL CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** CMOS Amplifiers: Common Source Amplifier with Resistive and Active Loads, Common Source Amplifier with Source Degeneration, Common Gate and Common Drain Amplifier (only voltage gain and input and output impedances of the circuits)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 CMOS Single-Stage Amplifiers вҖ” The Big Picture

A **CMOS amplifier** is a single-transistor (or two-transistor) Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect-Transistor circuit biased in the **saturation region** that converts a small input signal variation (voltage or current) into a larger output signal, providing **power gain** while drawing current from a DC supply. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEECT755, Module 1), the focus is restricted to the four canonical single-transistor topologies, and **only the three small-signal parameters** of each topology are required:

- **Voltage Gain $\bigl(A_v\bigr)$**
- **Input Resistance $\bigl(R_{in}\bigr)$**
- **Output Resistance $\bigl(R_{out}\bigr)$**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Restriction (Module 1):** *Only* $A_v$, $R_{in}$, and $R_{out}$ are examinable for these four amplifier stages. Bias currents, bandwidth, and noise analysis are covered in later modules.

### 1.2 The Four Canonical Topologies

| Abbreviation | Topology Name | Input Node | Output Node | Common Node (AC Ground) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CS** | Common Source | Gate | Drain | Source |
| **CS-Active** | CS with Active (Current-Source) Load | Gate | Drain | Source |
| **CS-Degen** | CS with Source Degeneration | Gate | Drain | Source (with $R_S$) |
| **CG** | Common Gate | Source | Drain | Gate |
| **CD** | Common Drain (Source Follower) | Gate | Source | Drain |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” The "Water-Tap" Model of a MOSFET

Imagine a **MOSFET as a smart water tap**:

- The **gate voltage** $V_{GS}$ is how much you *turn* the tap handle.
- The **drain current** $I_D$ is the water flow out of the tap.
- The **transconductance** $g_m$ is the *sensitivity* of the flow to your hand movement вҖ” a higher $g_m$ means a small twist produces a big flow change.
- The **drain resistance** $r_o$ represents the *pipe friction* вҖ” when friction is high ($r_o$ is large), the flow is nearly independent of the downstream pressure.

Now think of an amplifier as a **pressure-to-pressure converter**: a tiny twist of the handle (small $v_{gs}$) creates a large flow change ($g_m v_{gs}$), and that flow is then pushed through an *output load resistor* to produce a *big pressure (voltage) change* at the drain. This is precisely the essence of a Common-Source amplifier.

### 1.4 Why These Four Topologies?

- **CS** is the workhorse вҖ” high gain, inverting, infinite input resistance.
- **CS with Active Load** swaps the resistor for a PMOS current source, giving **very high gain** in a tiny silicon area.
- **CS with Source Degeneration** adds a resistor at the source to **stabilise / linearise** the gain at the cost of a lower magnitude.
- **CG** is a **non-inverting** amplifier with **low input resistance** вҖ” useful for high-frequency or current-mode input signals.
- **CD (Source Follower)** is a **unity-gain buffer** with **low output resistance** вҖ” the perfect "voltage courier".

> [!NOTE]
> **Key CMOS Transistor Small-Signal Parameters (assumed known):**
> - **Transconductance:** $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \tfrac{W}{L} I_D} = \dfrac{2 I_D}{V_{GS}-V_{TH}}$
> - **Output Resistance:** $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \dfrac{V_A}{I_D}$
> - **Intrinsic Gain:** $A_{int} = g_m r_o$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Generic Small-Signal MOSFET Model (Hybrid-ПҖ)

Every analysis in this module uses the **small-signal hybrid-ПҖ equivalent** of the MOSFET, valid when the device is biased in saturation and the input signal is "small" ($\vert v_{gs} \vert \ll 2(V_{GS}-V_{TH})$):

- A **voltage-controlled current source** $g_m v_{gs}$ between drain and source.
- A drain-source resistance $r_o$ in parallel with the controlled source.
- **Gate draws no current** (ideal oxide insulation $\Rightarrow R_{in} = \infty$ for the device itself).

### 2.2 Four-Step Recipe to Solve Any Single-Stage CMOS Amplifier

1. **Draw the small-signal circuit**: replace $V_{DD}$ and the bias source at the gate with AC ground; replace the MOSFET with its hybrid-ПҖ model.
2. **Identify the input node, output node, and the "common" (AC-grounded) node**.
3. **Apply KCL** at the output node and at any internal node (e.g., the source node when $R_S$ is present).
4. **Compute the three required quantities**:
   - $A_v = \dfrac{v_o}{v_i}$
   - $R_{in} = \dfrac{v_i}{i_i}$
   - $R_{out}$ = resistance seen at the output with $v_i = 0$ and $R_L$ removed.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are the **exam-ready results** for a MOSFET in saturation with body-effect ignored ($v_{bs}=0$). Master the table вҖ” every Part-B question in this module is a *plug-in* of these.

| # | Amplifier Topology | Voltage Gain $A_v$ | Input Resistance $R_{in}$ | Output Resistance $R_{out}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CS with Resistive Load $R_D$** | $A_v = -g_m(R_D \Vert r_o)$ | $R_{in} = \infty$ | $R_{out} = R_D \Vert r_o$ |
| 2 | **CS with Active (Current-Source) Load**, load transistor has $r_{o2}$ | $A_v = -g_{m1}(r_{o1} \Vert r_{o2})$ | $R_{in} = \infty$ | $R_{out} = r_{o1} \Vert r_{o2}$ |
| 3 | **CS with Source Degeneration $R_S$** (assume $r_o \to \infty$) | $A_v = -\dfrac{g_m R_D}{1 + g_m R_S}$ | $R_{in} = \infty$ | $R_{out} \approx R_D$ (idealised, $r_o \to \infty$) |
| 3a | CS-Degen including $r_o$ | $A_v = -\dfrac{g_m(r_o \Vert R_D)}{1 + g_m R_S}$ | $R_{in} = \infty$ | $R_{out} = \bigl[r_o + R_S + g_m r_o R_S\bigr] \Vert R_D$ |
| 4 | **Common Gate (CG)** | $A_v = +g_m(R_D \Vert r_o)$ | $R_{in} \approx \dfrac{1}{g_m}$ | $R_{out} = R_D \Vert r_o$ |
| 5 | **Common Drain / Source Follower (CD)** | $A_v = \dfrac{g_m(r_o \Vert R_S)}{1 + g_m(r_o \Vert R_S)}$ | $R_{in} = \infty$ | $R_{out} = R_S \Vert r_o \Vert \dfrac{1}{g_m}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The **sign** of $A_v$ is critical in KTU exams:
> - **CS family** $\Rightarrow$ **inverting** (negative gain)
> - **CG** $\Rightarrow$ **non-inverting** (positive gain)
> - **CD** $\Rightarrow$ **non-inverting**, $\vert A_v \vert < 1$ (follower)

### 2.4 Key Design Insights (Why These Formulas Matter)

- **Resistive load vs Active load:** A resistor $R_D$ consumes a large DC voltage drop; a PMOS current-source load draws the same current with a much smaller drop, **freeing up headroom** and yielding a higher small-signal resistance $r_{o2}$ (tens of kО©) compared to a typical $R_D$ (a few kО©). This is why active loads give **higher gain**.
- **Source degeneration** trades gain for **linearity and bias stability**: the gain becomes a ratio of *resistors* ($R_D / R_S$) rather than a transistor parameter, so it is **insensitive to process, voltage, and temperature (PVT) variations**.
- **Common Gate** has a low $R_{in}$ вҖ” exactly what is needed to terminate a transmission line or to sense a current input. It is the input stage of choice in **RF low-noise amplifiers (LNAs)**.
- **Source Follower** has $R_{out} \approx 1/g_m$, which can be made very small (a few tens of ohms), making it an ideal **output buffer** that can drive low-impedance loads (e.g., a 50 О© cable) without losing signal level.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention used throughout:** lowercase $v$ denotes the small-signal (AC) phasor, $V$ denotes the DC bias quantity, $i$ denotes small-signal current. The body (substrate) is tied to the source so $\gamma = 0$ (no body effect), unless stated otherwise.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1 вҖ” Common Source with Resistive Load $R_D$

**Topology description:** Gate is the input, drain is the output, source is at AC ground. The load is a physical resistor $R_D$ connected from the drain to $V_{DD}$ (AC ground).

**Small-signal equivalent circuit:**
- Gate node: $v_i = v_{gs}$
- Drain node: $v_o$ connected to ground via $R_D \Vert r_o$ (the current source $g_m v_{gs}$ is between drain and source; $r_o$ is in parallel).

**Step-by-step derivation:**

KCL at the drain node (sum of currents leaving = 0):

$$i_{R_D} + i_{r_o} + i_{g_m} = 0$$

Express each current in terms of $v_o$ and $v_{gs} = v_i$:

- Current through $R_D$ (drain to ground): $\dfrac{v_o}{R_D}$
- Current through $r_o$ (drain to source = ground): $\dfrac{v_o}{r_o}$
- Dependent source current (drain to source): $g_m v_{gs} = g_m v_i$

Combining:

$$\frac{v_o}{R_D} + \frac{v_o}{r_o} + g_m v_i = 0$$

Solve for $v_o / v_i$:

$$v_o \left( \frac{1}{R_D} + \frac{1}{r_o} \right) = -g_m v_i$$

$$A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_i} = -g_m \left( R_D \Vert r_o \right)$$

**Input resistance:** Gate is connected only to the oxide; no DC path exists.

$$R_{in} = \frac{v_i}{i_i} = \frac{v_i}{0} = \infty$$

**Output resistance:** Set $v_i = 0 \Rightarrow g_m v_{gs} = 0$ (the controlled source becomes an open circuit). The resistance at the drain is then just $R_D \Vert r_o$.

$$R_{out} = R_D \Vert r_o$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick sanity check:** If $r_o \to \infty$ (ideal current source), $A_v \to -g_m R_D$. The negative sign confirms the **180В° phase inversion** between gate and drain.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 вҖ” Common Source with Active (Current-Source) Load

**Topology description:** The resistor $R_D$ is replaced by a **PMOS transistor $M_2$** whose gate is tied such that it behaves as a DC current source (saturated, with very high small-signal output resistance $r_{o2}$). The driver is $M_1$ (NMOS) with transconductance $g_{m1}$ and output resistance $r_{o1}$.

**Small-signal equivalent circuit:** At the output (drain of $M_1$ = drain of $M_2$), we see two resistances in parallel:
- $r_{o1}$ вҖ” output resistance of the driver $M_1$.
- $r_{o2}$ вҖ” output resistance of the load $M_2$ (PMOS).

The controlled source $g_{m1} v_{i}$ still flows from drain to source of $M_1$.

**KCL at the drain node:**

$$\frac{v_o}{r_{o1}} + \frac{v_o}{r_{o2}} + g_{m1} v_i = 0$$

$$v_o \left( \frac{1}{r_{o1}} + \frac{1}{r_{o2}} \right) = -g_{m1} v_i$$

$$A_v = -g_{m1}\,(r_{o1} \Vert r_{o2})$$

**Input resistance:** Still $\infty$ (oxide-isolated gate).

$$R_{in} = \infty$$

**Output resistance:** With $v_i = 0$, the controlled source vanishes; what remains at the drain is the parallel combination:

$$R_{out} = r_{o1} \Vert r_{o2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this is the *king* of single-stage gain:** Both $r_{o1}$ and $r_{o2}$ are typically tens of kО©, so $r_{o1} \Vert r_{o2}$ is still in the kО© range вҖ” yet the **DC voltage drop** across the active load is only $V_{DS,sat}$, far less than the $I_D R_D$ drop of a resistive load. This allows **stacking more stages** on the same supply rail.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 вҖ” Common Source with Source Degeneration

**Topology description:** A resistor $R_S$ is inserted between the source of $M_1$ and ground. The drain still has load $R_D$.

**Small-signal equivalent circuit:** Two nodes other than ground: gate (input, $v_i$) and source (internal, $v_x$). The drain is the output ($v_o$).

**Equations:**

Let $v_{gs} = v_i - v_x$ and let the source node have small-signal voltage $v_x$.

**KCL at the source node** (currents leaving the node):

$$\frac{v_x}{R_S} + \frac{v_x - v_o}{r_o} - g_m(v_i - v_x) = 0$$

**KCL at the drain node** (currents leaving the node):

$$\frac{v_o}{R_D} + \frac{v_o - v_x}{r_o} + g_m(v_i - v_x) = 0$$

#### 3.3.1 Simplified Case: $r_o \to \infty$

If $r_o$ is much larger than $R_S$ and $R_D$, the $1/r_o$ terms vanish, leaving:

**At source node:**

$$\frac{v_x}{R_S} = g_m(v_i - v_x)$$

$$v_x = \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}\, v_i$$

The effective gate-source control voltage is therefore "divided":

$$v_{gs} = v_i - v_x = \frac{1}{1 + g_m R_S}\, v_i$$

**At drain node (with $r_o \to \infty$):**

$$\frac{v_o}{R_D} + g_m(v_i - v_x) = 0$$

$$v_o = -g_m(v_i - v_x)\, R_D = -\frac{g_m R_D}{1 + g_m R_S}\, v_i$$

$$\boxed{A_v = -\frac{g_m R_D}{1 + g_m R_S}}$$

**Input resistance:** Gate is floating $\Rightarrow R_{in} = \infty$.

**Output resistance:** With $v_i = 0$, looking into the drain (ignoring $r_o$): the controlled source becomes zero, so the drain is connected to ground only through $R_D$.

$$R_{out} = R_D$$

#### 3.3.2 General Case: $r_o$ Finite

Keeping $r_o$, the result (after algebraic reduction, dropping intermediate algebra for brevity) is:

$$A_v = -\frac{g_m\,(r_o \Vert R_D)}{1 + g_m R_S + \frac{R_D + R_S}{r_o}}$$

**Output resistance** seen looking into the drain (with $R_D$ removed):

$$R_{out,\text{drain}} = r_o + R_S + g_m r_o R_S = r_o(1 + g_m R_S) + R_S$$

This is the famous **"resistance reflection"** formula: the degeneration resistor $R_S$ is "reflected" to the drain side, **amplified by the factor $g_m r_o$**.

The **total output resistance** with the load $R_D$ re-attached:

$$R_{out} = \bigl[r_o(1 + g_m R_S) + R_S\bigr] \;\Vert\; R_D$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Designer's rule of thumb:** Source degeneration increases $R_{out}$ by the factor $(1 + g_m R_S)$. This is *both* a curse (headroom) and a blessing (less sensitive to load variations).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 вҖ” Common Gate (CG) Amplifier

**Topology description:** The input is applied to the **source**, the gate is at AC ground, and the output is at the drain.

**Small-signal equivalent circuit:** Apply $v_i$ at the source node, gate grounded. The dependent source $g_m v_{gs}$ still flows from drain to source, but now $v_{gs} = -v_i$ (since gate is grounded, $v_{gs} = 0 - v_i = -v_i$).

**Voltage gain derivation:**

KCL at the drain node (currents leaving):

$$\frac{v_o}{R_D} + \frac{v_o - v_i}{r_o} + g_m(-v_i) = 0$$

Multiply by $r_o$ and collect $v_o$:

$$v_o\left(\frac{r_o}{R_D} + 1\right) = v_i(1 + g_m r_o)$$

$$v_o = \frac{(1 + g_m r_o)}{\left(1 + \dfrac{r_o}{R_D}\right)} v_i = \frac{1 + g_m r_o}{1 + \dfrac{r_o}{R_D}}\, v_i$$

For typical $g_m r_o \gg 1$ and $r_o \gg R_D$:

$$A_v \approx g_m(R_D \Vert r_o)$$

**Input resistance** (looking into the source):

Apply a test voltage $v_x$ at the source and measure the current $i_x$ entering. KCL at the source node (gate grounded, $v_{gs} = -v_x$):

$$i_x = \frac{v_x}{r_o} + g_m v_{gs} + i_{R_D,\text{branch}}$$

For a clean derivation with $R_D$ tied to AC ground through $V_{DD}$:

$$i_x = \frac{v_x}{r_o} + g_m(-v_x) + \frac{v_x - v_o}{R_D}$$

Using the gain expression (which gives $v_o \approx g_m R_D v_x$), and simplifying for $r_o \gg R_D$:

$$R_{in} = \frac{v_x}{i_x} \approx \frac{1}{g_m}$$

The exact expression including $r_o$:

$$R_{in} = \frac{r_o + R_D}{1 + g_m r_o} \approx \frac{1}{g_m} + \frac{R_D}{g_m r_o}$$

**Output resistance:** Set $v_i = 0$; the controlled source is killed (because $v_{gs} = 0$). The drain sees only $R_D$ and $r_o$ to ground:

$$R_{out} = R_D \Vert r_o$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 вҖ” Common Drain (Source Follower)

**Topology description:** Input at the gate, output at the source, drain tied to AC ground (typically to $V_{DD}$).

**Small-signal equivalent circuit:** Gate has $v_i$, source has $v_o = v_x$ (output). Drain is at AC ground, so $r_o$ is between the source node and ground.

**KCL at the source (output) node:**

The currents leaving the source node:
- Through $R_S$: $\dfrac{v_o}{R_S}$
- Through $r_o$ (drain to source $\Rightarrow$ source to drain at the source node): $\dfrac{v_o}{r_o}$
- Through the controlled source (current entering the source node from drain $\Rightarrow$ leaving the source node is $-g_m v_{gs}$): $-g_m(v_i - v_o)$

Setting the sum to zero:

$$\frac{v_o}{R_S} + \frac{v_o}{r_o} - g_m(v_i - v_o) = 0$$

Collect terms in $v_o$:

$$v_o\left(\frac{1}{R_S} + \frac{1}{r_o} + g_m\right) = g_m v_i$$

$$A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_i} = \frac{g_m}{\dfrac{1}{R_S} + \dfrac{1}{r_o} + g_m} = \frac{g_m(R_S \Vert r_o)}{1 + g_m(R_S \Vert r_o)}$$

**Input resistance:** $R_{in} = \infty$ (gate is isolated).

**Output resistance:** With $v_i = 0$, apply a test voltage $v_x$ at the source. Then $v_{gs} = -v_x$, so the controlled source pulls current $g_m v_x$ into the source node. The resistance is:

$$R_{out} = R_S \Vert r_o \Vert \frac{1}{g_m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check on unity gain:** When $g_m R_S \gg 1$, $A_v \to \dfrac{g_m R_S}{g_m R_S} = 1$. Hence the name **"Source Follower"** вҖ” the source voltage *follows* the gate voltage (with a slight attenuation).

---

### 3.6 Consolidated Results Matrix (Final Exam Cheat Card)

| Topology | $A_v$ | $R_{in}$ | $R_{out}$ | Inverting? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CS + $R_D$ | $-g_m(R_D \Vert r_o)$ | $\infty$ | $R_D \Vert r_o$ | Yes |
| CS + Active Load | $-g_{m1}(r_{o1} \Vert r_{o2})$ | $\infty$ | $r_{o1} \Vert r_{o2}$ | Yes |
| CS + $R_S$ (deg.) | $-\dfrac{g_m R_D}{1 + g_m R_S}$ | $\infty$ | $R_D$ (or full expression) | Yes |
| CG | $+g_m(R_D \Vert r_o)$ | $\dfrac{1}{g_m}$ | $R_D \Vert r_o$ | No |
| CD (Follower) | $\dfrac{g_m(R_S \Vert r_o)}{1 + g_m(R_S \Vert r_o)}$ | $\infty$ | $R_S \Vert r_o \Vert \dfrac{1}{g_m}$ | No |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification of CMOS Single-Stage Amplifiers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CMOS Single Stage Amplifiers] --> B[Common Source CS]
    A --> C[Common Gate CG]
    A --> D[Common Drain CD]

    B --> B1[Resistive Load RD]
    B --> B2[Active Current Source Load PMOS]
    B --> B3[Source Degeneration RS Added]

    B1 --> B1a[Inverting High Gain]
    B2 --> B2a[Inverting Very High Gain]
    B3 --> B3a[Inverting Stable Linear Gain]

    C --> C1[Non Inverting Low Rin Buffer]
    D --> D1[Non Inverting Unity Gain Buffer]
```

### 4.2 Common Source Amplifier вҖ” Topological Flow (Resistive Load)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn[Vin at Gate] --> nodeGm[Gm Stage gm vgs]
    nodeGm --> nodeOut[Vout at Drain]
    nodeBias[VDD] --> nodeRD[Load Resistor RD]
    nodeRD --> nodeOut
    nodeOut --> nodeRo[Output Resistance ro]
    nodeRo --> nodeGnd[Source to AC Ground]
    nodeIn -. AC Ground Gate Leakage .-> nodeGnd2[Infinite Rin]
```

### 4.3 Common Source with Source Degeneration вҖ” Signal Path

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputNode[Input Vin] --> gateNode[Gate]
    gateNode --> mosfetCore[MOSFET with vgs Vin minus Vx]
    mosfetCore --> drainNode[Drain Node Vout]
    mosfetCore --> sourceNode[Source Node Vx]
    sourceNode --> rsNode[Degeneration Resistor RS]
    rsNode --> gndNode[AC Ground]
    drainNode --> rdNode[Load Resistor RD]
    rdNode --> vddNode[VDD AC Ground]
    drainNode --> outNode[Output Vout]
```

### 4.4 Common Gate Amplifier вҖ” Signal Routing

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sourceInNode[Input Vin at Source] --> mosfetCG[NMOS Transistor]
    mosfetCG --> drainOutNode[Output Vout at Drain]
    mosfetCG --> gateCG[Gate Tied to AC Ground]
    drainOutNode --> rdCG[RD]
    rdCG --> vddCG[VDD AC Ground]
    sourceInNode --> rsCG[Optional Source RS to Ground]
    rsCG --> gndCG[Ground]
```

### 4.5 Common Drain (Source Follower) вҖ” Output Buffer Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cdInput[Input Vin at Gate] --> cdGate[MOSFET Gate]
    cdGate --> cdMosfet[NMOS Transistor]
    cdMosfet --> cdDrain[Drain Tied to VDD AC Ground]
    cdMosfet --> cdSource[Source Node Vout]
    cdSource --> cdRs[Load Resistor RS]
    cdRs --> cdGnd[Ground]
    cdSource --> cdVout[Output Vout Follows Vin]
```

### 4.6 Functional Comparison вҖ” Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CS_Family[CS Family Inverting]
        cs1[CS with RD] -->|g m RD| cs_gain1[Gain minus g m times RD parallel ro]
        cs2[CS with Active Load] -->|g m1 times ro1 parallel ro2| cs_gain2[Gain minus g m1 times ro1 parallel ro2]
        cs3[CS with RS Degeneration] -->|g m RD over 1 plus g m RS| cs_gain3[Reduced Stable Gain]
    end

    subgraph CG_Family[CG Family Non Inverting]
        cg1[Common Gate] -->|g m RD| cg_gain1[Positive Gain g m times RD parallel ro]
    end

    subgraph CD_Family[CD Family Unity Buffer]
        cd1[Source Follower] -->|Approximately 1| cd_gain1[Unity Gain Non Inverting]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block diagram abstracts away the transistor symbol and emphasises the *signal path* and the *AC-grounded nodes*. When drawing on the exam paper, you only need to show the **MOSFET symbol + three external resistors/transistors + DC supplies**, then convert mentally to the small-signal model for analysis.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam вҖ” May 2024 Model]**
For a Common Source MOSFET amplifier with a resistive drain load $R_D$, derive the expression for the small-signal voltage gain $A_v$. State the expressions for the input and output resistances.

**Model Answer (3 marks вҖ” to-the-point, formula-centric):**

The small-signal equivalent circuit consists of the controlled current source $g_m v_{gs}$ and output resistance $r_o$, with the drain connected to ground through $R_D \Vert r_o$. Applying KCL at the drain node:

$$\frac{v_o}{R_D} + \frac{v_o}{r_o} + g_m v_i = 0$$

Solving:

$$A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_i} = -g_m\,(R_D \Vert r_o)$$

The input resistance at the gate is **infinite** (oxide-isolated) and the output resistance is:

$$R_{in} = \infty, \qquad R_{out} = R_D \Vert r_o$$

The negative sign indicates a **180В° phase inversion** between input and output. **(3 marks вҖ” split: 2 marks derivation, 1 mark $R_{in}$/$R_{out}$)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam вҖ” Dec 2023 Model]**
A Common Drain (Source Follower) amplifier is often used as a buffer between a high-impedance source and a low-impedance load. Justify this application by writing the expressions for its voltage gain, input resistance, and output resistance.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Source Follower has **infinite input resistance** ($R_{in} = \infty$, due to the gate oxide) so it does not load down the high-impedance source. Its **output resistance is very small**:

$$R_{out} = R_S \Vert r_o \Vert \frac{1}{g_m} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{g_m}$$

so it can drive low-impedance loads effectively. The voltage gain is **near-unity and non-inverting**:

$$A_v = \frac{g_m(R_S \Vert r_o)}{1 + g_m(R_S \Vert r_o)} \;\approx\; 1 \quad \text{(for } g_m R_S \gg 1\text{)}$$

Thus, the signal at the source "follows" the signal at the gate with negligible attenuation, justifying its use as a **unity-gain buffer**. **(3 marks вҖ” 1 mark each for $A_v$, $R_{in}$, $R_{out}$)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each вҖ” Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part-B questions carry 14 marks with **internal choice** (either Q(A) OR Q(B)). Each part is split into (a) вҖ” 7 marks and (b) вҖ” 7 marks. Full derivations and diagrams are expected.

---

#### **Question A вҖ” Option 1 [KTU University Exam вҖ” May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** For a **Common Source amplifier with a resistive load $R_D$**, derive the small-signal voltage gain, input resistance, and output resistance using the hybrid-ПҖ model. **Include $r_o$ in your analysis.** (7 marks)

**(b)** A **Common Source stage has source degeneration resistor $R_S$**. Assuming $r_o \to \infty$ for simplicity, derive the new voltage gain expression. Show that the gain becomes approximately $-R_D/R_S$ for large $g_m R_S$, and explain the practical significance of this result. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) CS with Resistive Load вҖ” full derivation (7 marks):**

1. **Draw the small-signal model** (1 mark): Replace the MOSFET with $g_m v_{gs}$ in parallel with $r_o$; replace $V_{DD}$ with AC ground; the gate is $v_i$ and the drain is $v_o$. The source is at AC ground.

2. **Write the KCL at the drain node** (2 marks):

The current $g_m v_{gs}$ flows from drain to source (i.e., *out* of the drain node). Currents leaving the drain node are $\frac{v_o}{R_D}$ and $\frac{v_o}{r_o}$:

$$\frac{v_o}{R_D} + \frac{v_o}{r_o} + g_m v_{gs} = 0$$

3. **Substitute $v_{gs} = v_i$** (source is grounded) and solve (2 marks):

$$v_o \left( \frac{1}{R_D} + \frac{1}{r_o} \right) = -g_m v_i$$

$$A_v = \frac{v_o}{v_i} = -g_m\,(R_D \Vert r_o)$$

4. **State $R_{in}$ and $R_{out}$** (2 marks): $R_{in} = \infty$ (no gate current). For $R_{out}$, set $v_i = 0$ (controlled source becomes an open circuit); the resistance seen at the drain is $R_D$ in parallel with $r_o$:

$$R_{in} = \infty, \qquad R_{out} = R_D \Vert r_o$$

**(b) CS with Source Degeneration (7 marks):**

1. **Draw the small-signal model** (1 mark): Source node now has voltage $v_x$; $R_S$ from source to ground; gate is $v_i$. Apply KCL at the source node (with $r_o \to \infty$):

$$\frac{v_x}{R_S} = g_m(v_i - v_x)$$

2. **Solve for $v_x$** (1.5 marks):

$$v_x(1 + g_m R_S) = g_m R_S v_i \quad\Rightarrow\quad v_x = \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}\, v_i$$

3. **Find $v_{gs}$** (1 mark): $v_{gs} = v_i - v_x = \dfrac{v_i}{1 + g_m R_S}$

4. **KCL at the drain node** (1.5 marks): Output current $g_m v_{gs}$ flows through $R_D$:

$$v_o = -g_m v_{gs} R_D = -\frac{g_m R_D}{1 + g_m R_S}\, v_i$$

$$\boxed{A_v = -\frac{g_m R_D}{1 + g_m R_S}}$$

5. **Limiting case** (1 mark): For $g_m R_S \gg 1$:

$$A_v \approx -\frac{R_D}{R_S}$$

6. **Practical significance** (1 mark): The gain now depends only on the *ratio of two passive resistors*, **not on $g_m$**. Since $g_m$ varies with process, voltage, and temperature, removing it from the gain expression makes the amplifier **PVT-stable and linear** вҖ” at the cost of a lower gain magnitude.

---

#### **Question B вҖ” Option 2 [KTU University Exam вҖ” Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Draw the small-signal equivalent circuit of a **Common Source amplifier with an active (current-source) load**, and derive the expressions for voltage gain, input resistance, and output resistance. Compare this with the resistive-load case. (7 marks)

**(b)** For a **Common Gate amplifier**, derive the small-signal voltage gain and the input resistance looking into the source terminal. Why is the CG stage preferred over the CS stage at very high frequencies? (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) CS with Active Load (7 marks):**

1. **Draw the circuit** (1 mark): Driver $M_1$ (NMOS, $g_{m1}, r_{o1}$) and load $M_2$ (PMOS current source, $r_{o2}$). The output node is the common drain of $M_1$ and $M_2$.

2. **Small-signal model** (1 mark): The drain of $M_1$ sees $r_{o1}$ and $r_{o2}$ to AC ground (small-signal). The controlled source $g_{m1} v_{gs1}$ flows from drain to source of $M_1$.

3. **KCL at the output node** (2 marks):

$$v_o \left( \frac{1}{r_{o1}} + \frac{1}{r_{o2}} \right) + g_{m1} v_i = 0$$

4. **Solve for $A_v$** (1.5 marks):

$$A_v = -g_{m1}\,(r_{o1} \Vert r_{o2})$$

5. **$R_{in} = \infty$**, **$R_{out} = r_{o1} \Vert r_{o2}$** (0.5 marks).

6. **Comparison with resistive load** (1 mark): The active load replaces $R_D$ with the much larger $r_{o2}$, so $\vert A_v \vert$ is much higher. Also, the active load drops only $V_{OV}$ of DC headroom, leaving more room for signal swing.

**(b) Common Gate Amplifier (7 marks):**

1. **Small-signal model** (1 mark): Gate at AC ground, input at source ($v_i = v_x$), output at drain ($v_o$). $v_{gs} = -v_i$.

2. **Voltage gain derivation** (3 marks):

KCL at the drain node:

$$\frac{v_o}{R_D} + \frac{v_o - v_i}{r_o} + g_m(-v_i) = 0$$

Solving:

$$v_o \left( \frac{1}{R_D} + \frac{1}{r_o} \right) = v_i \left( g_m + \frac{1}{r_o} \right)$$

For $g_m r_o \gg 1$:

$$\boxed{A_v \approx +g_m(R_D \Vert r_o)}$$

3. **Input resistance** (2 marks): Apply test voltage $v_x$ at source, KCL at source:

$$i_x = g_m v_x + \frac{v_x}{r_o} + \text{(load branch)}$$

After algebra, for $r_o \gg R_D$:

$$R_{in} = \frac{r_o + R_D}{1 + g_m r_o} \approx \frac{1}{g_m}$$

4. **Why CG is preferred at high frequencies** (1 mark): The **Miller effect** at the gate-drain capacitance $C_{gd}$ is *eliminated* in the CG configuration (because the gate is grounded and the source is the input вҖ” the voltage across $C_{gd}$ does not get multiplied). This makes the CG stage the **input stage of choice in RF LNAs and high-frequency current-mode circuits**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in this module вҖ” read carefully:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign of $A_v$:** CS stages are *inverting* ($A_v < 0$); CG and CD are *non-inverting* ($A_v > 0$). Losing 1 mark for missing the sign is very common.
> 2. **Ignoring $r_o$ in $R_{out}$:** For a CS with active load, the output resistance is **not** $\infty$ вҖ” it is $r_{o1} \Vert r_{o2}$. Many students write $R_{out} = \infty$.
> 3. **Conflating "input resistance of the stage" with "input resistance looking into the source":** For CS-family topologies, the input is at the gate, so $R_{in} = \infty$ always. For CG, the input is at the source, so $R_{in} \approx 1/g_m$. **Read the question carefully!**
> 4. **Source Follower gain is *not* exactly 1:** It is $\dfrac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}$, which is slightly less than unity. Writing $A_v = 1$ costs a mark.
> 5. **Source Degeneration $A_v$ formula:** The denominator is $1 + g_m R_S$, **not** $1 + g_m R_D$ and **not** $1 + g_m(R_S + R_D)$. This is one of the most-frequently-flipped errors.
> 6. **Body effect is *not* in the KTU 2024 syllabus** for this module вҖ” do not introduce $\gamma$ or $g_{mb}$ unless the question explicitly mentions it.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Five single-stage CMOS amplifier topologies** form the heart of this module: **CS**, **CS + Active Load**, **CS + Degeneration**, **CG**, **CD**.
- **CS (resistive load):** $A_v = -g_m(R_D \Vert r_o)$, $R_{in} = \infty$, $R_{out} = R_D \Vert r_o$. Inverting, high gain.
- **CS (active load):** $A_v = -g_{m1}(r_{o1} \Vert r_{o2})$, $R_{in} = \infty$, $R_{out} = r_{o1} \Vert r_{o2}$. Very high gain, small DC drop.
- **CS (source degeneration):** $A_v \approx -\dfrac{g_m R_D}{1 + g_m R_S}$, $R_{in} = \infty$, $R_{out}$ increases by factor $(1 + g_m R_S)$. Trade gain for linearity/PVT stability.
- **Common Gate:** $A_v = +g_m(R_D \Vert r_o)$ (**non-inverting**), $R_{in} \approx 1/g_m$, $R_{out} = R_D \Vert r_o$. Low $R_{in}$, no Miller effect, ideal for HF.
- **Common Drain (Source Follower):** $A_v = \dfrac{g_m(R_S \Vert r_o)}{1 + g_m(R_S \Vert r_o)} \approx 1$, $R_{in} = \infty$, $R_{out} = R_S \Vert r_o \Vert 1/g_m \approx 1/g_m$. Unity-gain buffer, low $R_{out}$, ideal output stage.
- **The "$-1$" in $1 + g_m R_S$ comes from voltage-divider feedback at the source node** вҖ” this is the single most-tested algebraic identity in this module.
- **Active load > Resistive load** in terms of gain-per-volt of headroom.
- **Source degeneration makes gain = $-R_D/R_S$** in the limit вҖ” a *resistor ratio* that is process-independent.
- **CG is non-inverting and has $R_{in} \approx 1/g_m$** вҖ” don't confuse it with CS inverting stages.
- **Source Follower is non-inverting and has $R_{out} \approx 1/g_m$** вҖ” the lowest output resistance of all five topologies.
- **In the KTU exam, always draw the small-signal model and label $g_m v_{gs}$, $r_o$, and the nodes** вҖ” this alone can earn you 1вҖ“2 marks of partial credit even if your final algebra has an error.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cascode Amplifier: Cascoded Amplifier with Cascade Loads, Folded Cascode Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT755 - MIXED SIGNAL CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cascode Amplifier: Cascoded Amplifier with Cascade Loads, Folded Cascode Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Cascode Amplifier** is a two-stage amplifier configuration obtained by cascading a Common Source (CS) stage with a Common Gate (CG) stage, where the input is applied to the gate of the CS transistor and the output is taken from the drain of the CG transistor. The two transistors are stacked such that the drain of the input device feeds directly into the source of the load device. This topology decouples the Miller multiplication effect of $C_{gd}$ from the input node, yielding exceptional high-frequency performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):** "A cascode amplifier consists of a common-source (CS) stage feeding a common-gate (CG) stage. The cascode provides high output impedance, high gain, and excellent high-frequency response by isolating the input and output nodes."

### 1.2 Cascoded Amplifier with Cascade Loads

A **Cascoded Amplifier with Cascade Loads** (also called a *telescopic cascode* with cascode current-mirror loads) refers to a differential cascode topology in which the active loads themselves are realized using a cascode current mirror. Instead of a simple current source or diode-connected load, both the input transistors and the load transistors are cascoded. This produces an output impedance that is the product of four transconductances, achieving **maximum DC gain per unit current** in CMOS op-amp design.

### 1.3 Folded Cascode Amplifier

A **Folded Cascode Amplifier** is a modification of the telescopic cascode where the signal current from the input differential pair is "folded" back through PMOS common-gate transistors to a separate output branch supplied by a different current source. This frees up voltage headroom at the output, allowing larger output voltage swings вҖ” a critical advantage in low-voltage (sub-1.8V) mixed-signal design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction:** Telescopic cascode stacks NMOS and PMOS devices vertically (limited swing). Folded cascode "folds" the signal path so input and output branches share a common current source, doubling the available output swing.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” The Two-Valve Water Tower

Imagine two water valves stacked vertically in a pipeline:

- The **lower valve** (Common Source) receives the input water-pressure signal and controls the main flow.
- The **upper valve** (Common Gate) does not see the input signal directly; it merely regulates the existing flow, isolating the lower valve from pressure changes downstream.

In a regular single-valve system, any pressure surge at the output *pushes back* on the input valve through the connecting pipe (the **Miller effect**), causing oscillations and instability. By inserting the second valve, the back-pressure cannot reach the input, giving **stable, high-gain, high-bandwidth** flow control.

- The **Cascade Load** is like adding *valves to the output pipe too* вҖ” now flow is controlled from both ends, giving extreme precision (huge output impedance, $r_{out} \to$ product of four $r_o$'s).
- The **Folded Cascode** is like redirecting the water sideways into a *separate* downstream pipe once it passes the first valve. This frees vertical space (voltage headroom) and allows for **larger output swing** without spilling water (saturation).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** *Cascode = Cascade + Triode*. It is literally a cascade of two triode-region FETs, each protecting the next.

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Thermal voltage: $V_T = \mathbf{26 \text{ mV}}$ at **300 K**
- Transconductance parameter: $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_D}$
- Early voltage: $V_A$
- Output resistance of a single transistor: $r_o = \dfrac{V_A}{I_D}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Analysis вҖ” Step-by-Step Logic

#### A. Standard Cascode (CS + CG) Topology

1. **Input stage (M1 вҖ“ Common Source):** The input signal $v_{in}$ applied to the gate of M1 creates a small-signal drain current $i_{d1} = g_{m1} v_{gs1}$.
2. **Coupling node (Source of M2):** Since M2's gate is AC grounded (biased at a constant voltage), its source voltage moves with the drain of M1. The small-signal current is forced *through* M2 to the output.
3. **Output stage (M2 вҖ“ Common Gate):** M2 provides a low input impedance ($1/g_{m2}$) looking into its source, which acts as a buffer for M1's drain.
4. **Miller effect suppression:** The effective capacitance from the input node to ground is $C_{gs1} + C_{gd1}(1 - A_{v2})$, but since $A_{v2} \approx 1$ (CG has unity current gain), the Miller multiplier becomes negligible.
5. **Output collection:** The total current $i_{d1}$ flows into the output node, producing $v_{out} = -i_{d1} \cdot R_{out}$, where $R_{out}$ is now extremely large.

#### B. Cascode with Cascade Loads

1. Replace the simple current-source load with a **cascode current mirror** (M3вҖ“M4, where M3 is diode-connected and M4 is the cascode device).
2. The impedance looking back into the mirror is $r_{o3} + r_{o4} + g_{m4} r_{o4} r_{o3} \approx g_{m4} r_{o4} r_{o3}$.
3. The total output impedance is the parallel combination of the *NMOS cascode branch* and the *PMOS cascode load branch*.
4. Voltage gain scales with the **product of all four output resistances**.

#### C. Folded Cascode

1. A PMOS differential pair (M1, M2) acts as the input transconductor.
2. The tail current $I_{SS}$ splits; the AC signal current is steered from one branch and **folded** into the source of an NMOS cascode transistor (M3, M4).
3. The output branch is biased by an additional current source $I_{bias}$, allowing independent biasing of the input pair and the output branch.
4. The output impedance is $R_{out} = (g_{m3} r_{o3} r_{o1}) \parallel (g_{m5} r_{o5} r_{o7})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Folded"?** Because the signal current that would normally flow *down* through an NMOS cascode is instead routed *sideways* into a PMOS-NMOS combination вҖ” the current path literally folds at the source of M3.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value bars in the table below use `\vert` to prevent breakage of the markdown pipe syntax.

| **#** | **Quantity** | **Expression** | **Typical Approximation** | **Units** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | Small-signal gain, simple cascode | $A_v = -g_{m1} \cdot (g_{m2} r_{o2} r_{o1} \,\vert\, r_{oL})$ | $A_v \approx -g_{m1} \cdot g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2}$ | V/V |
| 2 | Output resistance, simple cascode (no load) | $R_{out} = r_{o1} + r_{o2} + g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2}$ | $R_{out} \approx g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2}$ | $\Omega$ |
| 3 | Intrinsic gain of single transistor | $A_{int} = g_m r_o$ | $A_{int} \approx 10$вҖ“$50$ | V/V |
| 4 | Cascode gain | $A_v \approx (g_m r_o)^2$ | $A_v \approx 100$вҖ“$2500$ | V/V |
| 5 | Telescopic cascode with cascade load gain | $A_v \approx (g_m r_o)^4$ | $A_v \approx 10^4$ | V/V |
| 6 | Folded cascode output resistance | $R_{out} = (g_{m3} r_{o3} r_{o1}) \,\vert\vert\, (g_{m5} r_{o5} r_{o7})$ | $R_{out} \approx \tfrac{1}{2} g_m^2 r_o^3$ | $\Omega$ |
| 7 | Folded cascode DC gain | $A_v = g_{m1,input} \cdot R_{out}$ | $A_v \approx g_{m1} g_m^2 r_o^3$ | V/V |
| 8 | 3-dB bandwidth (dominant pole) | $f_{-3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_{out} C_L}$ | Limited by load cap $C_L$ | Hz |
| 9 | Input pole (Miller-suppressed) | $f_{in} = \dfrac{g_{m2}}{2 \pi C_{gd1}}$ | Much higher than CS amp | Hz |
| 10 | Output swing (telescopic) | $V_{out,swing} = 2(V_{DD} - V_{ov,total})$ | 4 overdrives dropped | V |
| 11 | Output swing (folded) | $V_{out,swing} \approx 2(V_{DD} - V_{ov})$ | 2 overdrives dropped | V |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Op-Amps & Comparators:** Every modern CMOS op-amp (e.g., the famous **Miller-compensated two-stage** or **folded-cascode** op-amps) uses one of these topologies in its first stage.
- **RF LNAs:** Cascode LNAs provide high gain with excellent reverse isolation ($S_{12}$), preventing local-oscillator leakage in receivers.
- **Sample-and-Hold Circuits:** Folded cascode input stages provide fast slewing and large linear input range.
- **ADC Front-Ends:** Switched-capacitor ADCs in pipeline and SAR architectures use folded-cascode OTAs as their core amplifier.
- **Active Pixel Sensors (APS) in CMOS Image Sensors:** Source-follower + cascode readout provides high conversion gain.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Insight:** The folded cascode is the *de facto* standard input stage in **Texas Instruments**, **Analog Devices**, and **Qualcomm** mixed-signal ICs, particularly for low-voltage (1.2V) applications in mobile SoCs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Output Resistance of a Cascode Stage

**Setup:** We derive the output resistance $R_{out}$ looking into the drain of M2, with M1 as a CS device whose drain is the source of M2.

**Step 1 вҖ” Apply a test voltage $v_x$ at the output (drain of M2).**

This $v_x$ will cause a small-signal current $i_x$ to flow into the output node. We need to find $R_{out} = v_x / i_x$.

**Step 2 вҖ” Express the small-signal drain current of M1.**

The drain of M1 is connected to the source of M2. Since M1's gate is grounded (input is AC shorted for output-resistance test), the small-signal $v_{gs1} = -v_{s2}$, where $v_{s2}$ is the small-signal voltage at the source of M2.

Therefore:

$$
i_{d1} = g_{m1} v_{gs1} + \frac{v_{ds1}}{r_{o1}} = -g_{m1} v_{s2} - \frac{v_{s2}}{r_{o1}}
$$

The negative sign arises because the drain-source voltage of M1 is $v_{ds1} = 0 - v_{s2} = -v_{s2}$.

**Step 3 вҖ” Express the small-signal drain current of M2.**

The gate of M2 is AC grounded, so $v_{gs2} = 0 - v_{s2} = -v_{s2}$, and $v_{ds2} = v_x - v_{s2}$.

$$
i_{d2} = g_{m2} v_{gs2} + \frac{v_{ds2}}{r_{o2}} = -g_{m2} v_{s2} + \frac{v_x - v_{s2}}{r_{o2}}
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Apply KCL at the source of M2 (drain of M1) node.**

The current flowing into the source of M2 from M1's drain equals the current flowing into M2's source. By inspection:

$$
i_{d1} = -i_{d2}
$$

(The current $i_{d1}$ flowing *out* of M1's drain is the current flowing *into* M2's source.)

**Step 5 вҖ” Solve for $v_{s2}$ in terms of $v_x$.**

Substituting the expressions from Step 2 and Step 3:

$$
-g_{m1} v_{s2} - \frac{v_{s2}}{r_{o1}} = -(-g_{m2} v_{s2} + \frac{v_x - v_{s2}}{r_{o2}})
$$

$$
-g_{m1} v_{s2} - \frac{v_{s2}}{r_{o1}} = g_{m2} v_{s2} - \frac{v_x}{r_{o2}} + \frac{v_{s2}}{r_{o2}}
$$

Rearranging to collect $v_{s2}$ terms on the left:

$$
g_{m2} v_{s2} + g_{m1} v_{s2} + \frac{v_{s2}}{r_{o1}} - \frac{v_{s2}}{r_{o2}} = -\frac{v_x}{r_{o2}}
$$

$$
v_{s2} \left( g_{m1} + g_{m2} + \frac{1}{r_{o1}} - \frac{1}{r_{o2}} \right) = -\frac{v_x}{r_{o2}}
$$

**Step 6 вҖ” Approximation: $g_{m1}, g_{m2} \gg 1/r_{o1}, 1/r_{o2}$.**

$$
v_{s2} (g_{m1} + g_{m2}) \approx -\frac{v_x}{r_{o2}}
$$

If $g_{m1} = g_{m2} = g_m$:

$$
v_{s2} \approx -\frac{v_x}{2 g_m r_{o2}}
$$

This is a *very small* voltage вҖ” meaning the source of M2 barely moves, confirming the cascode's isolation property.

**Step 7 вҖ” Express total output current $i_x$.**

The test current $i_x$ flows into the output node and splits between $r_{o2}$ and the rest. The KCL at the output is:

$$
i_x = \frac{v_x - v_{s2}}{r_{o2}} - g_{m2} v_{s2}
$$

**Step 8 вҖ” Substitute $v_{s2}$ and simplify.**

$$
i_x = \frac{v_x}{r_{o2}} - \frac{v_{s2}}{r_{o2}} - g_{m2} v_{s2}
$$

$$
i_x = \frac{v_x}{r_{o2}} - v_{s2} \left( \frac{1}{r_{o2}} + g_{m2} \right)
$$

Plugging $v_{s2} \approx -v_x / (2 g_m r_{o2})$:

$$
i_x = \frac{v_x}{r_{o2}} - \left( -\frac{v_x}{2 g_m r_{o2}} \right) (g_{m2} + \frac{1}{r_{o2}})
$$

$$
i_x = \frac{v_x}{r_{o2}} + \frac{v_x (g_{m2} + 1/r_{o2})}{2 g_m r_{o2}}
$$

Since $g_{m2} \gg 1/r_{o2}$:

$$
i_x \approx \frac{v_x}{r_{o2}} + \frac{v_x g_{m2}}{2 g_m r_{o2}} = \frac{v_x}{r_{o2}} + \frac{v_x}{2 r_{o2}} = \frac{3 v_x}{2 r_{o2}}
$$

This is the *first-order* result. The full rigorous derivation (without assuming $g_{m1} = g_{m2}$) yields the celebrated result:

$$
\boxed{R_{out} = r_{o1} + r_{o2} + g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2} \approx g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The output resistance is multiplied by a factor of $g_m r_o$ (the intrinsic gain, typically 20вҖ“50) compared to a simple CS stage. This is the *raison d'ГӘtre* of the cascode.

### 3.2 Derivation: Voltage Gain of a Cascode Amplifier

The voltage gain is the product of the input transconductance and the output resistance:

$$
A_v = -G_m \cdot R_{out}
$$

For a cascode, $G_m = g_{m1}$ (only the input transistor contributes transconductance; M2 buffers the current with unity current gain). Therefore:

$$
\boxed{A_v = -g_{m1} \cdot (r_{o1} + r_{o2} + g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2}) \approx -g_{m1} g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2}}
$$

In the symmetric case ($g_{m1} = g_{m2} = g_m$ and $r_{o1} = r_{o2} = r_o$):

$$
A_v \approx -(g_m r_o)^2
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Folded Cascode DC Gain

For a folded cascode with PMOS input pair (M1, M2) of transconductance $g_{m,p}$ and NMOS cascode branch (M3, M4) plus PMOS current-mirror load (M5, M6, M7, M8) of output resistance $R_{out}$:

$$
A_v = g_{m,p} \cdot R_{out}
$$

The output resistance of the folded cascode is the parallel combination of the *NMOS cascode branch output resistance* and the *PMOS cascode load output resistance*:

$$
R_{out} = (g_{m3} r_{o3} r_{o1}) \parallel (g_{m5} r_{o5} r_{o7})
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{A_v = g_{m,p} \cdot \left[ (g_{m3} r_{o3} r_{o1}) \parallel (g_{m5} r_{o5} r_{o7}) \right] \approx g_{m,p} g_m^2 r_o^3}
$$

The "1/2" factor (from the parallel combination) is often absorbed in approximations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q1. Define a cascode amplifier. Why is the cascode configuration preferred over a simple common-source amplifier in high-frequency analog design?**

**Model Answer:**

A cascode amplifier is a two-stage configuration in which a Common Source (CS) transistor is stacked on top of a Common Gate (CG) transistor. The input is applied to the gate of the CS device, and the output is taken from the drain of the CG device.

The cascode is preferred over a simple CS amplifier in high-frequency design because:

- **Miller Effect Suppression:** The Miller multiplication of $C_{gd1}$ is reduced by a factor of $\approx g_{m2} r_{o2}$, since the voltage gain from the drain of M1 to the output is approximately unity.
- **Higher Output Impedance:** $R_{out} = g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2}$ versus $r_{o1}$ for a CS stage.
- **Higher Gain:** $A_v \approx (g_m r_o)^2$ versus $g_m r_o$.
- **Better Reverse Isolation:** Improved $S_{12}$ in RF applications.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q2. What is a folded cascode amplifier? List two advantages it offers over the telescopic cascode.**

**Model Answer:**

A folded cascode amplifier is a topology in which the input differential pair (typically PMOS) steers its signal current into NMOS common-gate cascode devices that are biased by a *separate* current source, rather than the same current source as the input pair. The signal path is "folded" from a vertical NMOS stack into a horizontal PMOS-NMOS cascade.

**Two Advantages over Telescopic Cascode:**

1. **Wider Output Voltage Swing:** Output swing is $\approx 2(V_{DD} - V_{ov})$ instead of $\approx 2(V_{DD} - 2V_{ov})$, which is critical in low-voltage (sub-1.8V) designs.
2. **Easier Input Common-Mode Range:** The input common-mode range can include one of the supply rails, enabling level-shifting at the input.

---

### 5.2 Part B вҖ” Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper, Question A Choice]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Derive the expression for the small-signal output resistance of a cascode amplifier. Show all intermediate steps clearly. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

[Refer to the complete derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes. The key steps are:]

1. Apply test voltage $v_x$ at output, find test current $i_x$.
2. Write small-signal drain currents of M1 and M2 using $i_d = g_m v_{gs} + v_{ds}/r_o$.
3. Express $v_{gs1} = -v_{s2}$ and $v_{gs2} = -v_{s2}$.
4. Apply KCL at the source of M2: $i_{d1} = -i_{d2}$.
5. Solve for $v_{s2}$ in terms of $v_x$:

$$
v_{s2} = \frac{-v_x / r_{o2}}{g_{m1} + g_{m2} + 1/r_{o1} + 1/r_{o2}}
$$

6. Apply KCL at output node: $i_x = (v_x - v_{s2})/r_{o2} - g_{m2} v_{s2}$.
7. Simplify using $g_m \gg 1/r_o$:

$$
\boxed{R_{out} = r_{o1} + r_{o2} + g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2} \approx g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2}}
$$

8. Note that the gain of the cascode is therefore $A_v = -g_{m1} R_{out} \approx -g_{m1} g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2} = -(g_m r_o)^2$.

**(b) For a telescopic cascode amplifier with cascode current-mirror loads, derive the expression for the low-frequency voltage gain. Assume all NMOS devices are matched and all PMOS devices are matched. Use $g_{mn} = g_{mp} = g_m$ and $r_{on} = r_{op} = r_o$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 вҖ” Identify the topology.**

The telescopic cascode with cascade loads has *four* transistors contributing to the output impedance: NMOS cascode branch (M1, M2) and PMOS cascode load (M3, M4).

**Step 2 вҖ” Output resistance of NMOS cascode branch.**

$$
R_{out,N} = g_{m2} r_{o2} r_{o1} \approx g_m r_o^2
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Output resistance of PMOS cascode load branch.**

By symmetry, for PMOS devices with cascode current-mirror topology:

$$
R_{out,P} = g_{m4} r_{o4} r_{o3} \approx g_m r_o^2
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Total output resistance (parallel combination).**

$$
R_{out,total} = R_{out,N} \parallel R_{out,P} = \frac{g_m r_o^2 \cdot g_m r_o^2}{g_m r_o^2 + g_m r_o^2} = \frac{g_m r_o^2}{2}
$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Voltage gain.**

The effective transconductance is $G_m = g_{m1}$ (the input NMOS transconductance).

$$
\boxed{A_v = -G_m \cdot R_{out,total} = -g_m \cdot \frac{g_m r_o^2}{2} = -\frac{(g_m r_o)^2}{2} \approx -\frac{(g_m r_o)^2}{2}}
$$

In practice, including the body effect and slight mismatch, the gain is approximately $(g_m r_o)^2 / 2$ to $(g_m r_o)^2$. Note that the order of magnitude is $(g_m r_o)^2$, *not* $(g_m r_o)^4$ вҖ” this is a common student error.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper, Question B Choice]**

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) With the help of a clear block-level architecture, explain the operation of a folded cascode amplifier. Compare its output swing with the telescopic cascode. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Operation:**

The folded cascode consists of:
- A **PMOS differential input pair** (M1, M2) with tail current source $I_{SS}$.
- An **NMOS common-gate cascode** (M3, M4) with gates biased at $V_{BIASN}$.
- A **PMOS current-source load** (M5, M6) providing $I_{LOAD}$.

When $v_{in+}$ increases, the current through M1 increases by $g_{m1} v_{in+}/2$. This additional current is **pulled away from the source of M3**, meaning the current through M3 decreases. Since the current through M5 is fixed at $I_{LOAD}$, the difference must come from $C_L$, causing the output voltage to *rise*. The opposite happens at $v_{in-}$. This is the "folding" вҖ” the input pair PMOS feeds into an NMOS cascode, and the current direction is reversed (folded).

**Output Swing Comparison:**

- **Telescopic cascode:** Output node has both NMOS cascode (M2) and PMOS cascode (M4) stacked. Swing = $2[V_{DD} - (V_{ov,NMOS} + V_{ov,PMOS})]$. This drops **two** overdrives on top and **two** at the bottom вҖ” total of **4 overdrives** lost.
- **Folded cascode:** Output node has only the NMOS cascode (M3) and PMOS current source (M5). Swing = $2[V_{DD} - V_{ov}]$, losing only **2 overdrives** total.

The folded cascode therefore has **approximately twice the output voltage swing** of the telescopic cascode, a decisive advantage in low-voltage CMOS design.

**(b) Design a folded cascode OTA (Operational Transconductance Amplifier) for a unity-gain-bandwidth of 100 MHz driving a 5 pF load. Assume $\mu_n C_{ox} = 100 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, $\mu_p C_{ox} = 50 \,\mu\text{A/V}^2$, and $L = 0.5 \,\mu\text{m}$. Determine the required transconductance, the input transistor dimensions, and the bias currents. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 вҖ” Required transconductance.**

The unity-gain bandwidth of a single-pole op-amp is:

$$
\omega_u = \frac{g_{m1}}{C_L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad g_{m1} = \omega_u \cdot C_L
$$

$$
g_{m1} = 2\pi \times 100 \times 10^6 \times 5 \times 10^{-12} = 3.14 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A/V} = 3.14 \text{ mA/V}
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Input transistor (PMOS) dimensions.**

For a PMOS in saturation:

$$
g_{m1} = \sqrt{2 \mu_p C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_{D1}}
$$

Assume half the tail current flows in each branch of the differential pair. Choose $I_{D1} = 50 \,\mu\text{A}$ (a typical low-power value).

$$
\frac{W}{L} = \frac{g_{m1}^2}{2 \mu_p C_{ox} I_{D1}} = \frac{(3.14 \times 10^{-3})^2}{2 \times 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\frac{W}{L} = \frac{9.86 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-9}} = 1972
$$

For $L = 0.5 \,\mu\text{m}$:

$$
\boxed{W = 986 \,\mu\text{m} \approx 1000 \,\mu\text{m} \text{ (per PMOS input transistor)}}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Overdrive voltage of input pair.**

$$
V_{ov} = \frac{2 I_D}{g_m} = \frac{2 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{3.14 \times 10^{-3}} = 31.8 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Tail and load bias currents.**

- Tail current: $I_{SS} = 2 I_{D1} = 100 \,\mu\text{A}$.
- Output branch bias (NMOS cascode + PMOS load): $I_{LOAD} \approx I_{D1} = 50 \,\mu\text{A}$ to keep symmetry.

**Step 5 вҖ” Cascode transistor dimensions.**

The cascode devices (M3, M4) carry the same current $I_{D1} = 50 \,\mu\text{A}$. They are typically sized for $V_{ov} \approx 100$вҖ“$200$ mV to ensure they remain in saturation. Choosing $V_{ov} = 150$ mV:

$$
\frac{W}{L} \bigg|_{cascode} = \frac{2 I_D}{\mu_n C_{ox} V_{ov}^2} = \frac{2 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{100 \times 10^{-6} \times (0.15)^2} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-6}}{2.25 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 44
$$

For $L = 0.5 \,\mu\text{m}$: $W_{cascode} \approx 22 \,\mu\text{m}$.

**Step 6 вҖ” Summary of design.**

| **Transistor** | **Type** | **W/L** | **Current** | **Vov** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| M1, M2 (Input) | PMOS | $1000/0.5$ | $50 \,\mu\text{A}$ | $31.8$ mV |
| M3, M4 (Fold Cascode) | NMOS | $22/0.5$ | $50 \,\mu\text{A}$ | $150$ mV |
| M5 (Output Load) | PMOS | $\sim 50/0.5$ | $50 \,\mu\text{A}$ | $\sim 100$ mV |
| Tail current source | NMOS | вҖ” | $100 \,\mu\text{A}$ | вҖ” |

**Predicted Performance:**

- DC Gain: $A_v \approx g_{m1} \cdot g_m^2 r_o^3$. With $r_o = V_A/I_D \approx 10\text{V}/50\mu\text{A} = 200$ k$\Omega$ and $g_m \approx 3$ mA/V: $A_v \approx 3\text{m} \times (3\text{m} \times 200\text{k})^2 \approx 3\text{m} \times 360^2 \approx 390$ V/V $\approx 52$ dB.
- Unity-gain bandwidth: $100$ MHz (by design).
- Phase margin: typically $60^\circ$вҖ“$70^\circ$ with proper compensation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout вҖ” Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state approximations clearly:** KTU examiners deduct 1вҖ“2 marks if you write $R_{out} = g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2}$ without justifying the approximation $g_m r_o \gg 1$.
> 2. **Confusing telescopic and folded cascode gains:** Telescopic with cascade loads gives $\approx (g_m r_o)^2$, **NOT** $(g_m r_o)^4$. The fourth power would only occur with *double* cascode (a cascode of cascodes), which is rare.
> 3. **Mixing up Miller effect cancellation:** Always state *why* the Miller effect is reduced вҖ” because the CG stage has unity current gain and low input impedance ($1/g_{m2}$).
> 4. **Skipping the body effect in folded cascode:** In folded cascode, the source of the NMOS cascode (M3) is at the drain of the PMOS input pair. The body of M3 is typically tied to VSS, causing a body-effect transconductance reduction. Mention this in 14-mark answers.
> 5. **Not drawing signal flow clearly in block diagrams:** A messy block diagram costs 2 marks easily. Use rectangular blocks and arrows.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision вҖ” Pin This!**

- [x] **Cascode = CS + CG** (Common Source on bottom, Common Gate on top).
- [x] **Output resistance of cascode:** $R_{out} \approx g_{m2} r_{o1} r_{o2}$ вҖ” multiplied by $g_m r_o$ compared to a CS stage.
- [x] **Gain of cascode:** $A_v \approx -(g_m r_o)^2$ вҖ” multiplied by $g_m r_o$ compared to CS.
- [x] **Miller effect suppressed** because the CG stage has near-unity voltage gain, so the $C_{gd1}(1-A_v)$ term collapses.
- [x] **Cascode with cascode loads (telescopic):** gain $\approx (g_m r_o)^2 / 2$.
- [x] **Folded cascode "folds" the signal** from PMOS input pair into NMOS cascode branch вҖ” output swing = $2(V_{DD} - V_{ov})$ vs telescopic $2(V_{DD} - 2V_{ov})$.
- [x] **Folded cascode gain:** $A_v \approx g_{m,input} \cdot g_m^2 r_o^3$ вҖ” comparable to telescopic but with **double the output swing**.
- [x] **Folded cascode needs more bias current** (separate input and output branches).
- [x] **Telescopic cascode** is preferred for **minimum power**; **folded cascode** for **minimum voltage** operation.
- [x] **Bandwidth limitation:** $f_{-3dB} = 1/(2\pi R_{out} C_L)$; the cascode boosts $R_{out}$, so to maintain bandwidth, the load capacitor or current must be sized carefully.
- [x] **Unity-gain bandwidth:** $\omega_u = g_{m1}/C_L$ вҖ” set by the input transconductance and load capacitance.
- [x] **Real-world use:** Every CMOS op-amp, every switched-capacitor gain stage, every pipeline ADC, every RF LNA uses a cascode or folded cascode.
- [x] **Remember the noise trade-off:** Cascode adds one extra noise source (the CG transistor); folded cascode adds two (CG + current mirror).
- [x] **Stability tip:** Folded cascode has a non-dominant pole at the *folding node* (source of cascode). Use compensation capacitor or cascode bias filtering if needed.
- [x] **Exam default values to memorize:** $V_T = 26$ mV at 300 K; $g_m/I_D \approx 1/V_{ov}$ rule of thumb for moderate inversion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT755 - MIXED SIGNAL CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 вҖ” Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "Basic Circuit" in Mixed Signal Design?

In the context of **PEECT755 вҖ“ Mixed Signal Circuit Design** (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2), the term **"Basic Circuit"** refers to the foundational **single-transistor amplifier stages** and their small-signal equivalent models. These stages act as the **fundamental building blocks** from which every complex analog, RF, and mixed-signal block вҖ” such as op-amps, ADCs, DACs, PLLs, and filters вҖ” is constructed.

The three canonical single-stage configurations that KTU expects you to master are:

1. **Common Source (CS) Stage** вҖ” based on a MOSFET in common-source configuration.
2. **Common Gate (CG) Stage** вҖ” based on a MOSFET in common-gate configuration.
3. **Common Drain (CD) Stage** (also called **Source Follower**) вҖ” based on a MOSFET in common-drain configuration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 вҖ“ Basic Circuit):**
> A *Basic Circuit* in mixed-signal IC design is a single-transistor amplifier with passive/active loading, analysed using small-signal (AC) equivalent models. Mastering CS, CG, and CD stages is **mandatory** because they form the gain/input-output impedance core of every op-amp, comparator, and data-converter building block.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy вҖ” The "Tap and Bucket" Model

Imagine a water tap connected to a bucket:

- The **transistor** is the *tap* вҖ” it regulates how much current flows from the supply (the pipe) to the output bucket, controlled by a small input signal (the tap handle).
- The **load resistor (or active current-source load)** is the *bucket's outlet resistance* вҖ” it converts the current variation into a voltage variation at the output.
- The **small-signal analysis** is the assumption that you turn the tap handle by a *very tiny amount* around an operating point вҖ” this linearises the nonlinear valve so you can predict flow with simple math.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Every "Basic Circuit" analysis is performed at a fixed **DC operating point (Q-point)** and then a *small AC perturbation* is applied. We then replace the transistor with its **small-signal model** (usually a transconductance $g_m$ and output resistance $r_o$) and compute gain, input resistance, and output resistance.

---

## 1.3 The MOSFET Small-Signal Model (Foundation)

For all three configurations, the MOSFET is replaced by the following small-signal equivalent:

- A **voltage-controlled current source (VCCS)**: $i_d = g_m \cdot v_{gs}$
- An **output resistance**: $r_o = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_D}$ between drain and source.

Where the fundamental KTU-level formulas are:

$$g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_D} = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{GS} - V_{TH}} = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}}$$

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{V_A}{I_D}$$

$$\mu = g_m \cdot r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{2}{V_{OV}/V_A} \approx \text{intrinsic gain of the device}$$

Here, $\mu$ (the intrinsic gain) is the **maximum possible voltage gain** of a single transistor stage when loaded only by its own $r_o$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical constants/parameters to remember:**
> - $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ вҖ” **Overdrive voltage** (in Volts)
> - $\lambda$ вҖ” **Channel-length modulation parameter** (in VвҒ»В№)
> - $V_A = 1/\lambda$ вҖ” **Early voltage** (in Volts)
> - $I_D$ вҖ” **DC drain bias current** (in Amperes)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 вҖ” Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Single-Stage Configurations

### (A) Common Source (CS) Stage
- **Input applied at:** Gate
- **Output taken at:** Drain
- **Source terminal:** AC-grounded (signal-wise)

The CS stage is the **workhorse voltage amplifier** of analog design. It provides **moderate-to-high voltage gain**, **high input resistance**, and **moderate output resistance**.

### (B) Common Gate (CG) Stage
- **Input applied at:** Source
- **Output taken at:** Drain
- **Gate terminal:** AC-grounded

The CG stage provides **wideband operation** (no Miller effect), **low input resistance**, and **high output resistance**. It is used as a **buffer** when driving low-impedance loads, or as a **cascode** stacked on a CS stage.

### (C) Common Drain (CD) Stage вҖ” Source Follower
- **Input applied at:** Gate
- **Output taken at:** Source
- **Drain terminal:** AC-grounded (tied to $V_{DD}$)

The CD stage is a **unity-gain voltage buffer** with **very high input resistance** and **low output resistance**. It is used to drive low-impedance loads without loading down a high-impedance previous stage.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Small-Signal Analysis

For all three stages, the KTU-recommended procedure is:

1. **Draw the circuit** and identify input/output nodes.
2. **Replace the MOSFET** with its small-signal model (VCCS $g_m v_{gs}$ in parallel with $r_o$).
3. **Ground all DC supplies** (since they are AC short circuits for small-signal analysis).
4. **Apply a test input voltage/current** at the input port.
5. **Compute the output voltage/current** at the output port.
6. **Derive**:
   - Voltage gain $A_v = v_{out}/v_{in}$
   - Input resistance $R_{in}$
   - Output resistance $R_{out}$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER THIS TABLE вҖ” these are the formulas KTU repeats across years for Module 2.**

| Stage | Voltage Gain $A_v$ | Input Resistance $R_{in}$ | Output Resistance $R_{out}$ | Key Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CS** with load $R_D$ | $-g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$ | $\infty$ (gate is insulated) | $R_D \parallel r_o$ | Voltage amplification |
| **CS** with active load (current source) | $-g_m r_o$ | $\infty$ | $r_o$ | High-gain stage |
| **CG** with load $R_D$ | $g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$ | $\dfrac{1}{g_m} \parallel r_o$ | $R_D \parallel r_o$ | Wideband buffer / cascode |
| **CD** (Source Follower) | $\dfrac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S}$ | $\infty$ | $\dfrac{1}{g_m} \parallel r_o \parallel R_S$ | Unity-gain buffer |

Where:
- $g_m$ вҖ” transconductance (Siemens, S)
- $r_o$ вҖ” output resistance of the MOSFET (Ohms, $\Omega$)
- $R_D$ вҖ” drain load resistor (Ohms, $\Omega$)
- $R_S$ вҖ” source load resistor (Ohms, $\Omega$)

> [!NOTE]
> The **intrinsic gain of a single transistor** is $\mu = g_m r_o$. This is the **upper limit** of voltage gain achievable from one transistor when the load is an ideal current source. In advanced CMOS processes (28 nm and below), $\mu$ can drop to **5вҖ“20**, making cascoding and multi-stage architectures necessary.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Op-amp input stages (differential pairs)** use CS stages with active current-mirror loads.
- **Cascode amplifiers** are simply a CS stage stacked under a CG stage вҖ” improving gain and bandwidth.
- **Source followers** are the **output buffers** of every op-amp (e.g., the classic 741 output stage has a source-follower-like emitter follower).
- **Low-noise amplifiers (LNAs)** in RFICs use CS or CG configurations to optimise noise figure and input matching.
- **Sample-and-hold circuits** and **switched-capacitor integrators** in ADCs use CS stages as the OTA core.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 вҖ” Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Common Source (CS) Stage with Resistive Load

**Given Circuit:** A MOSFET with source grounded, drain connected to $V_{DD}$ through resistor $R_D$, input $v_{in}$ applied at gate, output $v_{out}$ taken at drain.

### Step 1 вҖ” DC Bias Point
The transistor is biased in saturation by a DC gate-source voltage $V_{GS}$ that establishes the quiescent drain current $I_D$. For small-signal analysis, all DC sources are set to zero (replaced by AC ground).

### Step 2 вҖ” Replace MOSFET with Small-Signal Model
The MOSFET between drain and source is replaced by:
- A VCCS of value $g_m v_{gs}$ pointing from drain to source.
- A resistance $r_o$ in parallel with the VCCS.

The gate is open-circuit (no current flows into the gate in DC / low-frequency analysis).

### Step 3 вҖ” Apply Test Input
Let $v_{gs} = v_{in}$ (since source is grounded, $v_{gs} = v_g - v_s = v_{in} - 0 = v_{in}$).

### Step 4 вҖ” Write KCL at the Drain Node
The current flowing down through $R_D$ and the current through $r_o$ must sum to the current injected by the VCCS.

$$i_{RD} = i_{r_o} + i_{VCCS}$$

But since $v_{out}$ is the drain voltage (with respect to ground), the current through $R_D$ from $V_{DD}$ (now AC ground) to drain is $-v_{out}/R_D$.

Therefore:

$$\frac{-v_{out}}{R_D} = \frac{v_{out}}{r_o} + g_m v_{in}$$

Rearranging:

$$-v_{out} \left( \frac{1}{R_D} + \frac{1}{r_o} \right) = g_m v_{in}$$

$$v_{out} = -g_m v_{in} \left( \frac{R_D \cdot r_o}{R_D + r_o} \right)$$

### Step 5 вҖ” Final Result

$$\boxed{A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)}$$

**Input resistance:** $R_{in} = \infty$ (gate is insulated; ideal infinite input resistance).

**Output resistance:** $R_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o$.

> [!NOTE]
> The **negative sign** indicates a **180В° phase inversion** between input and output. This is a signature property of the CS stage and is heavily tested in KTU examinations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Common Gate (CG) Stage with Resistive Load

**Given Circuit:** Gate is AC-grounded, input $v_{in}$ applied at source, output $v_{out}$ taken at drain through $R_D$.

### Step 1 вҖ” Identify $v_{gs}$
Since gate is grounded, $v_g = 0$. The source is at $v_{in}$, so:

$$v_{gs} = v_g - v_s = 0 - v_{in} = -v_{in}$$

### Step 2 вҖ” Apply KCL at Drain Node
The drain node connects to $R_D$ (to AC ground) and the parallel combination of $r_o$ and the VCCS.

$$\frac{-v_{out}}{R_D} = \frac{v_{out} - v_{in}}{r_o} + g_m v_{gs} = \frac{v_{out} - v_{in}}{r_o} + g_m(-v_{in})$$

Multiply through by a common factor and simplify:

$$-\frac{v_{out}}{R_D} - \frac{v_{out}}{r_o} = -\frac{v_{in}}{r_o} - g_m v_{in}$$

$$-v_{out}\left(\frac{1}{R_D} + \frac{1}{r_o}\right) = -v_{in}\left(\frac{1}{r_o} + g_m\right)$$

$$v_{out} = v_{in} \cdot \frac{\dfrac{1}{r_o} + g_m}{\dfrac{1}{R_D} + \dfrac{1}{r_o}}$$

### Step 3 вҖ” Final Result (assuming $g_m r_o \gg 1$)

$$\boxed{A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} \approx g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The CG stage is **non-inverting** (positive gain) вҖ” this is a key exam point distinguishing it from the CS stage.

### Step 4 вҖ” Input Resistance
Apply a test voltage $v_x$ at the source, measure the current $i_x$ entering the source:

KCL at source node: $i_x = g_m v_{gs} + \dfrac{v_x}{r_o} = g_m(-v_x) + \dfrac{v_x}{r_o}$

$$R_{in} = \frac{v_x}{i_x} = \frac{1}{g_m} \parallel r_o = \frac{r_o}{1 + g_m r_o}$$

**Output resistance:** $R_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Common Drain (CD) Stage вҖ” Source Follower

**Given Circuit:** Drain tied to $V_{DD}$ (AC ground), input $v_{in}$ at gate, output $v_{out}$ taken at source across resistor $R_S$.

### Step 1 вҖ” Identify $v_{gs}$
$$v_{gs} = v_g - v_s = v_{in} - v_{out}$$

### Step 2 вҖ” Apply KCL at Source Node
Current leaving source through $R_S$ equals current from VCCS plus current through $r_o$:

$$\frac{v_{out}}{R_S} + \frac{v_{out}}{r_o} + g_m v_{gs} = 0$$

Substitute $v_{gs} = v_{in} - v_{out}$:

$$\frac{v_{out}}{R_S} + \frac{v_{out}}{r_o} + g_m(v_{in} - v_{out}) = 0$$

$$v_{out}\left(\frac{1}{R_S} + \frac{1}{r_o} + g_m\right) = g_m v_{in}$$

### Step 3 вҖ” Final Result

$$\boxed{A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = \frac{g_m}{\dfrac{1}{R_S} + \dfrac{1}{r_o} + g_m} = \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S + \dfrac{R_S}{r_o}}}$$

If $g_m R_S \gg 1$ and $R_S/r_o \ll 1$, then $A_v \to 1$ вҖ” the source "follows" the input.

**Input resistance:** $R_{in} = \infty$.

**Output resistance:** $R_{out} = \dfrac{1}{g_m} \parallel r_o \parallel R_S$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | CS | CG | CD (Source Follower) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phase Inversion** | Yes (180В°) | No (0В°) | No (0В°) |
| **$A_v$ Magnitude** | High | High | $\approx 1$ |
| **$R_{in}$** | Infinite | Low ($\sim 1/g_m$) | Infinite |
| **$R_{out}$** | High | High | Low |
| **Miller Effect** | Yes (large $C_{gd}$ impact) | No | Yes (reduced) |
| **Bandwidth** | Limited | Wide | Moderate |
| **Primary Use** | Voltage gain | Cascode / wideband | Buffer / level shift |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 вҖ” Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic "Basic Circuit" Stage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[INPUT SIGNAL VIN] --> nodeB[BIAS NETWORK Q POINT]
    nodeB --> nodeC[TRANSISTOR M1 SMALL SIGNAL MODEL]
    nodeC --> nodeD[LOAD R D OR ACTIVE CURRENT SOURCE]
    nodeD --> nodeE[OUTPUT NODE VOUT]
    nodeF[DC SUPPLY VDD] --> nodeC
    nodeG[DC SUPPLY VSS] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeH[SMALL SIGNAL VCCS GM VGS]
    nodeC --> nodeI[OUTPUT RESISTANCE RO]
    nodeE --> nodeJ[GAIN CALCULATION AV]
    nodeE --> nodeK[ROUT CALCULATION]
    nodeB --> nodeL[RIN CALCULATION]

    classDef sig fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000
```
