```mermaid
    class C,D,H,I stage
    class J,K,L calc
```

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart вҖ” Which Basic Circuit to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[Design Requirement] --> nodeQ1{Need high voltage gain}
    nodeQ1 -- YES --> nodeCS[Use Common Source CS]
    nodeQ1 -- NO --> nodeQ2{Need wide bandwidth}
    nodeQ2 -- YES --> nodeCG[Use Common Gate CG or Cascode]
    nodeQ2 -- NO --> nodeQ3{Need to drive low impedance load}
    nodeQ3 -- YES --> nodeCD[Use Common Drain CD Source Follower]
    nodeQ3 -- NO --> nodeCS

    classDef startNode fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000
    classDef configNode fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#0dcaf0,color:#000
    class Start startNode
    class CS,CG,CD configNode
```

## 4.3 Small-Signal Model Equivalence Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeMOS[MOSFET SYMBOL] --> nodeSS[SMALL SIGNAL MODEL]
    nodeSS --> nodeVCCS[VCCS GM VGS]
    nodeSS --> nodeRO[OUTPUT RESISTANCE RO]
    nodeVCCS --> nodeCSapp[CS STAGE A V NEGATIVE]
    nodeVCCS --> nodeCGapp[CG STAGE A V POSITIVE]
    nodeVCCS --> nodeCDapp[CD STAGE A V APPROX 1]
    nodeRO --> nodeCSapp
    nodeRO --> nodeCGapp
    nodeRO --> nodeCDapp
    nodeCSapp --> nodeOP1[VOLTAGE AMPLIFIER]
    nodeCGapp --> nodeOP2[CASCODE BUFFER]
    nodeCDapp --> nodeOP3[UNITY GAIN BUFFER]

    classDef device fill:#e2d9f3,stroke:#6610f2,color:#000
    classDef app fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#d63384,color:#000
    class MOS,SS,VCCS,RO device
    class CSapp,CGapp,CDapp,OP1,OP2,OP3 app
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 вҖ” KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model Question, Module 2]**

### Q1. Define transconductance ($g_m$) of a MOSFET. Why is it the most important parameter in analog small-signal analysis? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **transconductance** $g_m$ of a MOSFET is defined as the small-signal change in drain current per unit small-signal change in gate-to-source voltage, with $V_{DS}$ held constant:

$$g_m = \left.\frac{\partial i_D}{\partial v_{GS}}\right\vert_{V_{DS}} = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} I_D} = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}}$$

It is the most important parameter in analog small-signal analysis because **every voltage gain, input resistance, and output resistance expression** for a single-stage amplifier (CS, CG, CD) is directly proportional to $g_m$. It characterises the **input-to-output signal conversion efficiency** of the transistor in the saturation region.

---

### Q2. What is the intrinsic gain ($\mu$) of a MOSFET? State its significance. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **intrinsic gain** $\mu$ of a MOSFET is defined as the product of transconductance and output resistance:

$$\mu = g_m \cdot r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{2}{V_{OV}}$$

It represents the **maximum possible voltage gain** of a single-transistor amplifier when loaded by an ideal current source (infinite load resistance). Its significance:

- Sets the **upper bound on single-stage gain** in CMOS technology.
- Determines **how many cascaded stages** are needed to achieve a target op-amp gain.
- In modern nanometer CMOS, $\mu$ is small (5вҖ“50), so **cascoding, gain-boosting, and multi-stage topologies** are required.

---

## 5.2 Part B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam вҖ“ Model Question, Module 2 вҖ” Internal Choice Pattern]**

### вӯҗ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Draw the small-signal equivalent circuit of a **Common Source amplifier** with a resistive load $R_D$, and derive the expression for voltage gain $A_v$, input resistance $R_{in}$, and output resistance $R_{out}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A CS amplifier is biased at $I_D = 1 \text{ mA}$ with $V_{OV} = 0.5 \text{ V}$, $R_D = 5 \text{ k}\Omega$, and $\lambda = 0.01 \text{ V}^{-1}$. Calculate $g_m$, $r_o$, and the voltage gain. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution вҖ” Part (a)

**Step 1:** Replace the MOSFET with its small-signal model: a VCCS $g_m v_{gs}$ in parallel with $r_o$.

**Step 2:** Ground all DC supplies and the source terminal (since it is AC grounded).

**Step 3:** Apply test input $v_{in}$ at the gate, take $v_{out}$ at the drain.

**Step 4:** KCL at the drain node:

$$\frac{-v_{out}}{R_D} = \frac{v_{out}}{r_o} + g_m v_{in}$$

**Step 5:** Solving:

$$A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$$

**Step 6:** Since the gate draws no current at low frequency:

$$R_{in} = \infty$$

**Step 7:** To find $R_{out}$, deactivate the input ($v_{in} = 0$, so VCCS = 0), apply test voltage $v_x$ at the drain, and measure $i_x$:

$$R_{out} = R_D \parallel r_o$$

---

#### Model Solution вҖ” Part (b)

**Step 1:** Calculate $g_m$:

$$g_m = \frac{2 I_D}{V_{OV}} = \frac{2 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5} = 4 \text{ mA/V} = 4 \text{ mS}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $r_o$:

$$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_D} = \frac{1}{0.01 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1}{10^{-5}} = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 3:** Calculate $R_D \parallel r_o$:

$$R_D \parallel r_o = \frac{5 \text{k} \times 100 \text{k}}{5 \text{k} + 100 \text{k}} = \frac{500}{105} \text{ k}\Omega \approx 4.76 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 4:** Calculate voltage gain:

$$A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o) = -4 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.76 \times 10^{3} \approx -19.05 \text{ V/V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall вҖ” Common Mark-Loss Points:**
> 1. Forgetting the **parallel combination** of $R_D$ and $r_o$ (students often write only $g_m R_D$).
> 2. Dropping the **negative sign** in the CS gain вҖ” KTU awards 1 mark explicitly for the phase inversion.
> 3. Using wrong units for $g_m$ (must be in **S or A/V**, NOT mA/V when plugging into V/V gain).
> 4. Confusing $V_{OV}$ with $V_{GS}$ вҖ” $V_{OV} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ is the correct overdrive voltage.

---

### вӯҗ Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” Alternative Choice

**(a)** With the help of a small-signal model, derive the voltage gain, input resistance, and output resistance of a **Source Follower (Common Drain)** stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **Common Source, Common Gate, and Common Drain** configurations in terms of voltage gain, input/output resistance, phase, and bandwidth. State one typical application of each. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution вҖ” Part (a)

**Step 1:** Replace the MOSFET with small-signal model (VCCS $g_m v_{gs}$ and $r_o$).

**Step 2:** Drain is AC grounded, source carries the output $v_{out}$.

**Step 3:** Note that $v_{gs} = v_{in} - v_{out}$.

**Step 4:** KCL at the source node:

$$\frac{v_{out}}{R_S} + \frac{v_{out}}{r_o} + g_m(v_{in} - v_{out}) = 0$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $v_{out}/v_{in}$:

$$A_v = \frac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S + \dfrac{R_S}{r_o}}$$

**Step 6:** $R_{in} = \infty$ (gate is insulated).

**Step 7:** $R_{out} = \dfrac{1}{g_m} \parallel r_o \parallel R_S$.

---

#### Model Solution вҖ” Part (b)

| Configuration | Voltage Gain | $R_{in}$ | $R_{out}$ | Phase | Bandwidth | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CS** | High ($g_m R_D$) | $\infty$ | High | 180В° inversion | Limited (Miller) | Gain stage |
| **CG** | High ($g_m R_D$) | Low | High | 0В° (non-inverting) | Wide (no Miller) | Cascode / LNA input |
| **CD** | $\approx 1$ | $\infty$ | Low ($\approx 1/g_m$) | 0В° (non-inverting) | Moderate | Output buffer |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist вҖ” Module 2: Basic Circuit**

- вң… The **three basic circuits** are **CS, CG, and CD** вҖ” all are single-MOSFET stages.
- вң… CS is the **only inverting** stage; CG and CD are non-inverting.
- вң… **CS gain:** $A_v = -g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$.
- вң… **CG gain:** $A_v \approx +g_m (R_D \parallel r_o)$.
- вң… **CD gain:** $A_v = \dfrac{g_m R_S}{1 + g_m R_S + R_S/r_o} \to 1$.
- вң… **$g_m = 2 I_D / V_{OV}$** вҖ” remember this exact form for KTU derivations.
- вң… **$r_o = 1/(\lambda I_D)$** вҖ” output resistance depends on bias current and channel-length modulation.
- вң… **Intrinsic gain** $\mu = g_m r_o$ вҖ” maximum gain of one transistor with ideal current source load.
- вң… **Source Follower** has very **low output resistance** ($\approx 1/g_m$) вҖ” ideal for **buffering**.
- вң… **CG stage** has **low input resistance** ($\approx 1/g_m$) and **no Miller multiplication** вҖ” ideal for **wideband / cascode** use.
- вң… All small-signal analyses are done at a fixed **Q-point** with **DC sources AC-grounded**.
- вң… The **input resistance** of CS and CD stages is **infinite** at low frequency (gate is insulated).
- вң… **Modern nanoscale CMOS** has small $\mu$ (5вҖ“20) вҖ” drives the need for **cascoding** in practical op-amp design.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PMOS and NMOS Current Mirrors: Simple and Cascode Current Mirror Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT755 - MIXED SIGNAL CIRCUIT DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** PMOS and NMOS Current Mirrors: Simple and Cascode Current Mirror Circuits ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Current Mirror** is a fundamental analog integrated circuit building block engineered to copy (mirror) a reference current flowing in one branch of the circuit into one or more output branches, while maintaining a precisely defined current ratio. In CMOS technology, current mirrors are realized using **MOSFETs** (NMOS or PMOS) operating in the **saturation region**, leveraging the predictable square-law relationship between drain current and gate-source voltage. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A current mirror is a circuit that produces a constant bias current in integrated analog and mixed-signal systems by replicating a reference current $I_{REF}$ with high accuracy. The accuracy is determined by transistor matching, channel-length modulation ($\lambda$), and the output resistance ($r_o$) of the mirroring transistor. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” The "Water Flow Splitter" Intuition Imagine a water pipeline system: - A **reference pipe** (the input) carries a precisely metered flow of water using a calibrated nozzle. - This flow is directed into a **measurement chamber** with a floating piston. - The piston is mechanically linked to one or more **output valves** that reproduce the same flow rate in other branches. The water pressure here is analogous to **$V_{GS}$**, the flow rate to **$I_D$**, and the piston linkage represents the **gate-to-drain connection** of the diode-connected MOSFET. Just as the piston ensures all output valves see the same "command," the diode-connected transistor forces identical $V_{GS}$ on all mirroring transistors, making their drain currents equal (or scaled). ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Electron mobility** $\mu_n \approx 450 \text{ cm}^2/\text{VВ·s}$ (NMOS, typical 0.18 Вөm process) - **Hole mobility** $\mu_p \approx 150 \text{ cm}^2/\text{VВ·s}$ (PMOS, ~1/3 of NMOS) - **Thermal voltage** $V_T = kT/q \approx \mathbf{26 \text{ mV}}$ at **300 K** - **Process transconductance parameter** $k'_n = \mu_n C_{ox}$, $k'_p = \mu_p C_{ox}$ - **Output resistance unit**: $\Omega$ (Ohms), typically in **kО© to MО©** range - **Reference current** $I_{REF}$: typically **10 ВөA to 1 mA** in analog ICs > [!NOTE] > **NMOS vs PMOS Current Mirror:** NMOS mirrors use **N-channel** transistors and are usually built around a positive reference current sourced from $V_{DD}$. PMOS mirrors use **P-channel** transistors with reference current sunk to $V_{SS}$. PMOS mirrors are commonly placed at the top of a stack to bias other PMOS current sources in differential pairs and OTAs. ### 1.4 Why Current Mirrors Matter in Mixed-Signal Design Current mirrors form the **backbone** of nearly every analog sub-block: | Application | Role of Current Mirror | |---|---| | **Operational Transconductance Amplifier (OTA)** | Provides tail bias current and active loads | | **Differential Pair** | Acts as a high-impedance active load (current-mirror load) | | **Bandgap Reference** | Establishes PTAT/CTAT current ratios | | **ADC/DAC Biasing** | Sets precise bias currents for comparators and samplers | | **Voltage-to-Current Converters** | First stage in many translinear loops | > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners often start with a 3-mark question asking for the *role* of a current mirror. Always mention: "to provide a stable bias current independent of supply voltage and temperature, and to act as an active load with high small-signal output impedance." ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Square-Law Foundation A MOSFET operating in **saturation** has drain current given by: $$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$ When $\lambda V_{DS} \ll 1$, the equation simplifies to: $$I_D \approx \frac{1}{2} k'_n \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2$$ This **square-law** dependence on $V_{GS}$ is the bedrock of current mirror behavior. The current is set **solely** by $V_{GS}$ and the aspect ratio $(W/L)$. ### 2.2 Operational Principle вҖ” Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1: Diode-Connected Reference Transistor** - MOSFET M1 has its **gate tied to its drain** ($V_{GS1} = V_{DS1}$). - This forces M1 to always operate in **saturation** (since $V_{DS} = V_{GS} > V_{GS} - V_{TH}$). - M1 acts as a non-linear voltage-to-current converter driven by $I_{REF}$. **Step 2: Gate Voltage Replication** - The same gate voltage $V_{GS1}$ is applied to the gate of M2. - M2 also operates in saturation, drawing a current $I_{OUT}$ determined by its $W/L$ ratio. **Step 3: Current Mirroring Action** - The two transistors share the same $V_{GS}$ but can have different $W/L$ ratios. - This produces a **scaled output current**. **Step 4: Channel Length Modulation Error ($\lambda$ effect)** - M1 and M2 may have different $V_{DS}$ values in real circuits. - This causes $I_{OUT}$ to **deviate** from the ideal value вҖ” a critical non-ideality. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates **all critical equations** for both simple and cascode current mirrors. Memorize the boundary conditions carefully вҖ” KTU board papers test these exact relations. | # | Quantity | Equation (Ideal, $\lambda = 0$) | Equation (with $\lambda$) | Condition / Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Reference current $I_{REF}$ | $I_{REF} = \frac{1}{2} k' \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 (V_{GS1} - V_{TH})^2$ | $I_{REF} = \frac{1}{2} k' \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 (V_{GS1} - V_{TH})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS1})$ | Diode-connected M1 | | 2 | Output current $I_{OUT}$ | $I_{OUT} = I_{REF} \cdot \frac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1}$ | $I_{OUT} = I_{REF} \cdot \frac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1} \cdot \frac{1 + \lambda V_{DS2}}{1 + \lambda V_{DS1}}$ | Same $V_{GS}$, possibly different $V_{DS}$ | | 3 | Output resistance (simple) | $R_{out} = r_{o2}$ | $R_{out} = r_{o2} = \frac{1}{\lambda I_{OUT}}$ | Limited by $\lambda$ | | 4 | Output resistance (cascode) | $R_{out} \approx g_{m4} \cdot r_{o4} \cdot r_{o2}$ | $R_{out} \approx (g_m r_o)^2 / g_m = g_m r_o^2$ order of magnitude | $g_m r_o$ is the **intrinsic gain** $A_0$ | | 5 | Minimum output voltage (simple) | $V_{OUT,min} = V_{GS2} - V_{TH} = V_{OV,1}$ | Same as ideal | One $V_{OV}$ of headroom | | 6 | Minimum output voltage (cascode) | $V_{OUT,min} = 2V_{OV} + V_{TH}$ | Same as ideal | Two stacked $V_{OV}$s required | | 7 | Transconductance | $g_m = \sqrt{2 k' (W/L) I_D}$ | $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) I_D}$ | Square-root dependence on bias | | 8 | Mismatch error | $\frac{\Delta I_{OUT}}{I_{OUT}} \approx \frac{\Delta (W/L)}{(W/L)} + \frac{2 \Delta V_{TH}}{V_{OV}}$ | Same form | Critical for precision mirrors | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In a modern **65 nm CMOS mixed-signal IC** (e.g., a 12-bit SAR ADC), a precision PMOS cascode current mirror might: - Provide a **2 ВөA tail current** to a differential comparator. - Achieve an **output impedance of > 10 MО©** so the gain of the comparator stage is not load-sensitive. - Occupy a silicon area of just **~ 50 ВөmВІ**, far smaller than a discrete implementation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Trade-off:** Higher output impedance (cascode) comes at the cost of **reduced voltage headroom**. In low-voltage (e.g., 1.2 V) designs, **wide-swing cascode** mirrors are used to mitigate this penalty вҖ” a likely KTU follow-up question. ### 2.5 PMOS vs NMOS вҖ” Behavioral Comparison | Property | NMOS Current Mirror | PMOS Current Mirror | |---|---|---| | Source connection | To $V_{SS}$ (ground) | To $V_{DD}$ | | Current direction | Flows from drain to source | Flows from source to drain | | $V_{GS}$ sign | Positive | Negative | | Mobility | Higher $\mu_n$ (faster) | Lower $\mu_p$ (slower) | | Typical use | Bottom bias, sinks current | Top bias, sources current, OTA active loads | | Body effect | Can be eliminated by tying source to bulk (in dedicated well) | Source often tied to $V_{DD}$, body effect present | ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Simple NMOS Current Mirror Current Ratio **Given Circuit:** - M1: Diode-connected NMOS, drain and gate tied together, carries $I_{REF}$. - M2: NMOS mirroring transistor, gate connected to M1's gate, source to $V_{SS}$. - Both M1 and M2 share the same source potential ($V_{SS} = 0$). **Step 1 вҖ” Apply Saturation Equation to M1 (Reference Branch)** Since M1 is diode-connected, $V_{GS1} = V_{DS1}$. The drain current $I_{REF}$ is: $$I_{REF} = \frac{1}{2} k'_n \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 (V_{GS1} - V_{THN})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS1})$$ **Step 2 вҖ” Apply Saturation Equation to M2 (Output Branch)** M2 has the same $V_{GS2} = V_{GS1}$ (because their gates are tied and sources are tied). Therefore: $$I_{OUT} = \frac{1}{2} k'_n \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_2 (V_{GS2} - V_{THN})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS2})$$ **Step 3 вҖ” Take the Ratio to Cancel $V_{GS}$** $$\frac{I_{OUT}}{I_{REF}} = \frac{\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS2})}{\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 (1 + \lambda V_{DS1})}$$ **Step 4 вҖ” Ideal Case ($\lambda = 0$)** $$\boxed{\frac{I_{OUT}}{I_{REF}} = \frac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1}}$$ **Step 5 вҖ” Interpretation** By sizing the aspect ratio, the designer can set the output current to a precise multiple of $I_{REF}$. For example, if $(W/L)_2 = 2 \cdot (W/L)_1$, then $I_{OUT} = 2 I_{REF}$. ### 3.2 Derivation: Output Resistance of Simple Current Mirror The output resistance determines how well the mirror behaves as an **ideal current source**. We apply a test voltage $v_x$ at the output node and find the resulting current $i_x$: $$R_{out} = \frac{v_x}{i_x}$$ For M2 in saturation with gate AC-grounded (since M1's gate is held by the reference), the small-signal model is a transistor with source at AC ground and a drain resistance $r_{o2}$: $$\boxed{R_{out} = r_{o2} = \frac{1}{\lambda I_{OUT}} = \frac{V_{A}}{I_{OUT}}}$$ where $V_A = 1/\lambda$ is the **Early voltage** (typically 5вҖ“50 V in modern processes). The higher the $V_{DS2}$ of M2, the higher its actual $I_{OUT}$ (and lower $r_{o2}$), making the current source **load-dependent** вҖ” a major non-ideality. ### 3.3 Derivation: Cascode Current Mirror вҖ” Output Resistance **Motivation:** To eliminate the $\lambda V_{DS}$ mismatch between M1 and M2, we cascade a second transistor (M4) on top of M2. This forces $V_{DS2}$ to be **fixed and equal** to $V_{DS1}$ (the reference side), eliminating the $(1+\lambda V_{DS2})/(1+\lambda V_{DS1})$ error. **Circuit Topology (NMOS Cascode):** - M1, M3 form the diode-connected reference branch (M3 cascode on top of M1). - M2, M4 form the output branch (M4 cascode on top of M2). - M3 and M4 share a common gate voltage, set so that $V_{GS3} = V_{GS1} + V_{GS4} - V_{THN}$ (a common bias voltage). **Step 1 вҖ” Output Resistance of Cascode Stage** Apply test source $v_x$ at the output drain. The small-signal model for M4 (a common-gate transistor) feeding into M2's $r_{o2}$ gives: $$R_{out} = r_{o4} + r_{o2} + g_{m4} r_{o4} r_{o2}$$ **Step 2 вҖ” Simplification (since $g_m r_o \gg 1$)** The dominant term is the last one: $$\boxed{R_{out} \approx g_{m4} r_{o4} r_{o2} \approx (g_m r_o)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{g_m} = g_m r_o^2}$$ In modern processes where $g_m r_o \approx 50$вҖ“$500$, the cascode mirror offers an output impedance of **MО© to GО©** вҖ” orders of magnitude higher than the simple mirror. ### 3.4 Derivation: Minimum Output Voltage Compliance **Simple Mirror:** M2 must remain in saturation: - $V_{DS2} \geq V_{GS2} - V_{THN} = V_{OV,1}$ - Therefore, the minimum $V_{OUT}$ is **one overdrive voltage** above $V_{SS}$. **Cascode Mirror:** Both M2 and M4 must remain in saturation. - For M2: $V_{DS2} \geq V_{OV,2}$ - For M4: $V_{DS4} \geq V_{OV,4}$ - $V_{OUT,min} = V_{OV,2} + V_{OV,4} = 2V_{OV}$ This is the **headroom penalty**: a cascode mirror consumes twice the voltage swing of a simple mirror, motivating **wide-swing cascode** variants in low-voltage designs. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Performance Comparison > [!IMPORTANT] > This table is a **favourite KTU question** ("Compare simple and cascode current mirrors"). Use the following format in your exam answer: | Parameter | Simple Mirror | Cascode Mirror | |---|---|---| | **Output resistance $R_{out}$** | $r_o$ (low) | $g_m r_o^2$ (very high) | | **Headroom / $V_{OUT,min}$** | $V_{OV}$ (excellent) | $2V_{OV}$ (poor) | | **Matching sensitivity to $V_{DS}$** | High (direct $\lambda V_{DS}$ error) | Very low (forces $V_{DS2} = V_{DS1}$) | | **Current ratio accuracy** | Moderate | High | | **Power consumption** | Low | Slightly higher (extra $V_{DS}$ drop) | | **Speed (bandwidth)** | High (no cascode node) | Lower (extra node adds pole) | | **Area** | Minimal | ~2Г— the transistors | | **PSRR** | Moderate | Better (cascoding isolates) | ### 3.6 PMOS Cascode вҖ” Sign and Biasing Considerations For a PMOS cascode, all NMOS equations are mirrored. The currents flow from $V_{DD}$ down to the mirror, and the $V_{GS}$ values are negative (i.e., $V_{SG} = \vert V_{GS} \vert$). The output current $I_{OUT}$ is sourced from the mirror into the load: $$I_{OUT} = \frac{1}{2} k'_p \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_2 (V_{SG} - \vert V_{THP} \vert)^2 (1 + \lambda_p \vert V_{SD} \vert)$$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Board Trap:** In PMOS mirrors, students often forget that $|V_{THP}| \approx 0.5$вҖ“$0.7$ V (larger than $V_{THN}$). This reduces headroom further. Always quote magnitudes when writing PMOS equations. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Schematic вҖ” Simple NMOS Current Mirror ```mermaid graph TD A[VDD] --> B[I_REF Source] B --> C[M1 Gate-Drain Node] C --> D[Diode-Connected M1
