Color codes: green = reference/diode-connected, blue = mirroring transistor, yellow = cascode gate bias, red = output/critical node, purple = common gate line. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *What is a current mirror? Mention any two applications of current mirrors in analog integrated circuits.* **Model Answer:** A current mirror is a circuit configuration that copies a reference current $I_{REF}$ flowing through a diode-connected MOSFET into one or more output branches, maintaining a precise current ratio set by transistor aspect ratios $(W/L)$. The fundamental equation governing its operation (ignoring channel length modulation) is: $$I_{OUT} = I_{REF} \cdot \frac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1}$$ **Applications (any two):** 1. **Active load** in differential amplifiers and OTAs, providing high small-signal impedance. 2. **Biasing network** for analog sub-circuits, generating stable reference currents. 3. **Current steering** in DACs and gain stages. 4. **Level shifting** in source-follower and cascode configurations. > [!TIP] > **Valuation Key:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for two distinct applications. Avoid vague answers like "for biasing" вҖ” specify *which* circuit. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]** *Distinguish between simple and cascode current mirrors in terms of output resistance and voltage headroom.* **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Simple Current Mirror | Cascode Current Mirror | |---|---|---| | **Output resistance** | $R_{out} = r_{o2} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda I_{OUT}}$ (low, kО© range) | $R_{out} \approx g_{m4} r_{o4} r_{o2}$ (very high, MО©вҖ“GО© range) | | **Minimum output voltage** | $V_{OUT,min} = V_{OV}$ (one overdrive) | $V_{OUT,min} = 2V_{OV}$ (two overdrives) | | **Headroom efficiency** | Excellent | Poor (penalty of $V_{OV}$) | **Explanation:** The cascode mirror achieves higher output resistance by stacking a common-gate transistor (M4) on top of the mirroring transistor (M2). This bootstraps the drain of M2, dramatically reducing the effect of channel-length modulation. However, both M2 and M4 must remain in saturation, requiring an additional $V_{OV}$ of headroom. --- ### 5.2 PART B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Derive the current ratio equation of a simple NMOS current mirror. Show how channel-length modulation ($\lambda$) introduces error in the mirror accuracy. (7 marks) **(b)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an **NMOS cascode current mirror**. Derive its small-signal output resistance. (7 marks) --- **Solution to Q.A(a):** **Step 1 вҖ” Circuit Description** Consider a simple NMOS current mirror with: - M1: diode-connected (gate tied to drain), in the reference branch. - M2: mirroring transistor with the same gate-source voltage as M1. - Both sources connected to $V_{SS}$ (ground). **Step 2 вҖ” Reference Current (M1)** Since M1 is diode-connected, $V_{GS1} = V_{DS1}$, ensuring saturation. The reference current is: $$I_{REF} = \frac{1}{2} k'_n \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_1 (V_{GS1} - V_{THN})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS1})$$ **Step 3 вҖ” Output Current (M2)** M2 shares the same $V_{GS}$ but may have a different $V_{DS}$: $$I_{OUT} = \frac{1}{2} k'_n \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_2 (V_{GS2} - V_{THN})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS2})$$ **Step 4 вҖ” Take the Ratio** $$\frac{I_{OUT}}{I_{REF}} = \frac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1} \cdot \frac{1 + \lambda V_{DS2}}{1 + \lambda V_{DS1}}$$ **Step 5 вҖ” Ideal Case ($\lambda = 0$)** $$\boxed{\frac{I_{OUT}}{I_{REF}} = \frac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1}}$$ **Step 6 вҖ” Effect of $\lambda$ (Non-Ideal Error)** The error factor is: $$\epsilon_\lambda = \frac{1 + \lambda V_{DS2}}{1 + \lambda V_{DS1}}$$ If $V_{DS2} \neq V_{DS1}$, this factor deviates from unity. In typical analog circuits, $V_{DS1} = V_{GS1}$ (small, e.g., 0.7 V) while $V_{DS2}$ is large (e.g., $V_{DD}/2$). With $\lambda = 0.05$ V$^{-1}$: $$\epsilon_\lambda = \frac{1 + 0.05 \cdot 1.0}{1 + 0.05 \cdot 0.7} = \frac{1.050}{1.035} \approx 1.0145 \quad (\approx 1.45\% \text{ error})$$ **Conclusion:** Channel-length modulation causes a $\lambda$-dependent mismatch error, motivating the cascode configuration. --- **Solution to Q.A(b):** **Step 1 вҖ” Circuit Diagram** ``` VDD | M4 (cascode) | +-- Vout | M2 (mirror) | VSS ``` The reference branch is: M3 (cascode) on top of M1 (diode-connected), with M3's gate connected to M4's gate at bias voltage $V_B$. **Step 2 вҖ” Bias Condition** To keep M2 and M4 in saturation, $V_B$ is set such that $V_{GS4} = V_{OV,4}$ and $V_{GS2} = V_{OV,2}$. This forces $V_{DS2} = V_{DS1} = V_{OV,1}$, eliminating the $\lambda$ mismatch. **Step 3 вҖ” Small-Signal Analysis** Apply a test voltage $v_x$ at the output node. The small-signal model gives: - Current through M4 (common-gate): $i_x \approx g_{m4} v_x$ (where $v_x$ is the AC voltage at M2's drain as seen from M4's source). - This current develops a voltage across $r_{o2}$: $v_{gs4} \approx -i_x r_{o2}$. **Step 4 вҖ” Output Resistance** $$R_{out} = r_{o4} + r_{o2} + g_{m4} r_{o4} r_{o2}$$ **Step 5 вҖ” Dominant Term** Since $g_{m4} r_{o4} r_{o2} \gg r_{o4} + r_{o2}$: $$\boxed{R_{out} \approx g_{m4} r_{o4} r_{o2} \approx (g_m r_o)^2 / g_m = g_m r_o^2}$$ **Numerical Example:** For $g_m = 1$ mA/V, $r_o = 100$ kО©: $R_{out} \approx 1 \text{ mA/V} \times (100 \text{ k}\Omega)^2 = 10^9 \Omega = 1$ GО©. This is a $10^4$вҖ“$10^5$Г— improvement over the simple mirror. **Conclusion:** The cascode mirror offers near-ideal current-source behavior, with output impedance in the GО© range, at the cost of one extra $V_{OV}$ of voltage headroom. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **(a)** With the help of a circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **PMOS simple current mirror**. Derive the expression for the output current and comment on the effect of body effect. (7 marks) **(b)** Design a PMOS cascode current mirror to provide an output current of 200 ВөA from a reference current of 50 ВөA. Assume $\mu_p C_{ox} = 50$ ВөA/VВІ, $V_{THP} = -0.7$ V, $V_{OV} = 0.3$ V, and $\lambda = 0.04$ V$^{-1}$. Calculate the required aspect ratios and the small-signal output resistance at $V_{DS} = 1$ V. (7 marks) --- **Solution to Q.B(a):** **Step 1 вҖ” PMOS Mirror Circuit** A PMOS current mirror consists of two PMOS transistors with sources tied to $V_{DD}$: - M1: diode-connected PMOS in the reference branch. - M2: mirroring PMOS in the output branch. - The current $I_{REF}$ flows *into* the drain of M1 (i.e., from $V_{DD}$ toward $V_{SS}$). **Step 2 вҖ” Governing Equation** For PMOS in saturation, using $V_{SG} = \vert V_{GS} \vert$: $$I_D = \frac{1}{2} k'_p \left(\frac{W}{L}\right) (V_{SG} - \vert V_{THP} \vert)^2 (1 + \lambda \vert V_{SD} \vert)$$ **Step 3 вҖ” Current Ratio** $$\boxed{\frac{I_{OUT}}{I_{REF}} = \frac{(W/L)_2}{(W/L)_1} \cdot \frac{1 + \lambda_p \vert V_{SD2} \vert}{1 + \lambda_p \vert V_{SD1} \vert}}$$ **Step 4 вҖ” Effect of Body Effect** In PMOS mirrors, the source is typically tied to $V_{DD}$, but the **bulk (n-well)** is also at $V_{DD}$ (or at the most positive potential). Therefore, $V_{SB} = 0$, and ideally the body effect is **eliminated**. However, in **non-ideal layouts** where multiple PMOS share a common n-well at a different potential than the source, the threshold voltage increases: $$V_{THP} = V_{THP0} + \gamma \left(\sqrt{2\phi_F + V_{SB}} - \sqrt{2\phi_F}\right)$$ This causes $V_{SG}$ to shift, introducing a systematic error in $I_{OUT}$ that depends on $V_{OUT}$. In precision designs, **dedicated n-wells** are used per PMOS source to maintain $V_{SB} = 0$. --- **Solution to Q.B(b):** **Step 1 вҖ” Aspect Ratio Design** Given: - $I_{REF} = 50$ ВөA, $I_{OUT} = 200$ ВөA - $k'_p = \mu_p C_{ox} = 50$ ВөA/VВІ - $V_{OV} = 0.3$ V The mirroring ratio is $M = I_{OUT}/I_{REF} = 200/50 = 4$. **Step 2 вҖ” Choose $(W/L)_1$ for Reference Branch** Using $I_{REF} = \frac{1}{2} k'_p (W/L)_1 V_{OV}^2$: $$50 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 50 \cdot (W/L)_1 \cdot (0.3)^2$$ $$50 = 25 \cdot (W/L)_1 \cdot 0.09 = 2.25 \cdot (W/L)_1$$ $$(W/L)_1 = \frac{50}{2.25} \approx 22.22$$ **Step 3 вҖ” Choose $(W/L)_2$ for Output Branch** $$(W/L)_2 = 4 \cdot (W/L)_1 = 4 \cdot 22.22 \approx 88.89$$ **Step 4 вҖ” Small-Signal Parameters** $$g_m = \frac{2 I_{OUT}}{V_{OV}} = \frac{2 \cdot 200 \text{ ВөA}}{0.3 \text{ V}} = 1.333 \text{ mA/V}$$ $$r_o = \frac{1}{\lambda I_{OUT}} = \frac{1}{0.04 \cdot 200 \text{ ВөA}} = \frac{1}{8 \text{ ВөA/V}} = 125 \text{ k}\Omega$$ **Step 5 вҖ” Cascode Output Resistance** $$\boxed{R_{out} = g_m r_o^2 = 1.333 \text{ mA/V} \cdot (125 \text{ k}\Omega)^2 = 1.333 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 1.5625 \times 10^{10} = 20.83 \text{ M}\Omega}$$ **Conclusion:** The cascode mirror provides 20.83 MО© output impedance вҖ” a 167Г— improvement over the simple mirror (which would give $R_{out} = 125$ kО©). The trade-off is a headroom penalty of $2V_{OV} = 0.6$ V. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning вҖ” Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting $\lambda$ in current ratio:** Board answers often write $I_{OUT} = I_{REF} \cdot (W/L)_2 / (W/L)_1$ and stop there. The full equation with $\lambda$ is required for full marks. Deduct 2 marks. > 2. **Sign errors in PMOS:** Mixing up $V_{GS}$ vs $V_{SG}$, $V_{DS}$ vs $V_{SD}$, and the sign of $V_{THP}$. Always work with magnitudes for PMOS or consistently use negative values. > 3. **Cascode $R_{out}$ confusion:** Students sometimes write $R_{out} = g_m r_o$ (intrinsic gain), which is the gain of a single transistor, not the cascode output resistance. The correct form is $g_m r_o^2$ or $g_{m4} r_{o4} r_{o2}$. > 4. **Skipping the diagram:** A circuit diagram is **mandatory** for 14-mark answers. Without it, deduct 2вҖ“3 marks. > 5. **Unit errors:** Mixing $\mu$mВІ and cmВІ in mobility values, or omitting units in $g_m$ and $r_o$ calculations. Always state units. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist вҖ” KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2** - вң… **Current mirror** = circuit that copies $I_{REF}$ to output using diode-connected MOSFET + matched transistor. - вң… **Simple mirror equation** (ideal): $I_{OUT}/I_{REF} = (W/L)_2 / (W/L)_1$ - вң… **With $\lambda$:** multiply by $(1+\lambda V_{DS2})/(1+\lambda V_{DS1})$ вҖ” this is the **error term**. - вң… **Simple mirror output resistance:** $R_{out} = r_o = 1/(\lambda I_{OUT})$ - вң… **Cascode mirror output resistance:** $R_{out} \approx g_m r_o^2$ (order of magnitude: $g_{m4} r_{o4} r_{o2}$) - вң… **Cascode mirror headroom:** $V_{OUT,min} = 2V_{OV}$ (vs $V_{OV}$ for simple) вҖ” **headroom penalty**. - вң… **PMOS mirror:** $V_{SG} = |V_{GS}|$, currents flow from $V_{DD}$ to mirror; $k'_p \approx k'_n / 3$. - вң… **Body effect:** Eliminated in PMOS by tying n-well to source ($V_{SB} = 0$). In NMOS, often present. - вң… **Mismatch sources:** Aspect ratio mismatch + threshold voltage mismatch + channel-length modulation. - вң… **Wide-swing cascode:** Modified cascode that recovers $V_{OV}$ headroom вҖ” important for low-voltage design. - вң… **Applications:** OTA active loads, bias generators, differential pair tail current, ADC/DAC biasing, translinear loops. - вң… **Always draw the circuit diagram first** before solving any 14-mark KTU question. - вң… **Use proper units** in numerical problems: $I$ in ВөA or mA, $V$ in V, $g_m$ in mA/V, $r_o$ in kО© or MО©, $R_{out}$ in MО© or GО©. - вң… **KCL/KVL at the output node** is a common follow-up: if you connect a resistor $R_L$ to $V_{OUT}$, the current through $R_L$ is $I_{OUT} = I_{mirror}$, independent of $R_L$ (within compliance limits). > **Best of luck with your KTU 2024 Scheme ESE preparation!** Master the formulas, draw clean circuits, and explain the physical meaning behind every equation.

topics.title: CMOS Differential Amplifier: Differential Amplifier with Resistive, Current Source and Current Mirror Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT755 - MIXED SIGNAL CIRCUIT DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** CMOS Differential Amplifier: Differential Amplifier with Resistive, Current Source and Current Mirror Loads # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **CMOS Differential Amplifier** is a symmetric, two-input electronic building block that amplifies the *difference* between two input voltages while rejecting any signal common to both inputs. It consists of a matched pair of NMOS transistors (M1, M2) sharing a common source node that is biased by a tail current source ($I_{SS}$), and loads connected between the drains of M1/M2 and $V_{DD}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module-2 Definition:** The differential amplifier is the *fundamental analog gain stage* of every op-amp, comparator, ADC front-end, and mixer. Its three load variants вҖ” **resistive**, **current source**, and **current mirror** вҖ” trade off gain, output swing, common-mode rejection, and silicon area. The three classical CMOS differential amplifier load configurations studied in this module are: | Load Topology | Symbol | Key Property | |---|---|---| | Resistive Load (RD) | $R_D$ | Discrete-friendly, mid gain | | Current Source Load | PMOS biased by $V_{bias}$ | High gain, large swing | | Current Mirror Load | Diode-connected PMOS + mirror | Single-ended output, highest gain/area efficiency | ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy вҖ” "The Seesaw with a Hinge" Imagine a perfectly balanced **seesaw** floating on a frictionless pivot. Two children of equal weight sit on either end. The pivot is the *tail current source* вҖ” it controls the total effort available. - If both children push **equally** in the same direction, the seesaw does not tilt (this is the **common-mode** input вҖ” ideally rejected). - If they push in **opposite directions** (one pushes down, the other pushes up), the seesaw tilts sharply (this is the **differential-mode** input вҖ” amplified). - The **load resistors** at each end are the springs that convert the tilt back into a measurable voltage. The *current mirror load* is like tying one end of the seesaw to a clever pulley that forces the other end to move **twice as much** вҖ” converting a balanced push-pull into a single-sided output swing, just like converting a *double-ended* signal to *single-ended* (which the rest of the analog world prefers). > [!NOTE] > **Standard CMOS Design Metrics used throughout this module:** > - $g_m$ вҖ” transconductance (A/V) вҖ” typically **1 mA/V to 10 mA/V** in modern 180 nm CMOS > - $r_o$ вҖ” output resistance of a single MOSFET вҖ” typically **10 kО© to 100 kО©** > - $V_{ov} = V_{GS} - V_{TH}$ вҖ” overdrive voltage, typically **100 mV to 300 mV** > - $I_{SS}$ вҖ” tail current, typically **10 ОјA to 1 mA** --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Generic CMOS Differential Pair вҖ” Operating Principle Consider M1, M2 (matched NMOS) with sources tied together at node S, biased by a current source $I_{SS}$. **Bias condition (no signal applied):** $$I_{D1} = I_{D2} = \frac{I_{SS}}{2}$$ Each transistor is biased at $I_{D} = I_{SS}/2$. The $V_{GS}$ of each device settles to whatever value satisfies: $$I_{D} = \frac{1}{2}\mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2 (1 + \lambda V_{DS})$$ For small-signal analysis, $\lambda V_{DS}$ is dropped (unless $r_o$ is needed). ## 2.2 Differential-Mode Analysis (Half-Circuit Concept) Apply a pure differential input: $$v_{id} = v_{G1} - v_{G2}$$ By symmetry, the source node S is a **virtual ground** for differential signals. The circuit splits into two identical **half-circuits**, each with the source grounded. **Small-signal drain current of M1:** $$i_{d1} = \frac{g_m}{2} v_{id}$$ **Small-signal drain current of M2:** $$i_{d2} = -\frac{g_m}{2} v_{id}$$ ## 2.3 Common-Mode Analysis Apply a common-mode input $v_{ic}$ to both gates. The source node S now *moves* with the input through the finite output resistance of the tail current source, $R_{SS}$. Both transistors share the same $V_{GS}$, so drain currents are nearly equal вҖ” a small difference exists due to finite $R_{SS}$. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet вҖ” Differential Amplifier Loads > [!IMPORTANT] > **All quantities in the following table are in SI units.** Note the use of `\vert` instead of `\|` for absolute value to preserve markdown table integrity. | Parameter | Resistive Load ($R_D$) | Current Source Load (PMOS $r_{oP}$) | Current Mirror Load (PMOS M3-M4) | |---|---|---|---| | **Differential Gain $A_d$** | $g_m R_D$ | $g_{mN}(r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})$ | $g_{mN}(r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})$ | | **Common-Mode Gain $A_{cm}$** | $\dfrac{g_m R_D}{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}}$ | $\dfrac{g_{mN}(r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})}{1 + 2 g_{mN} R_{SS}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{2 g_{mP} R_{SS}}$ (approx.) | | **Output Resistance $R_{out}$** | $R_D$ | $r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP}$ | $r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP}$ | | **Input CM Range $V_{IC,min}$** | $V_{GS1} + V_{DS,sat,tail}$ | $V_{GS1} + V_{DS,sat,tail}$ | $V_{GS1} + V_{DS,sat,tail}$ | | **Input CM Range $V_{IC,max}$** | $V_{DD} - \dfrac{I_{SS} R_D}{2} - \vert V_{DS,sat,load}\vert + V_{GS1}$ | $V_{DD} - \vert V_{DS,sat,load}\vert$ | $V_{DD} - \vert V_{GS3}\vert$ | | **Output Swing (single-ended)** | $V_{DD} - I_{SS}R_D/2 \pm I_{SS}R_D/2$ | $2 V_{ov,N}$ (rail-limited) | $2 V_{ov,N}$ | | **Output Type** | Differential or single-ended | Differential or single-ended | **Single-ended from diff input** | | **Static Power $P_{diss}$** | $I_{SS}(V_{DD} + \vert V_{SS}\vert)$ | $I_{SS} V_{DD}$ (single supply) | $I_{SS} V_{DD}$ | | **CMRR Magnitude** | $\dfrac{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}}{2}$ | $\dfrac{1 + 2 g_{mN} R_{SS}}{2}$ | $g_{mN} g_{mP} R_{SS}(r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})$ | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The current mirror load provides **single-ended output directly from a differential input** вҖ” a property called *differential-to-single-ended conversion*. This eliminates the need for an extra active-load gain stage and saves silicon area, which is why nearly every CMOS op-amp (e.g., the classic two-stage Miller op-amp) uses it at the first stage. ## 2.5 Why Each Load Topology Exists вҖ” Engineering Rationale - **Resistive Load:** Used in **discrete/Mixed-Signal IC biasing stages**, in **class-AB drivers**, and in *educational circuits*. Disadvantages: requires large on-chip poly resistors (consumes area), produces mid-range gain, and is not the most area-efficient. - **Current Source (PMOS) Load:** Used in **telescopic cascode** and **folded cascode** op-amps. Provides high gain without requiring precise matching of a mirror; output is fully differential. - **Current Mirror (PMOS M3-M4) Load:** Used in **two-stage op-amp first stage**, **comparators**, and **active-RC filters**. The current mirror acts as a *differential-to-single-ended converter* вҖ” output current at the drain of M2 is **doubled** (one contribution from M1's current mirrored via M3-M4, plus the natural M2 current), giving effectively a 2Г— gain boost for free. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Differential Gain with Resistive Load **Circuit:** M1, M2 NMOS pair; $R_D$ at each drain; $I_{SS}$ tail source. ### Step A вҖ” Apply differential input $$v_{G1} = +\frac{v_{id}}{2}, \quad v_{G2} = -\frac{v_{id}}{2}$$ ### Step B вҖ” Use half-circuit symmetry Because the pair is perfectly symmetric, the source node S has zero AC voltage for purely differential excitation: $v_s = 0$. ### Step C вҖ” Drain current of M1 $$i_{d1} = g_m v_{gs1} = g_m \left(\frac{v_{id}}{2} - 0\right) = \frac{g_m v_{id}}{2}$$ ### Step D вҖ” Drain current of M2 $$i_{d2} = g_m v_{gs2} = g_m \left(-\frac{v_{id}}{2} - 0\right) = -\frac{g_m v_{id}}{2}$$ ### Step E вҖ” Differential output voltage $$v_{od} = v_{D1} - v_{D2} = -i_{d1} R_D - (-i_{d2} R_D) = -i_{d1} R_D + i_{d2} R_D$$ Substituting $i_{d1}$ and $i_{d2}$: $$v_{od} = -\frac{g_m v_{id}}{2} R_D + \left(-\frac{g_m v_{id}}{2}\right) R_D = -g_m R_D v_{id}$$ ### Step F вҖ” Differential Gain $$\boxed{A_d = \frac{v_{od}}{v_{id}} = -g_m R_D}$$ The negative sign indicates that $v_{D1}$ falls when $v_{G1}$ rises (inverting). The **magnitude** of differential gain is the canonical answer expected in KTU: $$\vert A_d \vert = g_m R_D$$ ### Step G вҖ” Single-ended output gain If only one output is taken: $$A_{v,single} = \frac{v_{D1}}{v_{id}} = -\frac{g_m R_D}{2}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Differential Gain with Current Source Load (Active PMOS) **Circuit:** M1, M2 NMOS; each drain loaded by a saturated PMOS current source with output resistance $r_{oP}$. The NMOS pair has $r_{oN}$ at each drain. ### Step A вҖ” Effective load at each drain At the drain of M1, the small-signal resistance is the parallel combination of $r_{o1}$ (NMOS) and $r_{oP1}$ (PMOS source). Similarly at M2. $$R_L = r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP} = \frac{r_{oN} \cdot r_{oP}}{r_{oN} + r_{oP}}$$ ### Step B вҖ” Apply half-circuit analysis For differential input, the source is a virtual ground; the half-circuit drain current is: $$i_{d} = \frac{g_m v_{id}}{2}$$ ### Step C вҖ” Differential output $$v_{od} = -i_{d} R_L - (-i_{d}) R_L = -2 i_{d} R_L$$ ### Step D вҖ” Gain $$\boxed{A_d = -g_m (r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})}$$ ### Step E вҖ” Numerical intuition For $g_m = 1 \text{ mA/V}$ and $r_{oN} = r_{oP} = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$: $$r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$$ $$A_d = -1\text{ mA/V} \times 10\text{ k}\Omega = -10 \text{ V/V}$$ Compare to a resistive load: $A_d = -g_m R_D = -10 \text{ V/V}$ only if $R_D = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, which on silicon consumes *significantly* more area than a tiny PMOS current source. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Differential Gain with Current Mirror Load (PMOS M3-M4) **Circuit:** M1, M2 NMOS; M3 is diode-connected PMOS in series with M1; M4 is the mirror copy of M3. Output is taken at the drain of M2 (which equals drain of M4). ### Step A вҖ” Diode-connected M3 sets the reference current With M1 and M3 in series and M1 carrying $I_{D1}$, M3 is forced to carry $I_{D1}$. Its $V_{GS3}$ is set by: $$I_{D1} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_p C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_3 (V_{SG3} - \vert V_{THP}\vert)^2$$ This same $V_{GS3}$ is applied to M4 (gate of M3 = gate of M4), so: $$I_{D4} = I_{D1}$$ ### Step B вҖ” Current at output node At the output node (drain of M2 = drain of M4), KCL gives: $$i_{out} = -i_{d2} - i_{d4} = -i_{d2} - (-i_{d1}) = -i_{d2} + i_{d1}$$ > *The minus sign on $i_{d4}$ arises because the current flowing **into** the drain of M4 is the negative of $i_{d4}$ defined as the small-signal current from drain to source.* ### Step C вҖ” Substitute the differential currents $$i_{d1} = \frac{g_m v_{id}}{2}, \quad i_{d2} = -\frac{g_m v_{id}}{2}$$ $$i_{out} = -\left(-\frac{g_m v_{id}}{2}\right) + \frac{g_m v_{id}}{2} = g_m v_{id}$$ ### Step D вҖ” Multiply by output resistance $$v_{out} = -i_{out} \cdot (r_{o2} \parallel r_{o4})$$ $$\boxed{A_d = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{id}} = -g_m (r_{o2} \parallel r_{o4})}$$ ### Step E вҖ” Why this is "2Г— the natural gain" A single-ended output from a half-circuit would normally yield $A_d = -\dfrac{g_m R_L}{2}$. The current mirror effectively **adds** the missing M1 current to the output, so the *effective* small-signal current at the output is doubled, restoring the full $g_m R_L$ gain. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4: Common-Mode Gain with Resistive Load **Circuit:** Apply $v_{ic}$ to both gates; tail current source has finite output resistance $R_{SS} = r_{o,tail}$. ### Step A вҖ” Gate-source voltage By symmetry, both gates rise by $v_{ic}$. The source node S also rises to some $v_s$: $$v_{gs1} = v_{gs2} = v_{ic} - v_s$$ ### Step B вҖ” Drain current of each transistor $$i_{d1} = i_{d2} = g_m (v_{ic} - v_s)$$ ### Step C вҖ” KCL at source node S The tail current source delivers $I_{SS}$. The total current leaving node S into M1+M2 is $i_{d1} + i_{d2} = 2 g_m(v_{ic} - v_s)$, which must equal the change in current through the tail source: $$2 g_m (v_{ic} - v_s) = \frac{v_s}{R_{SS}}$$ ### Step D вҖ” Solve for $v_s$ $$2 g_m v_{ic} R_{SS} - 2 g_m v_s R_{SS} = v_s$$ $$v_s (1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}) = 2 g_m v_{ic} R_{SS}$$ $$v_s = \frac{2 g_m R_{SS}}{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}} v_{ic}$$ ### Step E вҖ” Common-mode drain voltage $$v_{D1} = v_{D2} = -i_{d1} R_D = -g_m(v_{ic} - v_s) R_D$$ Substituting $v_s$: $$v_{D1} = -g_m \left(v_{ic} - \frac{2 g_m R_{SS}}{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}} v_{ic}\right) R_D$$ $$v_{D1} = -g_m R_D v_{ic} \left(\frac{1}{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}}\right)$$ ### Step F вҖ” Common-mode gain $$\boxed{A_{cm} = \frac{v_{D1}}{v_{ic}} = -\frac{g_m R_D}{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}}}$$ --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5: CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio) CMRR is the ratio of differential to common-mode gain magnitude: $$\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert$$ ### Resistive load: $$\text{CMRR}_{R} = \frac{g_m R_D}{\dfrac{g_m R_D}{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}}} = 1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}$$ ### Current source load: $$\text{CMRR}_{CS} = 1 + 2 g_{mN} R_{SS} \quad \text{(same form, different } R_{SS} \text{ effect)}$$ ### Current mirror load: Here $A_{cm}$ becomes the ratio of the **net common-mode current at output** to input. For a symmetric diff pair with current mirror, the common-mode current ideally cancels. The residual is: $$A_{cm,mirror} \approx \frac{1}{2 g_{mP} R_{SS}}$$ $$\text{CMRR}_{mirror} \approx 2 g_{mN} g_{mP} R_{SS} (r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})$$ This can be very large вҖ” typically **60 dB to 100 dB** in well-designed CMOS op-amps. > [!IMPORTANT] > **In dB:** $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(\vert A_d / A_{cm} \vert)$. A CMRR of **80 dB** means the differential amplifier amplifies differential signals **10,000Г—** more than common-mode signals. --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix вҖ” Comparison of Load Topologies > [!NOTE] > **Numerical reference design:** $g_m = 1$ mA/V, $r_{oN} = r_{oP} = 20$ kО©, $R_{SS} = 100$ kО©, $V_{DD} = 1.8$ V, $I_{SS} = 100$ ОјA. | Metric | Resistive ($R_D = 10$ kО©) | Current Source | Current Mirror | |---|---|---|---| | $\vert A_d \vert$ | 10 V/V (20 dB) | 10 V/V (20 dB) | 10 V/V (20 dB) | | $\vert A_{cm} \vert$ | 0.048 V/V | вүҲ 0.048 V/V | вүҲ 0.025 V/V | | **CMRR** | **208 (46 dB)** | **208 (46 dB)** | **400 (52 dB)** | | $R_{out}$ | 10 kО© | 10 kО© | 10 kО© | | Output swing | 1 V (mid rail Вұ 0.5 V) | 1.4 V (rail-to-rail minus $V_{ov}$) | 1.4 V | | $P_{diss}$ | 180 ОјW | 180 ОјW | 180 ОјW | | Area on silicon | **Large** (poly resistor) | Medium (2 PMOS) | **Smallest** (2 PMOS) | | Output type | Differential | Differential | **Single-ended** | | Matching sensitivity | Low | Medium | **High** (mirror needs $V_{GS3}=V_{GS4}$) | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 CMOS Differential Amplifier вҖ” Three Load Topologies (Block Schematic) ```mermaid graph TB subgraph IN["INPUT STAGE - M1 M2 NMOS Pair"] vG1["vG1 = vid/2 + vic"]:::inputNode vG2["vG2 = -vid/2 + vic"]:::inputNode M1[("M1 NMOS
drain at node D1")]:::nmos M2[("M2 NMOS
drain at node D2")]:::nmos Snode(("Common Source
Node S")):::source end subgraph BIAS["TAIL BIAS"] ISS[("ISS Current Source
with RSS")]:::tail end subgraph LOAD1["LOAD A - Resistive"] RD1[/"RD1"/]:::resistor RD2[/"RD2"/]:::resistor end subgraph LOAD2["LOAD B - Current Source"] MP1[("MP1 PMOS
vbias1")]:::pmos MP2[("MP2 PMOS
vbias2")]:::pmos end subgraph LOAD3["LOAD C - Current Mirror"] M3[("M3 Diode PMOS
mirror ref")]:::pmos M4[("M4 Mirror Copy
PMOS")]:::pmos end VDD[("VDD")]:::supply VSS[("VSS")]:::supply vG1 --> M1 vG2 --> M2 M1 --> Snode M2 --> Snode Snode --> ISS ISS --> VSS RD1 --> M1 RD2 --> M2 RD1 --> VDD RD2 --> VDD MP1 --> M1 MP2 --> M2 MP1 --> VDD MP2 --> VDD MP2 ---|vbias2| MP1 M3 --> M1 M4 --> M2 M3 ---|gate tied| M4 M3 --> VDD M4 --> VDD classDef inputNode fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef nmos fill:#bde0fe,stroke:#1d4e89,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef pmos fill:#ffadad,stroke:#c1121f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef tail fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d6a4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef resistor fill:#e0e1dd,stroke:#6c757d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef supply fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#bc4749,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Signal Flow вҖ” Differential vs Common-Mode Path ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Differential Input
vid = vG1 - vG2"]:::diff B["Common-Mode Input
vic = vG1 = vG2"]:::cm A -->|half-circuit| C["Source node S
is virtual ground"]:::vs C --> D["iD1 = +gm vid/2"]:::flow C --> E["iD2 = -gm vid/2"]:::flow D --> F["Load converts iD
to vod"]:::flow E --> F F --> G["Ad = -gm RL"]:::result B -->|symmetric| H["Source node S
moves with vic"]:::vs H --> I["iD1 = iD2 = gm vic/2"]:::flow I --> J["Acm = -gm RL
divided by 1+2gm RSS"]:::result G --> K["CMRR = Ad / Acm"]:::cmrr J --> K classDef diff fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#bc4749,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cm fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d6a4f,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef vs fill:#fdffb6,stroke:#cca000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef flow fill:#bde0fe,stroke:#1d4e89,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef result fill:#ffc8dd,stroke:#c9184a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef cmrr fill:#e0c3fc,stroke:#5a189a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Current Mirror Load вҖ” Internal Current Steering ```mermaid flowchart TB P1["M1 Drain Current
ID1 = ISS/2 - id"]:::flow1 P2["M2 Drain Current
ID2 = ISS/2 + id"]:::flow1 P1 --> N1["Forces ID1
through M3 diode"]:::node N1 --> N2["M3 sets VGS3
for M4"]:::node N2 --> N3["M4 copies ID1
at M2 drain"]:::node P2 --> N4["M2 carries ID2
naturally"]:::node N3 --> N5["M2 and M4 currents
ADD at output node"]:::final N4 --> N5 N5 --> N6["iout = ID2 - ID1
= 2 id = gm vid"]:::final N6 --> N7["vout = -gm vid x Ro2||Ro4"]:::final classDef flow1 fill:#bde0fe,stroke:#1d4e89,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef node fill:#fdffb6,stroke:#cca000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef final fill:#ffc8dd,stroke:#c9184a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]` **Define Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) of a CMOS differential amplifier. Mention the typical CMRR range expected in a well-designed CMOS op-amp first stage.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > CMRR is defined as the ratio of the magnitude of the differential-mode gain to the magnitude of the common-mode gain of a differential amplifier: > $$\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert$$ > Expressed in decibels: $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\vert A_d / A_{cm} \vert$. > > **(1 mark)** for definition, **(1 mark)** for the formula in linear and dB, **(1 mark)** for typical value. A well-designed CMOS differential amplifier with a current mirror load achieves **CMRR between 60 dB and 100 dB** in production silicon. Higher CMRR requires larger tail-source output resistance (e.g., cascode tail bias). ### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]` **List any three advantages of a current mirror load over a resistive load in a CMOS differential amplifier.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Higher DC gain per unit silicon area** вҖ” a single small PMOS current source provides $r_o$ of 10 kО©вҖ“100 kО©, equivalent to a large on-chip poly resistor, saving die area. **(1 mark)** 2. **Single-ended output directly available** from a fully differential input, eliminating the need for a separate differential-to-single-ended converter. **(1 mark)** 3. **Inherently active biasing** вҖ” current sources set operating points automatically, providing better PSRR and temperature stability than passive resistors. **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 Part B вҖ” Module Internal Choice (14 Marks each) ### **Question A (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]` **a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for the **differential-mode gain** of a CMOS differential amplifier using a **PMOS current source load**. State the necessary assumptions. **(7 marks)** **b)** For the same circuit, derive the **common-mode gain** and the resulting **CMRR expression**. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Differential-mode gain вҖ” Step-by-step:** 1. Draw the circuit: NMOS pair M1, M2 with PMOS current sources M3, M4 as loads. Show the tail current source $I_{SS}$ and the differential input $v_{id}$ applied across the gates. **(1 mark for diagram)** 2. State assumption: M1 = M2 (matched) and M3 = M4 (matched), with output resistance $r_{oN}$ and $r_{oP}$ respectively. **(1 mark)** 3. Apply differential input $v_{G1} = v_{id}/2$, $v_{G2} = -v_{id}/2$. By symmetry, source S is a virtual ground for differential mode. **(1 mark)** 4. Drain current of M1 half-circuit: $i_{d1} = (g_m/2) v_{id}$. **(1 mark)** 5. The effective load at each drain: $R_L = r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP}$. **(1 mark)** 6. Differential output: $v_{od} = -2 i_{d1} R_L = -g_m v_{id} (r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})$. **(1 mark)** 7. Final result: $$A_d = \frac{v_{od}}{v_{id}} = -g_{mN} (r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP}) \quad \textbf{(1 mark for boxed result)}$$ **(b) Common-mode gain and CMRR:** 1. Apply $v_{ic}$ to both gates; source S rises to $v_s$. **(1 mark)** 2. KCL at S: $2 g_m (v_{ic} - v_s) = v_s / R_{SS}$, giving $v_s = \dfrac{2 g_m R_{SS}}{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}} v_{ic}$. **(2 marks)** 3. Common-mode drain current of each transistor: $i_{d,cm} = g_m (v_{ic} - v_s)$. **(1 mark)** 4. Common-mode output: $v_{D,cm} = -g_m (r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP}) (v_{ic} - v_s)$. Substitute $v_s$: **(1 mark)** $$A_{cm} = \frac{v_{D,cm}}{v_{ic}} = -\frac{g_m (r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})}{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$ 5. CMRR: $$\text{CMRR} = \left\vert \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}} \right\vert = 1 + 2 g_m R_{SS} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]` **a)** Explain the operation of a **CMOS differential amplifier with PMOS current-mirror (active) load** as a differential-to-single-ended converter. Derive the **differential gain**. **(7 marks)** **b)** A CMOS differential amplifier with $I_{SS} = 200\ \mu\text{A}$ and $g_m = 2\text{ mA/V}$ uses load resistors $R_D = 5\text{ k}\Omega$ in each branch and a tail source with $R_{SS} = 250\text{ k}\Omega$. Calculate $\vert A_d \vert$, $\vert A_{cm} \vert$, and **CMRR in dB**. Comment on the design. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Current mirror load operation (7 marks):** 1. Circuit: M1, M2 NMOS; M3 diode-connected PMOS (mirror reference), M4 is mirror copy. Output is single-ended at drain of M2/M4. **(2 marks for diagram + labeling)** 2. Bias: M1 and M3 in series carry $I_{D1} = I_{SS}/2 - i_d$. M3's $V_{GS3}$ is set by this current and replicated on M4. **(1 mark)** 3. M4 therefore copies $I_{D1}$ at the M2 drain. KCL at output: $i_{out} = i_{D2} - i_{D4} = (I_{SS}/2 + i_d) - (I_{SS}/2 - i_d) = 2 i_d$. **(2 marks)** 4. With $i_d = (g_m/2) v_{id}$: $i_{out} = g_m v_{id}$. **(1 mark)** 5. Final result: $$A_d = -g_m (r_{o2} \parallel r_{o4}) \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$ **(b) Numerical calculation (7 marks):** Given: $I_{SS} = 200\ \mu\text{A}$, $g_m = 2\text{ mA/V}$, $R_D = 5\text{ k}\Omega$, $R_{SS} = 250\text{ k}\Omega$. **Differential gain:** $$\vert A_d \vert = g_m R_D = (2 \times 10^{-3}) \times (5 \times 10^3) = 10 \text{ V/V}$$ **1 mark** for substitution, **1 mark** for result. In dB: $20 \log_{10}(10) = 20 \text{ dB}$. **1 mark.** **Common-mode gain:** $$\vert A_{cm} \vert = \frac{g_m R_D}{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}}$$ Compute the denominator: $$1 + 2 g_m R_{SS} = 1 + 2 \times (2 \times 10^{-3}) \times (250 \times 10^3) = 1 + 1000 = 1001$$ Therefore: $$\vert A_{cm} \vert = \frac{10}{1001} \approx 9.99 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V/V} \approx 0.01 \text{ V/V}$$ **2 marks** for substitution + result. In dB: $20 \log_{10}(0.01) = -40 \text{ dB}$. **0.5 mark** (bonus only if asked). **CMRR:** $$\text{CMRR} = \frac{\vert A_d \vert}{\vert A_{cm} \vert} = \frac{10}{0.01} = 1000$$ $$\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(1000) = 60 \text{ dB}$$ **1.5 marks** for result. **Design comment (1 mark):** A CMRR of 60 dB is acceptable for general-purpose analog circuits (e.g., audio amplifiers) but is **insufficient for precision instrumentation** (which requires > 100 dB). To improve CMRR, the tail current source should be cascoded (e.g., using a cascode PMOS current mirror with output resistance > 1 MО©) or replaced by a wide-swing cascode bias. Additionally, the input pair should be sized for a high $g_m$ at the given $I_{SS}$ вҖ” i.e., use a small L and large W for M1, M2. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common pitfalls that cost marks in KTU 2024 ESE valuation:** > > 1. **Forgetting the half-circuit concept** вҖ” many students attempt to write full KCL on the diff pair and run out of time. **Use the symmetry shortcut**: source is virtual ground for differential input, and both halves are identical. > > 2. **Sign errors on the current mirror load** вҖ” when applying KCL at the output node of a current-mirror-loaded diff amp, students often write $i_{out} = i_{D2} + i_{D4}$ instead of $i_{out} = i_{D2} - i_{D4}$. The minus sign is critical because the current mirror *subtracts* M1's mirrored current from M2's drain current. > > 3. **Confusing $R_D$ and $r_o$** вҖ” $R_D$ is a *physical* resistor (for resistive load), while $r_o$ is the *output resistance* of a saturated MOSFET. Mixing them up in the gain formula gives incorrect answers. > > 4. **CMRR in linear vs. dB** вҖ” the KTU paper often asks for CMRR in **dB**; forgetting the $20 \log_{10}$ conversion loses 1 mark. > > 5. **Input common-mode range limits** вҖ” students frequently state only one of the two limits ($V_{IC,min}$ or $V_{IC,max}$). Always quote **both**. > > 6. **Static power formula** вҖ” $P_{diss} = I_{SS} \cdot V_{DD}$ (for single-supply CMOS) is **not** $I_{SS} \cdot (V_{DD} + V_{SS})$; the latter is for a bipolar op-amp with dual supply. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-minute revision вҖ” must-know points for KTU 2024 Module-2 viva & written exam:** - **CMOS differential pair =** matched NMOS pair M1/M2 + tail current source $I_{SS}$. **(Define the topology in 1 line.)** - **Differential input** = $v_{id} = v_{G1} - v_{G2}$ вҶ’ source node S is **virtual ground** вҶ’ use half-circuit analysis. - **Drain current split:** $i_{d1} = +(g_m/2) v_{id}$ and $i_{d2} = -(g_m/2) v_{id}$. - **Resistive load gain:** $\vert A_d \vert = g_m R_D$, **single-ended** $= g_m R_D / 2$. - **Current source load gain:** $\vert A_d \vert = g_{mN} (r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})$. - **Current mirror load gain:** $\vert A_d \vert = g_{mN} (r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})$, **single-ended output** from differential input вҖ” current mirror *doubles* the effective small-signal output current. - **Common-mode gain (resistive):** $A_{cm} = -g_m R_D / (1 + 2 g_m R_{SS})$. - **CMRR (resistive/source-load):** $1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}$. - **CMRR (current mirror load):** $2 g_{mN} g_{mP} R_{SS} (r_{oN} \parallel r_{oP})$ вҖ” typically the **highest**. - **Power dissipation:** $P_{diss} = I_{SS} V_{DD}$ (single-supply CMOS). - **Input common-mode range:** $V_{GS1} + V_{DS,sat,tail} \leq V_{IC} \leq V_{DD} - V_{GS3} + V_{THP}$ (for mirror load). - **Output swing (mirror load):** $2 V_{ov,N}$ below $V_{DD}$. - **Design rule of thumb:** For $g_m = I_{SS}/(2 V_{ov})$, choose $V_{ov} \approx 200$ mV and $L \geq 0.5\ \mu\text{m}$ to keep $r_o$ high and matching reasonable. - **Current mirror load uses** = first stage of nearly every CMOS op-amp (e.g., 2-stage Miller, folded cascode) because of area efficiency + single-ended output + high gain. - **CMRR in dB** = $20 \log_{10}(\vert A_d / A_{cm} \vert)$. **Numerical:** CMRR = 1000 вҮ’ 60 dB. - **Half-circuit trick** works *only* for differential excitation; never apply it for common-mode. ---

topics.title: MOS Telescopic Cascode Amplifier (only voltage gain and input and output impedance of the circuits)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT755 - MIXED SIGNAL CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** MOS Telescopic Cascode Amplifier (only voltage gain and input and output impedance of the circuits)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme вҖ” PEECT755)**
> A **MOS Telescopic Cascode Amplifier** is a high-gain, single-stage differential amplifier in which the input differential pair transistors are **stacked (cascoded)** beneath current-source load transistors that are also cascoded. The structure resembles an extended, telescopic arrangement of MOSFETs between the supply rails, hence the name *telescopic*. It is the most power-efficient cascode topology, achieving very high intrinsic DC gain by multiplying the transconductance of the input device with the **boosted output resistance** produced by the cascode stacking.

The amplifier is called **telescopic** because the four transistors (M1, M2 forming the NMOS cascode pair and M3, M4 forming the PMOS cascode pair) are stacked in series between **VDD** and **VSS**, similar to the way a telescope slides one section into another to extend its length.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” The "Building Stack" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy**
> Imagine a **multi-storey building** where:
> - The **ground floor (M1, M3)** = input transistors that convert input voltage (Vin) into a current.
> - The **first floor (M2, M4)** = cascode transistors that act as "buffer walls," preventing the current from leaking sideways, forcing all of it to flow upward toward the load.
> - The **roof (current sources I1, I2)** = the biasing ceiling that limits the total current.
>
> The more "floors" you stack, the harder it is for outside forces (load) to change the voltage at the output вҖ” thus **higher output impedance** and **higher voltage gain**. This is exactly what the telescopic cascode does electrically.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical CMOS Value (0.18 Вөm / 65 nm node) |
|---|---|
| **Intrinsic Gain (gmВ·ro)** of a single MOSFET | **10 вҖ“ 100 V/V** (вүҲ 20 вҖ“ 40 dB) |
| **DC Voltage Gain of Telescopic Cascode** | **(gmВ·ro)ВІ** вүҲ **100 вҖ“ 10 000 V/V** (вүҲ 40 вҖ“ 80 dB) |
| **Input Impedance (Zin)** | **Effectively Infinite (вү« 1 GО©)** вҖ” purely gate-input |
| **Output Impedance (Zout)** | **вүҲ gmВ·roВІ** вҖ” orders of magnitude higher than a common-source stage |
| **Power Consumption** | **Lowest** among cascode families (single bias current path) |
| **Output Voltage Swing** | **2В·(VOV + VDSsat)** вҖ” limited, the main drawback |

## 1.4 Circuit Topology Snapshot

The fully-differential telescopic cascode amplifier consists of:
- **M1, M2** вҖ” NMOS differential input pair with cascode (M2).
- **M3, M4** вҖ” PMOS current-mirror load with cascode (M4).
- **I1, I2** вҖ” Tail and load bias current sources.
- **Vin+ / VinвҲ’** вҖ” Differential inputs at the gates of M1/M2.
- **Vout+ / VoutвҲ’** вҖ” Differential outputs at the drains of M2/M4.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Telescopic"?** вҖ” If you draw the cross-section (VDD on top, VSS at the bottom), the four transistors *telescope* into one another in vertical alignment between the two supply rails. Only **one** bias current flows through the entire stack, hence the lowest power dissipation of all cascode topologies.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle вҖ” Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> Read these six operational bullets carefully; each maps directly to the small-signal derivations in Section 3.

1. **DC Bias Setup** вҖ” A tail current source **I1** forces a fixed DC current I/2 to flow through each branch. The PMOS current mirror (M3вҖ“M4) maintains this bias on the load side.
2. **Differential Input (Vin+ вҲ’ VinвҲ’)** вҖ” Applied to the gates of **M1** (NMOS) and **M3** (PMOS). This steers a small-signal current **id = gmВ·vd/2** into each branch.
3. **Cascode Buffer Action** вҖ” The cascode transistors **M2 (NMOS)** and **M4 (PMOS)** are held at a **fixed VGS** (biased by a stable DC voltage). Their role is to **isolate the high-impedance drain of M1/M3 from the low-impedance output node**.
4. **Current Summation** вҖ” The small-signal drain currents of M1 and M3 (which are 180В° out of phase) add at the output node, producing a differential current **iod = gmВ·Vin,d**.
5. **Output Voltage Generation** вҖ” This differential current flows through the high cascode output resistance, producing a large output voltage: **Vout = iod Г— Zout**.
6. **Gain Magnification** вҖ” Because **Zout вүҲ gmВ·roВІ**, the overall voltage gain reaches **(gmВ·ro)ВІ**, the theoretical maximum for a single-pole telescopic topology.

## 2.2 The Core "Why" Behind the Topology

| Design Goal | How the Telescopic Cascode Achieves It |
|---|---|
| **Higher Gain** | Stacking two transistors multiplies the output impedance by a factor of **gmВ·ro**. |
| **Lower Power** | Only **one** bias current path (no extra branches like in folded cascode). |
| **Better High-Frequency Response** | Reduced Miller effect because the cascode shields the high-gain node. |
| **Pole-Splitting** | The cascode node introduces a non-dominant pole that pushes the dominant pole to lower frequency. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table вҖ” every KTU exam question on this topic hinges on these equations.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Input Impedance Zin** | $Z_{in} = \infty$ | MOSFET gate is insulated; DC input current вүҲ 0. |
| 2 | **Output Impedance вҖ” single cascode branch** | $R_{out,branch} = r_{o1} + r_{o2} + g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2} \approx g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2}$ | M1 = common-source, M2 = cascode. |
| 3 | **Output Impedance вҖ” telescopic (NMOS branch)** | $R_{out,N} \approx g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2}$ | NMOS side, drain of M2 looking up. |
| 4 | **Output Impedance вҖ” telescopic (PMOS branch)** | $R_{out,P} \approx g_{m4}\,r_{o3}\,r_{o4}$ | PMOS side, drain of M4 looking down. |
| 5 | **Total Output Impedance** | $R_{out} = R_{out,N} \;\vert\vert\; R_{out,P}$ | Two branches in parallel between Vout+ and VoutвҲ’. |
| 6 | **Equivalent Gain Transconductance** | $G_{m} = g_{m1} = g_{m3}$ | Differential pair transconductance. |
| 7 | **Differential Voltage Gain (Magnitude)** | $\vert A_{v}\vert = g_{m1}\cdot\bigl[(g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2})\;\vert\vert\;(g_{m4}\,r_{o3}\,r_{o4})\bigr]$ | Negative sign indicates 180В° phase inversion. |
| 8 | **Approximate Gain (matched devices)** | $A_{v} \approx -\dfrac{1}{2}\,(g_{m}\,r_{o})^{2}$ | When NMOS and PMOS are identical and bias-symmetric. |
| 9 | **Intrinsic Gain of single MOSFET** | $A_{v0} = g_{m}\,r_{o}$ | Reference benchmark (вүҲ 20вҖ“50 V/V in sub-micron CMOS). |
| 10 | **Cascode Gain Multiplier** | $A_{v,tele} \approx (g_{m}\,r_{o})^{2}$ | Telescopic topology's main selling point. |

> **Boundary Conditions to Remember**
> - All `gm`, `ro` are **small-signal parameters** evaluated at the DC operating point.
> - The approximation $R_{out,branch} \approx g_m\,r_o^2$ holds when $g_m\,r_o \gg 1$, which is **always true** for an MOSFET in saturation.
> - Body effect is neglected here (assumed source-connected or in triple-well process).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Where is the Telescopic Cascode actually used?**

- **First stage of high-gain op-amps** (e.g., in two-stage Miller-compensated op-amps) where you need maximum gain in minimum power.
- **Read-out amplifiers** in switched-capacitor circuits and data converters.
- **Low-dropout (LDO) regulators** as the error amplifier.
- **Discrete-time integrators** in sigma-delta (ОЈ-О”) ADCs.
- **Biomedical front-ends** where power budget is tight but gain must be high.

Its main drawback вҖ” **limited output swing** вҖ” is why designers switch to a **folded cascode** when the output must swing rail-to-rail.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 вҖ” Output Resistance of a Single Cascode Branch

We begin with the simpler **NMOS cascode branch** (M1 below, M2 on top). A test voltage `vtest` is applied at the drain of M2 and the test current `itest` is injected.

**Step 1 вҖ” Write KCL at the drain of M1 (node X):**

The small-signal current flowing into node X from M1 equals the current that must leave through `ro1` into M2's source.

$$i_{test,X} = \frac{v_x}{r_{o1}} + g_{m2}\,v_{gs2}$$

> *Explanation:* `vx` is the small-signal voltage at node X; `vx / ro1` is the leakage through `ro1`, and `gm2В·vgs2` is the dependent current source of M2.

**Step 2 вҖ” Apply KVL around M2's gate-source loop:**

The gate of M2 is AC-grounded (biased by a fixed DC voltage), so:

$$v_{gs2} = 0 - v_x = -v_x$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Substitute back:**

$$i_{test,X} = \frac{v_x}{r_{o1}} + g_{m2}\,(-v_x) = \frac{v_x}{r_{o1}} - g_{m2}\,v_x$$

This is wrong because we must also account for the current through `ro2`. Let's redo it correctly:

**Step 1 (corrected) вҖ” Sum currents leaving node X:**

The total current flowing from node X through `ro1` to ground AND through `ro2` to `vtest` AND through the dependent source of M2 must equal zero (KCL with `itest` injected):

$$\begin{aligned}
i_{test} &= \frac{v_x}{r_{o1}} + \frac{v_x - v_{test}}{r_{o2}} + g_{m2}\,v_{gs2} \\
v_{gs2} &= -v_x \quad \text{(gate AC-grounded)} \\
i_{test} &= \frac{v_x}{r_{o1}} + \frac{v_x - v_{test}}{r_{o2}} - g_{m2}\,v_x
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Express `vx` in terms of `vtest`:**

$$\begin{aligned}
i_{test} \cdot r_{o1}\,r_{o2} &= v_x\,r_{o2} + (v_x - v_{test})\,r_{o1} - g_{m2}\,v_x\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2} \\
&= v_x\,(r_{o1} + r_{o2}) - v_{test}\,r_{o1} - g_{m2}\,v_x\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Solve for `vx`:**

$$v_x\,[\,r_{o1} + r_{o2} + g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2}\,] = i_{test}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2} + v_{test}\,r_{o1}$$

$$v_x = \frac{i_{test}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2} + v_{test}\,r_{o1}}{r_{o1} + r_{o2} + g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2}}$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Recognize that the test voltage equals the current times the branch output resistance:**

$$v_{test} = i_{test}\cdot R_{out,N} - \text{(feedback term from }v_x\text{)}$$

After careful algebra (using `vtest = itestВ·Rout` and the fact that `vtest` is the dominant voltage), the standard textbook result emerges:

$$\boxed{\;R_{out,N} = r_{o1} + r_{o2} + g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2}\;}$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Apply the dominant-term approximation:**

Since $g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2} \gg r_{o1} + r_{o2}$ for typical MOSFET operation:

$$R_{out,N} \approx g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 вҖ” Total Output Resistance of the Telescopic Cascode

The telescopic cascode has **two cascode branches** in parallel between the differential outputs:

- **NMOS side** (looking up from Vout+ through M2): $R_{out,N} = g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2}$
- **PMOS side** (looking down from Vout+ through M4): $R_{out,P} = g_{m4}\,r_{o3}\,r_{o4}$

These two resistances are in parallel because they are connected between the same two output nodes (Vout+ and VoutвҲ’ for differential, or Vout and AC-ground for single-ended):

$$\begin{aligned}
R_{out} &= R_{out,N} \;\vert\vert\; R_{out,P} \\
&= (g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2})\;\vert\vert\;(g_{m4}\,r_{o3}\,r_{o4})
\end{aligned}$$

> **Numerical Insight:** If each branch has $R_{out} \approx 1$ MО©, the parallel combination is **вүҲ 500 kО©**, which is still 100Г— larger than a common-source stage.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 вҖ” Voltage Gain of the Telescopic Cascode

**Step 1 вҖ” Identify the equivalent transconductance:**

For a differential input, each input transistor contributes a small-signal current of $g_{m1}\cdot(v_{in}^{+}-v_{in}^{-})/2$. The combined differential output current is:

$$i_{od} = g_{m1}\cdot v_{id}$$

where $v_{id} = v_{in}^{+} - v_{in}^{-}$.

**Step 2 вҖ” Apply Ohm's Law at the output:**

$$v_{od} = i_{od}\cdot R_{out} = g_{m1}\cdot v_{id}\cdot R_{out}$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Compute the differential voltage gain:**

$$\boxed{\;A_{v} = \frac{v_{od}}{v_{id}} = -g_{m1}\,\bigl[(g_{m2}\,r_{o1}\,r_{o2})\;\vert\vert\;(g_{m4}\,r_{o3}\,r_{o4})\bigr]\;}$$

The negative sign indicates the standard 180В° phase inversion of the differential amplifier.

**Step 4 вҖ” Apply the matched-device simplification:**

If NMOS and PMOS are matched ($g_{m1} = g_{m3}$, $r_{o1} = r_{o3}$, etc.) and the cascode devices are also matched, the two parallel branches have equal resistance $g_m r_o^2$ each, so:

$$R_{out} = \dfrac{g_{m}\,r_{o}^{2}}{2}$$

Substituting:

$$A_{v} = -g_{m}\cdot\dfrac{g_{m}\,r_{o}^{2}}{2} = -\dfrac{1}{2}\,(g_{m}\,r_{o})^{2}$$

The factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ comes from the parallel combination of the two halves (NMOS branch and PMOS branch).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 вҖ” Input Impedance

The input is applied to the **gate** of the MOSFET. Because the gate of a MOSFET is insulated by a thin SiOвӮӮ layer:

$$Z_{in} = \dfrac{v_{in}}{i_{in}} \to \infty$$

In practice, at DC the input impedance is limited only by leakage paths (вү« 1 GО©). At high frequency, the gate capacitance $C_{gs}$ dominates and $Z_{in}$ becomes capacitive, but for the **low-frequency KTU analysis**, we state:

$$\boxed{\;Z_{in} \approx \infty\;}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix вҖ” Summary of Final Results

| Quantity | Telescopic Cascode | Common-Source (for comparison) | Folded Cascode |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Zin** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| **Zout** | $g_m\,r_o^{2}$ (per branch) | $r_o$ | $g_m\,r_o^{2}$ |
| **Voltage Gain $\vert A_v\vert$** | $(g_m\,r_o)^{2}$ | $g_m\,r_o$ | $(g_m\,r_o)^{2}$ |
| **Output Swing** | Low | High | Medium-High |
| **Power** | Lowest | Medium | Higher (extra current branch) |

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A 0.18 Вөm CMOS telescopic cascode amplifier is biased with $I_{tail} = 100$ ВөA. Each transistor has $g_m = 1$ mA/V and $r_o = 20$ kО©. Compute the voltage gain.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
g_m\,r_o &= (1\;\text{mA/V})\times(20\;\text{k}\Omega) = 20\;\text{V/V} \\
R_{out,branch} &= g_m\,r_o^{2} = (1\;\text{mA/V})\times(20\;\text{k}\Omega)^{2} \\
&= 10^{-3}\times 4\times 10^{8}\;\Omega = 4\times 10^{5}\;\Omega = 400\;\text{k}\Omega \\
R_{out,total} &= 400\;\text{k}\Omega \;\vert\vert\; 400\;\text{k}\Omega = 200\;\text{k}\Omega \\
A_v &= -g_m\cdot R_{out} = -(1\;\text{mA/V})\times(200\;\text{k}\Omega) \\
&= -200\;\text{V/V}
\end{aligned}$$

Equivalently, $A_v \approx -\tfrac{1}{2}(g_m r_o)^2 = -\tfrac{1}{2}(20)^2 = -200$ V/V. вң“ **Matches!**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram вҖ” Telescopic Cascode Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **signal flow and functional architecture** of the telescopic cascode, including the small-signal model relationships.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeVin["Vin Differential Input"] --> nodeM1["M1 NMOS Input Transistor"]
    nodeVinB["Vin Negative Input"] --> nodeM3["M3 PMOS Input Transistor"]
    nodeM1 --> nodeX1["Cascode Node X1"]
    nodeM3 --> nodeX2["Cascode Node X2"]
    nodeX1 --> nodeM2["M2 NMOS Cascode Buffer"]
    nodeX2 --> nodeM4["M4 PMOS Cascode Buffer"]
    nodeM2 --> nodeVoutP["Vout Positive Drain Node"]
    nodeM4 --> nodeVoutN["Vout Negative Drain Node"]
    nodeBiasA["DC Bias Vb1 NMOS Cascode Gate"] --> nodeM2
    nodeBiasB["DC Bias Vb2 PMOS Cascode Gate"] --> nodeM4
    nodeTail["Tail Current Source I1"] --> nodeM1
    nodeLoad["Load Current Source I2"] --> nodeM4
    nodeZin["Zin Infinite at Gate"] -.-> nodeVin
    nodeZout["Zout gm times ro squared Parallel"] -.-> nodeVoutP
    nodeZout -.-> nodeVoutN
    nodeGain["Voltage Gain Av equals gm times Zout"] -.-> nodeVoutP
```

## 4.2 ASCII Cross-Section of the Telescopic Stack

```
                VDD  (Top Rail)
                 в”Ӯ
        в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
        в”Ӯ   M3  в”Ӯ   M4   в”Ӯ   вҶҗ PMOS cascode pair (loads)
        в”Ӯ (PMOS)в”Ӯ (PMOS) в”Ӯ
        в”ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Ө
        в”Ӯ   M1  в”Ӯ   M2   в”Ӯ   вҶҗ NMOS cascode pair (inputs)
        в”Ӯ (NMOS)в”Ӯ (NMOS) в”Ӯ
        в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                 в”Ӯ
                VSS  (Bottom Rail)
                 вҶ‘
            Tail Current Source
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Between VDD and VSS you see **four transistors stacked vertically**. The current flows from top to bottom in a single path вҖ” this is what makes the topology "telescopic."

## 4.3 Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA["Input Transconductor Stage gm1"] --> blockB["Current Summation Node"]
    blockB --> blockC["Cascode Buffer Stage Zout boosted"]
    blockC --> blockD["Output Node Vout"]
    blockE["PMOS Current Mirror Load"] --> blockB
    blockF["DC Bias Network for M2 M4 gates"] --> blockC
    blockG["Tail Bias Current I1"] --> blockA
```

## 4.4 Pole-Zero Behaviour (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    poleIn["Input Pole at Gate due to Cgs of M1"] --> sumNode["Summing Junction"]
    sumNode --> casPole["Cascode Pole at Node X1"]
    casPole --> outPole["Output Pole at Vout due to CL and Zout"]
    sumNode --> gainAmp["Gain Stage gm1 times Zout"]
    gainAmp --> outPole
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway from the Architecture:** The cascode node creates a **non-dominant high-frequency pole**, while the dominant pole sits at the output due to the load capacitance $C_L$. This pole-splitting is precisely why the telescopic cascode maintains good phase margin.

---
