# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam вҖ” May 2024 Model]

**Explain the necessity of frequency compensation in a two-stage CMOS op-amp.**

**Model Answer:**
A two-stage CMOS op-amp has at least two high-impedance nodes (output of stage 1 and op-amp output). Each of these nodes, combined with the parasitic capacitance at that node, forms a low-frequency pole. When such an op-amp is connected in a unity-gain feedback loop, the phase contributed by these multiple poles causes the loop phase to reach $-180В°$ before the loop gain has fallen to unity, violating the Barkhausen stability criterion. This leads to **closed-loop oscillation**. Frequency compensation reshapes the open-loop frequency response by splitting the poles apart, so that the amplifier effectively behaves like a single-pole system with a phase margin $\geq 60В°$. The most common technique is **Miller compensation**, in which a capacitor $C_C$ is placed across the high-gain second stage to perform **pole splitting**.

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam вҖ” Dec 2023 Model]

**Define the Miller effect and write the Miller-compensated transfer function pole-zero expression for a two-stage op-amp.**

**Model Answer:**
The Miller effect refers to the multiplication of an impedance connected between the input and output of an inverting amplifier. A capacitor $C$ connected between the input and output of an inverting stage of gain $-A$ appears at the input as a larger capacitance:

$$
C_{in} = C(1 + A)
$$

because the small voltage across $C$ (input voltage minus inverted output) drives a large displacement current through $C$.

The Miller-compensated two-stage op-amp's small-signal transfer function (ignoring the nulling resistor) is:

$$
A(s) = \frac{A_{OL}\left(1 - \dfrac{s C_C}{g_{m6}}\right)}{\left(1 + \dfrac{s}{p_1^{\,'}}\right)\left(1 + \dfrac{s}{p_2^{\,'}}\right)}
$$

where

$$
p_1^{\,'} \approx -\frac{1}{g_{m6} R_{out2} R_{out1} C_C}, \qquad p_2^{\,'} \approx -\frac{g_{m6}}{C_2}, \qquad z_1 = +\frac{g_{m6}}{C_C}
$$

The positive zero $z_1$ in the right-half plane is the well-known drawback of Miller compensation.

---

## 5.2 Part B вҖ” Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks). [KTU University Exam вҖ” June 2024 Model]

**Design and analyze a two-stage CMOS op-amp with Miller compensation.**

**(a)** Draw the complete transistor-level schematic of a two-stage CMOS op-amp with a PMOS current-mirror load and a Miller compensation capacitor $C_C$. Label every transistor, node, and supply. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the small-signal open-loop gain, the dominant and non-dominant pole frequencies after Miller compensation, and the gain-bandwidth product. Clearly show the pole-splitting effect. Compute the slew rate. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution вҖ” Part (a):**

The canonical schematic uses 7 transistors in the signal path plus 2 (or more) bias transistors. The complete schematic is:

- **M1, M2 (NMOS)**: differential input pair with gates tied to $V_{in}^+$ and $V_{in}^-$.
- **M3 (PMOS, diode-connected) and M4 (PMOS)**: current-mirror active load. The gate of M4 is tied to the gate and drain of M3.
- **M5 (PMOS)**: tail current source for the differential pair; gate tied to a bias voltage.
- **M6 (PMOS)**: common-source second-stage driver; gate tied to the drain of M2 (= drain of M4, the output of stage 1).
- **M7 (NMOS)**: current-source load of stage 2; gate tied to a bias voltage.
- **$C_C$**: compensation capacitor from drain of M2/M4 to drain of M6 (= $V_{out}$).
- (Optional) $R_z$ in series with $C_C$ for zero-nulling.

The schematic was given in Section 4.2.

**Model Solution вҖ” Part (b):**

**Step 1 вҖ” Open-loop DC gain:**
The small-signal differential-to-single-ended gain of stage 1:
$$
A_{v1} = -g_{m1}(r_{o2} \,\vert\vert\, r_{o4})
$$
The CS gain of stage 2:
$$
A_{v2} = -g_{m6}(r_{o6} \,\vert\vert\, r_{o7})
$$
Overall:
$$
A_{OL}(0) = g_{m1}\,g_{m6}\,(r_{o2}\,\vert\vert\,r_{o4})(r_{o6}\,\vert\vert\,r_{o7})
$$

**Step 2 вҖ” Pole-splitting with Miller capacitor $C_C$:**
Apply KCL at node A (output of stage 1) and node B (op-amp output). With $C_C$ bridging A and B, the effective input capacitance at A is:

$$
C_{A,eff} = C_1 + C_C(1 + g_{m6}R_{out2}) \approx C_C\,g_{m6}\,R_{out2}
$$

The dominant pole (after compensation):
$$
p_1^{\,'} = -\frac{1}{R_{out1} \cdot C_{A,eff}} = -\frac{1}{g_{m6}\,R_{out2}\,R_{out1}\,C_C}
$$

The non-dominant pole:
$$
p_2^{\,'} \approx -\frac{g_{m6}}{C_2}
$$

The RHP zero:
$$
z_1 = +\frac{g_{m6}}{C_C}
$$

**Step 3 вҖ” GBW product:**
$$
\omega_{GBW} = A_{OL}(0) \cdot \vert p_1^{\,'} \vert = \frac{g_{m1}}{C_C}
$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Slew rate:**
The tail current $I_{D5}$ of M5 is the maximum current available to charge $C_C$:
$$
SR = \frac{I_{D5}}{C_C}
$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Pole-splitting observation:**
Before compensation: $\vert p_1 \vert \sim 1/(R_{out1} C_1)$, $\vert p_2 \vert \sim 1/(R_{out2} C_2)$.
After compensation: $\vert p_1^{\,'} \vert \ll \vert p_1 \vert$ (pushed lower by factor $g_{m6} R_{out2} C_C / C_1$), and $\vert p_2^{\,'} \vert \gg \vert p_2 \vert$ (pushed higher by factor $g_{m6} R_{out2} C_C / C_1$). The two poles move *away* from each other вҖ” this is **pole splitting**.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall вҖ” Part B**
> - Students frequently forget that the differential-to-single-ended conversion with a current-mirror load gives a **factor-of-2 advantage**, so $A_{v1} = -g_{m1} R_{out1}$, **not** $-g_{m1} R_{out1}/2$. Forgetting this loses 1 mark.
> - In the pole-splitting derivation, students often confuse $C_1$ (which is the *parasitic* capacitance at node A) with $C_C$ (the *compensation* capacitor). $C_1$ is **negligible** compared to $C_C\,g_{m6} R_{out2}$ after compensation.
> - The RHP zero is **not** an LHP zero вҖ” it carries a positive sign. Many students incorrectly write $z_1 = -g_{m6}/C_C$.
> - Slew rate must use $I_{D5}$ (the tail current), not $I_{D6}$ (the stage-2 bias current). Confusion here is a common error.

---

### Question B (14 Marks вҖ” Internal Choice Alternative). [KTU University Exam вҖ” Dec 2023 Model]

**Analyze the Miller compensation technique in detail and discuss the role of the nulling resistor.**

**(a)** Derive the transfer function of a Miller-compensated two-stage op-amp, including the dominant pole, the non-dominant pole, and the right-half-plane zero. Clearly show the pole-splitting effect. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain why the RHP zero degrades phase margin. Show, with derivation, how a nulling resistor $R_z$ in series with $C_C$ can cancel the zero or move it to the LHP. State the design condition for $R_z$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution вҖ” Part (a):**

Refer to Section 3.2 derivation above. The final transfer function is:

$$
A(s) = \frac{A_{OL}\,(1 - s C_C / g_{m6})}{(1 - s/p_1^{\,'})(1 - s/p_2^{\,'})}
$$

with

$$
p_1^{\,'} = -\frac{1}{g_{m6}\,R_{out2}\,R_{out1}\,C_C}, \qquad p_2^{\,'} \approx -\frac{g_{m6}}{C_2}, \qquad z_1 = +\frac{g_{m6}}{C_C}
$$

The **pole-splitting** effect: the compensation capacitor appears at node A as a capacitance magnified by the loop gain $(1 + g_{m6}R_{out2})$. This pushes $p_1$ to a much lower frequency. Simultaneously, $p_2$ is pushed to a higher frequency because $C_C$ partially absorbs $C_2$. The result is a single-pole system with the non-dominant pole safely beyond the unity-gain bandwidth.

**Model Solution вҖ” Part (b):**

**Why the RHP zero degrades phase margin:**
A zero in the right-half plane contributes **negative** phase shift:
$$
\angle (1 - s/z_1) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega/z_1)
$$

Wait вҖ” the correct sign is: at $s = j\omega$, the term $(1 - j\omega/z_1)$ has a real part of 1 and an imaginary part of $-\omega/z_1$. Its phase is $-\tan^{-1}(\omega/z_1)$ at low frequencies, but as $\omega$ crosses $z_1$, the phase jumps by $180В°$ (because we cross from upper-half to lower-half of the complex plane). The net effect is that the zero *adds* phase lag of up to $-90В°$ (when $\omega \ll z_1$) and then *subtracts* phase lag beyond $z_1$. If $z_1 < \omega_{UG}$, the additional phase lag at $\omega_{UG}$ is significant and degrades phase margin.

**With nulling resistor $R_z$:**

Place $R_z$ in series with $C_C$. The new transfer function numerator (using KCL with $R_z$):

$$
\text{Numerator} = g_{m6} - s C_C - g_{m6}\,s C_C\,R_z / \text{(input stage gain)} \approx g_{m6} - s C_C(1 - g_{m6}R_z)
$$

More carefully, replacing $C_C$ with the series combination of $R_z$ and $C_C$ gives the new zero:

$$
z_1^{\,'} = \frac{1}{C_C\left(\dfrac{1}{g_{m6}} - R_z\right)}
$$

**Design conditions:**

- **Zero pushed to infinity:** $R_z = 1/g_{m6}$. The zero disappears from the transfer function.
- **Zero placed at LHP:** $R_z > 1/g_{m6}$. The zero becomes a "regular" left-half-plane zero, which *adds* phase (good for stability).
- **Practical realization:** Often realized using the on-resistance of a triode-biased MOSFET or by using $R_z = 1/g_{m3} - 1/g_{m6}$, exploiting the fact that the PMOS mirror transistor M3 has the same transconductance as M6.

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall вҖ” Question B**
> - Students often write $z_1 = -g_{m6}/C_C$ (LHP) instead of $z_1 = +g_{m6}/C_C$ (RHP). This is a critical error; lose 2 marks.
> - The phrase "the zero disappears" for $R_z = 1/g_{m6}$ is correct, but students sometimes confuse this with "the zero becomes a pole" вҖ” it does NOT. It simply moves to $s = \infty$.
> - For Part (b), failing to mention that the RHP zero produces *negative* real-part zero (i.e., a positive phase contribution that subtracts from phase margin) loses marks. State clearly: "RHP zero degrades phase margin because it adds phase lag near the unity-gain frequency."
> - The derivation of $z_1^{\,'}$ using $R_z$ is the most-skipped step; KTU examiners award 3 marks specifically for this derivation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist вҖ” KTU Module 3 / CMOS Op-Amp & Miller Compensation**

### Two-Stage CMOS Op-Amp Topology
- вҳҗ **Stage 1**: Differential pair (M1, M2) + PMOS current-mirror load (M3, M4) вҶ’ differential-to-single-ended conversion.
- вҳҗ **Stage 2**: PMOS CS amplifier (M6) + NMOS current-source load (M7) вҶ’ additional gain and low-impedance drive.
- вҳҗ **Bias network** (M5, M8, M9) sets tail current $I_{D5}$.
- вҳҗ **Open-loop DC gain** $A_{OL}(0) = g_{m1} g_{m6} (r_{o2}\,\vert\vert\,r_{o4})(r_{o6}\,\vert\vert\,r_{o7})$.

### Frequency Compensation вҖ” Why?
- вҳҗ Multiple poles вҶ’ phase hits $-180В°$ before gain hits 0 dB вҶ’ closed-loop oscillation.
- вҳҗ Compensation reshapes the response to **single-pole roll-off** with $PM \geq 60В°$.
- вҳҗ **Barkhausen criterion**: at $\vert A\beta \vert = 1$, total phase shift must be less than $-360В°$ (or equivalently, $\angle A\beta > -180В°$).

### Miller Compensation вҖ” The Pole-Splitting Mechanism
- вҳҗ Capacitor $C_C$ from output of stage 1 to op-amp output node.
- вҳҗ Effective capacitance at input of $C_C$ is multiplied by $(1 + A_{v2})$ вҖ” the **Miller effect**.
- вҳҗ Dominant pole: $p_1^{\,'} \approx -1/(g_{m6} R_{out2} R_{out1} C_C)$ вҖ” moved **lower**.
- вҳҗ Non-dominant pole: $p_2^{\,'} \approx -g_{m6}/C_2$ вҖ” moved **higher**.
- вҳҗ **Pole splitting**: the two poles move *away* from each other.

### Gain-Bandwidth Product
- вҳҗ $\omega_{GBW} = g_{m1}/C_C$ вҖ” independent of load capacitor!
- вҳҗ Increasing $C_C$ lowers GBW вҶ’ fundamental trade-off with power.

### Slew Rate
- вҳҗ $SR = I_{D5}/C_C$ вҖ” limited by tail current.
- вҳҗ Increasing $C_C$ lowers slew rate вҶ’ trade-off with bandwidth.

### The RHP Zero
- вҳҗ $z_1 = +g_{m6}/C_C$ вҖ” destabilizes; erodes phase margin.
- вҳҗ **Cancel with nulling resistor**: $R_z \geq 1/g_{m6}$.
- вҳҗ Common implementation: $R_z = 1/g_{m3} - 1/g_{m6}$ using mirror transistor.

### Design Rules (60В° Phase Margin)
- вҳҗ Place non-dominant pole at $\vert p_2^{\,'} \vert \geq 2.2 \cdot \omega_{GBW}$.
- вҳҗ Equivalent: $C_C \geq 2.2 \cdot (g_{m1}/g_{m6}) \cdot C_L$.
- вҳҗ Push the zero to $\geq 10 \cdot \omega_{GBW}$, or null it with $R_z$.

### Key Formulas to Memorize (No Pipes, Use Vert)
- вҳҗ $A_{OL} = g_{m1}\,g_{m6}\,(r_{o2}\,\vert\vert\,r_{o4})(r_{o6}\,\vert\vert\,r_{o7})$
- вҳҗ $\omega_{GBW} = g_{m1}/C_C$
- вҳҗ $SR = I_{D5}/C_C$
- вҳҗ $p_1^{\,'} = -1/(g_{m6} R_{out2} R_{out1} C_C)$
- вҳҗ $p_2^{\,'} = -g_{m6}/C_2$
- вҳҗ $z_1 = +g_{m6}/C_C$
- вҳҗ $R_{z,\text{null}} = 1/g_{m6}$
- вҳҗ $PM = 90В° - \tan^{-1}(\omega_{GBW}/\vert p_2^{\,'}\vert)$

### Engineering Real-World Applications
- вҳҗ Switched-capacitor integrators in $\Sigma\Delta$ ADCs.
- вҳҗ Active-RC filters in audio codecs.
- вҳҗ Buffer amplifiers in sensor readout circuits.
- вҳҗ Comparators in high-speed ADCs.
- вҳҗ Voltage references and LDOs (with output-stage buffering).

> **End of Notes вҖ” Module 3 / Topic: CMOS Operational Amplifier вҖ” Two Stage, Frequency Compensation, Miller Compensation.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Band Gap References: Supply Independent Biasing, Temperature Independent References
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT755 - MIXED SIGNAL CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Band Gap References: Supply Independent Biasing, Temperature Independent References

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Supply Independent Biasing

**Formal Definition:**
A *Supply Independent Biasing* circuit (also called a **self-biased current source** or **VBE-referenced bias circuit**) is an analog circuit topology that generates a stable DC bias current or voltage at its output whose magnitude is determined entirely by on-chip device parameters (such as the thermal voltage $V_T$, base-emitter voltage $V_{BE}$, sheet resistance, or device dimensions) and is **fundamentally independent of the supply voltage $V_{DD}$**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Supply independence does **not** mean temperature independence. A supply-independent reference may still drift heavily with temperature. Combining supply independence with temperature independence is the entire purpose of a Bandgap Reference.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of supply-independent biasing like a **water tank with a float valve**:
- The supply voltage $V_{DD}$ is like the **incoming water pressure** вҖ” it can fluctuate wildly (people opening/closing other taps).
- The float valve automatically adjusts how much water enters so that the water level (the reference current) stays at a fixed, predetermined height.
- The output (bias current) therefore doesn't depend on pressure variations, only on the geometric/mechanical setup of the tank itself.

The two canonical supply-independent topologies are:
1. **$V_{BE}$-referenced self-biasing** (uses the bandgap voltage of silicon indirectly).
2. **Threshold-voltage ($V_{TH}$) referenced self-biasing** (uses the MOS threshold voltage).

## 1.2 Temperature Independent References (Bandgap References)

**Formal Definition:**
A **Bandgap Voltage Reference (BGR)** is a precision voltage reference circuit that produces a fixed, temperature-independent output voltage $V_{REF} \approx \mathbf{1.205\text{ V}}$ by summing two voltages with **opposite temperature coefficients (TCs)**:
- A **CTAT** (Complementary To Absolute Temperature) voltage, usually derived from a forward-biased BJT $V_{BE}$.
- A **PTAT** (Proportional To Absolute Temperature) voltage, derived from the *difference* of two $V_{BE}$s operating at unequal current densities ($\Delta V_{BE} = V_T \ln(N)$).

The sum is scaled so the positive temperature coefficient of the PTAT term exactly cancels the negative temperature coefficient of the CTAT term at the reference temperature $T_0 = \mathbf{300\text{ K}}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Origin of 1.205 V:**
> The numerical value $\approx 1.205\text{ V}$ is the linearly extrapolated silicon bandgap voltage $E_g/q$ at **0 K**. Any bandgap reference that is properly temperature-compensated produces a reference voltage equal to this extrapolated bandgap вҖ” hence the name.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a **seesaw (balance beam)** where:
- On one end sits a heavy ball that *shrinks* when it gets hotter (CTAT: $V_{BE}$).
- On the other end sits a light ball that *expands* when it gets hotter (PTAT: $\Delta V_{BE}$).
- If you weigh them correctly (choose $K$ in $V_{REF} = V_{BE} + K \cdot \Delta V_{BE}$), the seesaw stays perfectly level regardless of room temperature.

## 1.3 Why Are These References Essential?

| Application | Why a BGR is needed |
|---|---|
| **ADCs / DACs** | LSB accuracy depends on an absolute voltage reference. |
| **LDO Regulators** | Output voltage must not drift with $V_{DD}$ or temperature. |
| **DRAM Sense Amps** | Need a process/temperature-independent trip point. |
| **Sensor Read-out** | Sensor signals are tiny; bias drift would swamp them. |
| **PLL Charge Pumps** | Current sources must be PSRR- and TC-immune. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Need for Supply Independence

A simple resistor + MOSFET current source gives:

$$I_{OUT} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} \left( V_{GS} - V_{TH} \right)^2$$

This current **depends on** $V_{GS}$, which in turn depends on $V_{DD}$ through the bias network. A $\pm 10\%$ variation in $V_{DD}$ translates directly into a $\pm 10\%$ variation in $I_{OUT}$ вҖ” unacceptable for precision analog design.

> [!NOTE]
> **Supply Rejection Ratio (PSRR)** quantifies this immunity. A BGR typically achieves **PSRR > 80 dB** at DC.

## 2.2 Supply-Independent Biasing: Topology Analysis

### 2.2.1 The Bootstrap / Self-Bias Principle

A self-biased current source uses **negative feedback** through a startup loop:
- If $I_{OUT}$ tries to increase due to $V_{DD}$ rise, the feedback loop *increases* the voltage at the gate of the current-mirror device, which *reduces* the overdrive and clamps $I_{OUT}$.

The reference is usually set by forcing a known voltage across a precision resistor:
- **$V_{BE}$-based:** Drop $V_{BE}$ across $R_1$ вҶ’ $I_{OUT} = V_{BE}/R_1$.
- **$V_{TH}$-based:** Drop $V_{GS} - V_{TH} = V_{OV}$ across $R_1$ вҶ’ $I_{OUT} = V_{OV}/R_1$.
- **PTAT-based:** Drop $\Delta V_{BE}$ across $R_1$ вҶ’ $I_{OUT} = V_T \ln(N)/R_1$.

## 2.3 Temperature-Dependent Behavior of $V_{BE}$

The EbersвҖ“Moll model for a BJT gives:

$$V_{BE}(T) = V_{GO} \left( 1 - \frac{T}{T_0} \right) + V_{BE0} \frac{T}{T_0} - \eta V_T \ln\left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right)$$

where:
- $V_{GO} = \mathbf{1.205\text{ V}}$ = silicon extrapolated bandgap voltage at 0 K.
- $V_{BE0}$ = $V_{BE}$ measured at reference temperature $T_0$.
- $\eta$ = a process-dependent constant (typically 3 to 4 for IC BJTs, depends on the temperature exponent of saturation current).

**Temperature Coefficient of $V_{BE}$ at $T_0$:**

$$\frac{\partial V_{BE}}{\partial T}\bigg|_{T_0} \approx -2 \, \text{mV/В°C} \quad \text{(CTAT)}$$

This is the **CTAT** component.

## 2.4 Temperature-Dependent Behavior of $\Delta V_{BE}$ (PTAT)

For two BJTs with emitter-area ratio $N:1$ operating at the same collector current:

$$\Delta V_{BE} = V_{BE1} - V_{BE2} = V_T \ln(N) = \frac{kT}{q} \ln(N)$$

$$\frac{\partial \Delta V_{BE}}{\partial T}\bigg|_{T_0} = \frac{k}{q} \ln(N) = \frac{V_T}{T}\ln(N) \approx +0.087 \ln(N) \, \text{mV/В°C} \quad \text{(PTAT)}$$

This is the **PTAT** component.

## 2.5 The Bandgap Core Equation

The canonical bandgap output:

$$\boxed{V_{REF} = V_{BE}(T) + K \cdot \Delta V_{BE}(T)}$$

Zero temperature coefficient at $T = T_0$ requires:

$$\frac{\partial V_{REF}}{\partial T}\bigg|_{T_0} = 0 \implies K = \frac{\vert \partial V_{BE}/\partial T \vert_0}{\partial \Delta V_{BE}/\partial T \vert_0}$$

Solving numerically:

$$K \cdot \frac{k}{q} \ln(N) = \vert -2 \, \text{mV/В°C} \vert$$

$$K = \frac{2 \text{ mV/В°C}}{0.08617 \text{ mV/В°C} \times \ln(N)}$$

For typical $N = 10$: $K \approx 10.8$.

Then $V_{REF}(T_0) = V_{BE0} + K V_T \ln(N) \approx 0.65 + 10.8 \times 0.02585 \times 2.302 \approx \mathbf{1.21 \text{ V}}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The result is **not exactly** $V_{GO}$ because of the nonlinear $\eta V_T \ln(T/T_0)$ term. Curvature correction techniques (e.g., non-linear $K$, piecewise compensation) are required for high-precision (< 5 ppm/В°C) designs.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Thermal voltage | $V_T = kT/q$ | V; $V_T(300\text{K}) = 25.85$ mV |
| 2 | PTAT voltage | $\Delta V_{BE} = V_T \ln(N)$ | V |
| 3 | PTAT TC | $\partial \Delta V_{BE}/\partial T = (k/q)\ln(N)$ | V/K |
| 4 | CTAT voltage | $V_{BE}(T)$ from EbersвҖ“Moll | V |
| 5 | CTAT TC @ 300 K | $\partial V_{BE}/\partial T \approx -2$ mV/В°C | V/K |
| 6 | Bandgap output | $V_{REF} = V_{BE} + K\Delta V_{BE}$ | V |
| 7 | Optimal gain | $K = \frac{\vert \partial V_{BE}/\partial T \vert}{\partial \Delta V_{BE}/\partial T}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Silicon $E_g/q$ @ 0 K | $V_{GO} = 1.205$ | V |
| 9 | Typical $V_{REF}$ | $\approx 1.21$ | V |
| 10 | PSRR of good BGR | $> 80$ | dB (DC) |
| 11 | TC of good BGR | $< 50$ | ppm/В°C |

> [!NOTE]
> When writing the markdown table above, the vertical bar symbol `\vert` is used instead of `\|` so as not to break markdown table parsing вҖ” this aligns with the KTU Formula Sheet requirement.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

Bandgap references are ubiquitous in **every mixed-signal IC**: PMICs, automotive ECUs (AEC-Q100 Grade-0 requires < Вұ5 mV reference drift from -40 В°C to +150 В°C), biomedical implants (pacemakers, cochlear implants), RF transceivers, and even precision digital thermometers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Self-Biased $V_{BE}$-Referenced Current Source

Consider the classic **Widlar-style self-biased** topology where $Q_1$ has 1Г— emitter area, $Q_2$ has $N \times$ emitter area, and resistor $R_2$ carries the PTAT current.

**Step 1 вҖ” KVL around the baseвҖ“emitter loop:**

The op-amp (or transistor feedback loop) forces $V_A = V_B$, so the voltage across $R_1$ equals $\Delta V_{BE} = V_{BE1} - V_{BE2}$ (for the case where $Q_1$ carries $I_{PTAT}$ and $Q_2$ carries $N \cdot I_{PTAT}$).

**Step 2 вҖ” Current through $R_1$:**

$$I_{R1} = \frac{\Delta V_{BE}}{R_1} = \frac{V_T \ln(N)}{R_1}$$

**Step 3 вҖ” Current through $R_2$ (carrying $I_{PTAT}$):**

The op-amp also forces a defined relationship so that the current in $R_2$ is $M$ times $I_{R1}$. Then:

$$I_{PTAT} = \frac{V_T \ln(N)}{R_2/M} = \frac{M \, V_T \ln(N)}{R_2}$$

**Step 4 вҖ” Output voltage across $R_{OUT}$:**

If $V_{REF}$ is taken across $R_{OUT}$ carrying $I_{PTAT}$:

$$V_{REF} = I_{PTAT} \cdot R_{OUT} = \frac{M \, V_T \ln(N) \cdot R_{OUT}}{R_2}$$

**Step 5 вҖ” Add the $V_{BE}$ term:**

To get $V_{REF} = V_{BE} + K \Delta V_{BE}$, an additional $V_{BE}$ drop (typically from $Q_2$'s base-emitter) is added in series with the IR-drop across $R_{OUT}$:

$$V_{REF} = V_{BE2} + \frac{M \, V_T \ln(N) \cdot R_{OUT}}{R_2}$$

**Step 6 вҖ” Identify $K$:**

$$K = \frac{M \cdot R_{OUT}}{R_2}$$

**Step 7 вҖ” Apply zero-TC condition:**

$$\frac{\partial V_{REF}}{\partial T} = \frac{\partial V_{BE}}{\partial T} + K \cdot \frac{k}{q} \ln(N) = 0$$

$$K \cdot \frac{k}{q} \ln(N) = -\frac{\partial V_{BE}}{\partial T} = +2 \text{ mV/В°C}$$

$$\boxed{K = \frac{q \cdot 2 \text{ mV/В°C}}{k \ln(N)} = \frac{0.002}{0.00008617 \cdot \ln(N)}}$$

For $N = 8$: $K = \frac{0.002}{0.00008617 \times 2.0794} = \frac{0.002}{0.0001792} \approx \mathbf{11.16}$

**Step 8 вҖ” Compute $V_{REF}$ at $T_0 = 300$ K:**

Assume $V_{BE0} = 0.65$ V, $V_T = 0.02585$ V:

$$V_{REF} = 0.65 + 11.16 \times 0.02585 \times \ln(8)$$

$$V_{REF} = 0.65 + 11.16 \times 0.02585 \times 2.0794$$

$$V_{REF} = 0.65 + 0.6002 \approx \mathbf{1.250 \text{ V}}$$

**Step 9 вҖ” Curvature analysis (first-order):**

Higher-order terms give a residual curvature:

$$\frac{\partial^2 V_{REF}}{\partial T^2} \approx \frac{\eta (\eta - 1) V_T}{T^2} - \frac{K \ln(N) \cdot k}{qT}$$

This residual causes the **banana-shaped** $V_{REF}(T)$ curve with a non-zero second derivative, which is why even well-designed BGRs have a finite TC (typically 20вҖ“50 ppm/В°C).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Supply-Independent Topologies

| Topology | Reference Quantity | $V_{DD}$ Sensitivity | Temperature Behavior | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Resistor + $V_{GS}$ bias | $V_{OV}/R$ | High (gate-coupled to $V_{DD}$) | Poor (resistor TC + $V_{TH}$ TC) | Biasing only |
| $V_{BE}$-referenced | $V_{BE}/R$ | Low | Strongly CTAT | Threshold-detect circuits |
| $V_{TH}$-referenced | $V_{OV}/R$ | Medium | Strongly PTAT | Sub-threshold biasing |
| $\Delta V_{BE}$ / PTAT | $V_T \ln(N)/R$ | Very low | PTAT | BGR core |
| **Full Bandgap** | $V_{BE} + K\Delta V_{BE}$ | **Negligible** | **Near-zero TC** | **Voltage reference** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[VDD Power Supply] --> B[Supply Independent Bias Block]
    B --> C[PTAT Current Generator - Delta VBE over R1]
    B --> D[CTAT Voltage Source - BJT VBE]
    C --> E[Scaling Amplifier K]
    D --> F[Output Node Summation]
    E --> F
    F --> G[VREF Output ~1.21 V]
    H[Opamp Feedback Loop] --> C
    H --> D
    I[Startup Circuit] --> B
    J[Temperature T] --> D
    J --> C
```

## 4.2 Canonical CMOS Bandgap Reference Topology

```mermaid
graph TD
    node1[VDD] --> node2[PMOS Current Mirror M1]
    node2 --> node3[Diode-Connected BJT Q1 Area 1x]
    node2 --> node4[Diode-Connected BJT Q2 Area Nx]
    node5[R1] --> node6[Node A - Opamp Positive Input]
    node3 --> node6
    node4 --> node7[Node B - Opamp Negative Input]
    node5 --> node7
    node8[Opamp] --> node2
    node9[R2] --> node10[VREF Output]
    node7 --> node9
    node3 --> node10
```

**Operating principle:**
1. Op-amp forces $V_A = V_B$.
2. Voltage across $R_1$ is therefore $\Delta V_{BE} = V_T \ln(N)$, generating a **PTAT current** $I_{PTAT} = \Delta V_{BE}/R_1$.
3. PMOS mirror replicates this PTAT current into the $R_2$ branch.
4. $V_{REF} = V_{BE,Q1} + I_{PTAT} \cdot R_2 = V_{BE} + \frac{R_2}{R_1} V_T \ln(N)$.
5. Choose $R_2/R_1$ so that the PTAT term cancels the CTAT $V_{BE}$ drift.

## 4.3 Self-Biased $V_{BE}$-Referenced Current Source (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA[VDD] --> stepB[M1 PMOS Mirror]
    stepB --> stepC[Q1 BJT - Diode Connected]
    stepC --> stepD[R1 - Precision Resistor]
    stepD --> stepG[GND]
    stepB --> stepE[Q2 BJT - Diode Connected]
    stepE --> stepF[R2 - Sets IOUT]
    stepF --> stepG
    stepF --> stepH[IOUT to Load]
```

## 4.4 Startup Circuit Concept

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[VDD] --> startB[Startup Network Detects Zero Current]
    startB --> startC[Inject Current into Bias Loop]
    startC --> startD[Normal Operation Reached]
    startD --> startE[Startup Circuit Turns Off]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a startup circuit?** Self-biased loops have a **degenerate operating point at $I = 0$**. Without a startup circuit, the BGR could remain at zero current indefinitely when $V_{DD}$ is first applied. The startup circuit forces a small current to "kick" the loop into the desired operating point, then turns itself off.

## 4.5 Functional Summary Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    blockA[Supply Voltage VDD] --> blockB[PSRR of Reference]
    blockB --> blockC[Stable Internal Bias]
    blockC --> blockD[PTAT + CTAT Combination]
    blockD --> blockE[Temperature Compensated VREF]
    blockE --> blockF[Output ~1.21 V Independent of VDD and T]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define a bandgap voltage reference. Why is its output nominally equal to 1.205 V?**

**Model Answer:**
A bandgap voltage reference is a temperature-compensated voltage reference that combines a CTAT voltage (a BJT $V_{BE}$) with a scaled PTAT voltage (a $\Delta V_{BE}$) to produce an output with zero temperature coefficient. The nominal output equals **1.205 V** because this is the linearly extrapolated silicon bandgap voltage $E_g/q$ at 0 K; when the CTAT and PTAT components are perfectly balanced, the resulting reference equals this extrapolated bandgap. **(3 marks)**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What is the difference between PTAT and CTAT voltages? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **PTAT (Proportional To Absolute Temperature):** A voltage that *increases linearly* with absolute temperature. Example: $\Delta V_{BE} = V_T \ln(N) = (kT/q)\ln(N)$ вҖ” has TC of $+(k/q)\ln(N)$ вүҲ +0.087 ln(N) mV/В°C.
- **CTAT (Complementary To Absolute Temperature):** A voltage that *decreases linearly* with absolute temperature. Example: A BJT base-emitter voltage $V_{BE}$ вҖ” has TC of approximately вҲ’2 mV/В°C at 300 K. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) вҖ” Module 3 Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks) вҖ” [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **CMOS bandgap voltage reference** using an op-amp. Derive the expression for the reference voltage $V_{REF}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a bandgap reference using BJTs with emitter-area ratio $N = 10$ and $V_{BE0} = 0.65$ V at $T_0 = 300$ K, calculate:
   (i) The required gain $K$ for zero temperature coefficient.
   (ii) The reference voltage $V_{REF}$.
   (iii) The PTAT and CTAT contributions at $T_0$.
   **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) вҖ” 7 Marks**

Refer to the canonical CMOS bandgap circuit (Section 4.2). The op-amp negative feedback forces $V_A = V_B$, so the voltage across $R_1$ equals $\Delta V_{BE}$:

$$V_{R1} = V_{BE,Q1} - V_{BE,Q2} = V_T \ln\left(\frac{A_{E2}}{A_{E1}}\right) = V_T \ln(N)$$

The PTAT current through $R_1$:

$$I_{PTAT} = \frac{V_T \ln(N)}{R_1}$$

The PMOS current mirror replicates $I_{PTAT}$ into the $R_2$ branch. The output:

$$\boxed{V_{REF} = V_{BE,Q1} + \frac{R_2}{R_1} V_T \ln(N)}$$

The first term $V_{BE,Q1}$ is CTAT (TC вүҲ вҲ’2 mV/В°C). The second term is PTAT (TC = $(k/q)(R_2/R_1)\ln(N)$). Choosing $R_2/R_1$ correctly cancels the temperature drift. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b) вҖ” 7 Marks**

Given: $N = 10$, $V_{BE0} = 0.65$ V, $T_0 = 300$ K, $V_T(300) = 0.02585$ V.

**(i) Required gain K:**

TC of $V_{BE}$ = вҲ’2 mV/В°C = вҲ’0.002 V/K.
TC of $\Delta V_{BE} = (k/q)\ln(N) = (1.381\times 10^{-23}/1.602\times 10^{-19}) \times \ln(10)$
$= 8.617 \times 10^{-5} \times 2.3026 = 1.984 \times 10^{-4}$ V/K = $0.1984$ mV/В°C.

$$K = \frac{|TC_{V_{BE}}|}{TC_{\Delta V_{BE}}} = \frac{2.0 \text{ mV/В°C}}{0.1984 \text{ mV/В°C}} = \mathbf{10.08}$$

**(ii) Reference voltage:**

$$V_{REF} = 0.65 + 10.08 \times 0.02585 \times \ln(10)$$
$$= 0.65 + 10.08 \times 0.02585 \times 2.3026$$
$$= 0.65 + 0.6002 = \mathbf{1.2502 \text{ V}}$$

**(iii) PTAT and CTAT contributions:**

- **CTAT contribution:** $V_{BE0} = \mathbf{0.65 \text{ V}}$
- **PTAT contribution:** $K \cdot \Delta V_{BE} = 10.08 \times 0.05954 = \mathbf{0.6002 \text{ V}}$

Total = 1.2502 V (matches part ii). **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) вҖ” [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain **supply-independent biasing**. With a circuit diagram, describe a **$V_{BE}$-referenced self-biased current source** and derive its output current expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bandgap reference is designed using two BJTs with emitter-area ratio 8:1. The op-amp forces equal currents in the two branches. Resistors $R_1 = 1$ kО© and $R_2 = 12$ kО©. At 300 K, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V. Compute:
   (i) $\Delta V_{BE}$ at 300 K.
   (ii) PTAT current $I_{PTAT}$.
   (iii) Reference voltage $V_{REF}$.
   (iv) Verify whether the design achieves zero TC at 300 K.
   **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution**

**Part (a) вҖ” 7 Marks**

**Supply-independent biasing** means the bias current/voltage is independent of $V_{DD}$, depending only on device parameters and on-chip components.

For a $V_{BE}$-referenced self-biased source (using a BJT $Q_1$ diode-connected in series with $R_1$, mirrored via PMOS to drive the output):

The op-amp forces $V_A = V_B$, so the loop forces:

$$I_{BIAS} \cdot R_1 = V_{BE1} \implies I_{BIAS} = \frac{V_{BE1}}{R_1}$$

This current is independent of $V_{DD}$ (only depends on the BJT's $V_{BE}$ and on the resistor $R_1$). The mirrored output current $I_{OUT} = M \cdot I_{BIAS}$ where $M$ is the mirror ratio. The reference voltage taken across an output resistor:

$$V_{OUT} = I_{OUT} \cdot R_{OUT} = \frac{M \cdot V_{BE1} \cdot R_{OUT}}{R_1}$$

This voltage depends only on $V_{BE}$ (a device parameter) and resistor ratios (matched on-chip) вҖ” hence supply-independent. **(7 marks)**

**Part (b) вҖ” 7 Marks**

Given: $N = 8$, $R_1 = 1$ kО©, $R_2 = 12$ kО©, $V_{BE} = 0.7$ V, $T = 300$ K.

**(i) $\Delta V_{BE}$ at 300 K:**

$$V_T(300) = \frac{kT}{q} = \frac{1.381 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.02585 \text{ V}$$

$$\Delta V_{BE} = V_T \ln(N) = 0.02585 \times \ln(8) = 0.02585 \times 2.0794 = \mathbf{0.05375 \text{ V}}$$

**(ii) PTAT current:**

$$I_{PTAT} = \frac{\Delta V_{BE}}{R_1} = \frac{0.05375}{1000} = \mathbf{53.75 \text{ ОјA}}$$

**(iii) Reference voltage:**

$$V_{REF} = V_{BE} + \frac{R_2}{R_1} \Delta V_{BE} = 0.7 + 12 \times 0.05375$$
$$= 0.7 + 0.645 = \mathbf{1.345 \text{ V}}$$

**(iv) Verify zero TC:**

Effective gain $K = R_2/R_1 = 12$.
PTAT TC: $12 \times 0.1984$ mV/В°C = $+2.381$ mV/В°C.
CTAT TC: $-2.0$ mV/В°C.

Net TC: $-2.0 + 2.381 = +0.381$ mV/В°C вү  0.

**Conclusion:** The design does **NOT** achieve zero TC; it has a residual positive TC of **+0.381 mV/В°C**. To fix this, reduce $R_2/R_1$ slightly:

Required $K = 2.0/0.1984 = 10.08$, so $R_2/R_1 = 10.08$ вҶ’ $R_2 \approx 10.08$ kО©. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Bandgap Reference Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert absolute temperature correctly** вҖ” KTU expects Kelvin (300 K), not Celsius (27 В°C). $V_T$ at 300 K is 25.85 mV, **not** 25.85 mV + offset.
> 2. **Sign error in TC cancellation** вҖ” CTAT TC is *negative*; when equating magnitudes, students often write $K \cdot (k/q)\ln N = +2$ mV/В°C but forget that the CTAT TC must be subtracted. Correct equation: $\partial V_{BE}/\partial T + K \cdot \partial \Delta V_{BE}/\partial T = 0$, hence $K \cdot (k/q)\ln N = -\partial V_{BE}/\partial T = +2$ mV/В°C.
> 3. **Confusing $\ln(N)$ with $\log_{10}(N)$** вҖ” always use natural logarithm (ln) for $\Delta V_{BE}$.
> 4. **Ignoring the startup circuit** вҖ” self-biased loops need a startup circuit; failing to mention it in a design question loses 1вҖ“2 marks.
> 5. **Quoting "1.205 V" without explanation** вҖ” must justify using the silicon bandgap concept.
> 6. **Missing PSRR argument** вҖ” examiners look for supply independence argument; not just temperature compensation.
> 7. **No mention of curvature correction** вҖ” for higher-marks questions, curvature (the $\eta V_T \ln(T/T_0)$ term) is expected to be discussed.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Supply Independent Biasing** = bias current/voltage independent of $V_{DD}$; achieved by negative feedback loops and using on-chip device parameters ($V_{BE}$, $V_{TH}$, $\Delta V_{BE}$) rather than raw $V_{DD}$.
- **Bandgap Reference** combines a **CTAT** voltage ($V_{BE}$, TC вүҲ вҲ’2 mV/В°C) with a **PTAT** voltage ($\Delta V_{BE} = V_T \ln N$, TC вүҲ +0.087 ln N mV/В°C).
- **Output equation:** $V_{REF} = V_{BE} + K \cdot V_T \ln(N)$, with $K$ chosen so that $\partial V_{REF}/\partial T = 0$ at $T_0$.
- **Nominal $V_{REF} \approx \mathbf{1.205\text{ V}}$** = silicon bandgap $E_g/q$ extrapolated to 0 K.
- **Thermal voltage $V_T = kT/q = 25.85$ mV at 300 K.**
- **Always start the BGR with a startup circuit** to escape the $I = 0$ degenerate point.
- **Curvature** in $V_{REF}(T)$ arises from the nonlinear $\eta V_T \ln(T/T_0)$ term вҖ” residual TC ~ 20вҖ“50 ppm/В°C in basic designs, < 5 ppm/В°C in curvature-corrected designs.
- **PTAT** = Proportional To Absolute Temperature; **CTAT** = Complementary To Absolute Temperature.
- **PSRR > 80 dB** is the design target for any practical BGR.
- **Common topologies to know for KTU:** op-amp-based BGR, Brokaw bandgap, current-mode BGR, sub-bandgap references (using resistor ratios to scale $V_{BE}$).
- **Practical implementations:** CMOS bandgaps use parasitic vertical PNPs (in standard CMOS processes) since NPNs are not always available.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DAC Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT755 - MIXED SIGNAL CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** DAC Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** is a mixed-signal subsystem that converts a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital code (typically an N-bit binary word) into a corresponding continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog voltage or current. The **DAC Specifications** constitute the formal set of static (DC) and dynamic (AC) performance metrics that quantitatively characterize the fidelity, speed, linearity, and power behavior of the converter, and form the primary basis for architectural selection, device sizing, and system-level validation in mixed-signal integrated circuit design.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, DAC specifications are partitioned into two principal classes:

| Class | Specifications Included | Operating Domain |
|---|---|---|
| **Static (DC) Specifications** | Resolution, LSB, Full-Scale Range, Offset Error, Gain Error, INL, DNL, Monotonicity | Slow or steady-state input codes |
| **Dynamic (AC) Specifications** | Settling Time, Slew Rate, Glitch Impulse, SFDR, SNR, THD, ENOB, Update Rate | Time-varying / sinusoidal input codes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Anchor (KTU 2024 вҖ” PEECT755 Module 4):**
> Specifications of DACs вҖ” *Resolution, Linearity, Speed, Glitch, SFDR, SNR, Dynamic Range, Monotonicity* вҖ” together with architectural trade-offs in current-steering, R-2R ladder, and charge-redistribution topologies.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy вҖ” "The Digital Staircase to a Smooth Hill"

Imagine you are a sculptor trying to recreate a smooth marble hill using nothing but a stack of cubic wooden blocks. Each block has the *same height* (this is your **LSB**), and the total number of blocks you have is your **resolution** (2вҒҝ blocks for an N-bit DAC). When you stack them, you get a **staircase approximation** of the smooth hill:

- **Resolution (number of steps)**: More steps вҶ’ smoother approximation вҶ’ finer detail.
- **LSB (step height)**: Smaller steps вҶ’ less quantization error.
- **INL/DNL (step uniformity)**: A perfect staircase has all steps equal in height. If one step is too tall, you have a **DNL error**; if the cumulative deviation drifts, you have an **INL error**.
- **Settling Time (speed of placement)**: How fast you can finish placing the blocks before the next wave comes in.
- **Glitch (accidental slip)**: The moment you swap from one block height to another, your hand may overshoot вҖ” this is a **glitch impulse**.
- **SNR (signal vs. background chatter)**: The louder the crowd (noise) talking while you sculpt, the harder it is to see the hill (signal) clearly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A specification is **never** evaluated in isolation. KTU board questions almost always require *cross-relation* вҖ” e.g., "How does reducing LSB (increasing resolution) impact SNR and INL simultaneously?" Always think in terms of *trade-off matrices*.

## 1.3 The Master Signal Chain вҖ” Where Specifications Originate

Every DAC non-ideality is introduced at one of the following functional blocks in the signal chain:

$$
\text{Digital Input} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Decoder} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Switching Network} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Analog Reconstruction Element} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Output Buffer} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Analog Output}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **First-Read Tip:** Memorize the acronyms **SNDR, SFDR, INL, DNL, ENOB, LSB** вҖ” these six terms account for over 80% of KTU exam questions on DAC specifications.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Static (DC) Specifications вҖ” Detailed Walkthrough

### 2.1.1 Resolution (N) and Least Significant Bit (LSB)

The **resolution** of a DAC is the number of distinct analog output levels it can produce, equal to **2вҒҝ** for an N-bit converter. The **LSB** is the analog increment corresponding to a 1-code change, and is the *fundamental quantization step*.

**Key logic chain:**

- Digital codes range from **0 to 2вҒҝ вҲ’ 1**.
- Reference voltage is **V_REF** (or V_FS for full-scale).
- Each code step = V_REF / 2вҒҝ.
- Therefore, **LSB = V_REF / 2вҒҝ**.

### 2.1.2 Full-Scale Range (FSR)

The **Full-Scale Range** is the *output span*, not the maximum output value. For a unipolar DAC:

$$
\text{FSR} = V_{REF} - 0 = V_{REF}
$$

The **Full-Scale Output (V_FS)** is the output for code 111вҖҰ1:

$$
V_{FS} = V_{REF} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{2^n}\right) = V_{REF} - 1\,\text{LSB}
$$

### 2.1.3 Offset Error

A **DC shift** of the entire transfer characteristic, expressed in LSB or ВөV. Caused by mismatch in the output buffer or current-mirror tail transistor.

### 2.1.4 Gain Error

The *slope* of the actual transfer characteristic deviates from the ideal unity-gain line, expressed as a percentage of FSR or in LSB units.

### 2.1.5 Differential Non-Linearity (DNL)

The deviation of each *actual step* from the *ideal step* of 1 LSB:

$$
\text{DNL}(k) = \frac{V_{OUT}(k) - V_{OUT}(k-1) - V_{LSB}}{V_{LSB}} \quad [\text{in LSB units}]
$$

> [!WARNING]
> If **DNL(k) вүӨ вҲ’1 LSB** at any code k, the DAC is **non-monotonic** вҖ” the output *decreases* for an increasing input, which is catastrophic in control loops and audio applications.

### 2.1.6 Integral Non-Linearity (INL)

The deviation of the *cumulative transfer characteristic* from the ideal straight line. The endpoint definition (most common in KTU problems):

$$
\text{INL}(k) = \frac{V_{OUT}(k) - V_{OUT,\text{ideal}}(k)}{V_{LSB}} \quad [\text{in LSB units}]
$$

$$
\text{INL}(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{DNL}(i)
$$

### 2.1.7 Monotonicity

A DAC is **monotonic** if its output never decreases as the input digital code monotonically increases from 0 to 2вҒҝ вҲ’ 1. A sufficient (but not necessary) condition:

$$
\text{DNL}(k) \geq -1 \text{ LSB} \quad \forall\, k
$$

## 2.2 Dynamic (AC) Specifications

### 2.2.1 Settling Time (t_s)

The time required for the output to settle within a specified error band (typically **Вұ0.5 LSB** or **Вұ1 LSB**) of its final value, after a full-scale code transition. It comprises:

- **Slew phase** (limited by slew rate SR).
- **Linear settling phase** (exponential decay governed by the output pole).

$$
t_s = t_{\text{slew}} + t_{\text{linear}} = \frac{\Delta V}{SR} + \tau \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{\text{error band}}\right)
$$

### 2.2.2 Slew Rate (SR)

The maximum rate of change of the analog output:

$$
SR = \left.\frac{dV_{OUT}}{dt}\right|_{\max} \quad [\text{in V/Вөs}]
$$

### 2.2.3 Glitch Impulse

A transient output spike occurring during a major code transition (e.g., 0111вҖҰ1 вҶ’ 1000вҖҰ1) due to *asymmetric switching* of the MSB. Quantified as **glitch energy** in VВ·s:

$$
E_{\text{glitch}} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \left\vert V_{OUT}(t) - V_{OUT,\text{ideal}}(t) \right\vert \, dt
$$

### 2.2.4 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) вҖ” Ideal Quantization Limit

For an ideal N-bit DAC driven by a full-scale sinusoid, the theoretical SNR is:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{SNR}_{\text{ideal}} = 6.02\,N + 1.76 \quad [\text{dB}]\;}
$$

This is a **direct consequence of uniform quantization noise theory** (assuming quantization error is uniformly distributed between ВұВҪ LSB and uncorrelated with the signal).

### 2.2.5 Effective Number of Bits (ENOB)

The *actual* resolution implied by the measured SNR (which is degraded by non-idealities):

$$
\boxed{\;\text{ENOB} = \frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{measured}} - 1.76}{6.02} \quad [\text{bits}]\;}
$$

### 2.2.6 Total Harmonic Distortion (THD)

Ratio of RMS harmonic power to RMS fundamental power:

$$
\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{V_2^2 + V_3^2 + V_4^2 + \cdots}}{V_1} \times 100\%
$$

### 2.2.7 Signal-to-Noise-and-Distortion Ratio (SNDR / SINAD)

$$
\text{SNDR} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}} + P_{\text{distortion}}}\right) \quad [\text{dB}]
$$

### 2.2.8 Spurious-Free Dynamic Range (SFDR)

The ratio of the RMS amplitude of the fundamental to the RMS amplitude of the *largest spurious component* (harmonic or otherwise) in the output spectrum:

$$
\text{SFDR} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{\text{fundamental}}}{V_{\text{largest spur}}}\right) \quad [\text{dBc}]
$$

### 2.2.9 Dynamic Range (DR)

The ratio between the largest and smallest representable signals:

$$
\text{DR} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{\text{FS}}}{V_{\text{noise,RMS}}}\right) \quad [\text{dB}]
$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** you are likely to need for KTU 2024 ESE questions on this topic. Memorize the boxed entries first.

| # | Specification | Formula | Units | Critical Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LSB weight | $\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^{N}}$ | V | Smallest analog step |
| 2 | Full-Scale Output | $V_{FS} = V_{REF}\!\left(1 - 2^{-N}\right)$ | V | Code = 2вҒҝ вҲ’ 1 |
| 3 | Resolution (levels) | $L = 2^{N}$ | levels | Discrete steps |
| 4 | DNL | $\text{DNL}(k) = \dfrac{V_{OUT}(k) - V_{OUT}(k-1) - V_{LSB}}{V_{LSB}}$ | LSB | DNL вүҘ вҲ’1 for monotonicity |
| 5 | INL (endpoint) | $\text{INL}(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{DNL}(i)$ | LSB | Linearity drift measure |
| 6 | Ideal SNR | $\text{SNR} = 6.02\,N + 1.76$ | dB | Quantization-noise limit |
| 7 | ENOB | $\text{ENOB} = \dfrac{\text{SNR} - 1.76}{6.02}$ | bits | De facto resolution |
| 8 | Settling time | $t_s = \dfrac{\Delta V}{SR} + \tau \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon}\right)$ | s | Within Вұ0.5 LSB |
| 9 | SFDR | $\text{SFDR} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{\text{spur,max}}}\right)$ | dBc | Spectral purity |
| 10 | THD | $\text{THD} = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{h \geq 2} V_{h}^{2}}}{V_{1}} \times 100\%$ | % | Harmonic content |
| 11 | Dynamic Range | $\text{DR} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{FS}}{V_{n,\text{RMS}}}\right)$ | dB | Smallest signal fidelity |
| 12 | Glitch Energy | $E_{g} = \int \vert V_{err}(t) \vert \, dt$ | VВ·s | Major-carry transition |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio DACs (headphones, mixers):** SFDR, THD, and jitter dominate perceived quality; INL/DNL are secondary.
- **Video / Display drivers:** Settling time and glitch energy are critical вҖ” a glitch on the MSB transition can be visible as a stripe.
- **Precision instrumentation (PLL, DDS, ATE):** INL, DNL, and monotonicity are non-negotiable.
- **Communications (5G, radar, software-defined radio):** SFDR and ENOB drive the bit-error rate.
- **Industrial control (motor drives, actuators):** Monotonicity is a *safety* requirement; non-monotonicity can cause physical oscillation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Every algebraic step below is shown explicitly. **Do not skip rows when transcribing to your answer sheet** вҖ” KTU examiners award *method marks* for intermediate transitions.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 вҖ” Ideal SNR of an N-bit DAC (Quantization Noise Model)

### Step 1 вҖ” Model the quantization error

Let the input be a continuous signal $x(t)$ and the quantized output be $\hat{x}(t)$. The quantization error is defined as:

$$
e_q(t) \;=\; \hat{x}(t) - x(t)
$$

For an ideal uniform quantizer with step size $\Delta = V_{LSB}$, the error $e_q$ is bounded:

$$
-\frac{\Delta}{2} \;\leq\; e_q(t) \;\leq\; +\frac{\Delta}{2}
$$

### Step 2 вҖ” Assume uniform distribution

A standard KTU-approved assumption is that $e_q$ is **uniformly distributed** over $[-\Delta/2,\, +\Delta/2]$ and statistically independent of the input. The probability density function is:

$$
p(e_q) \;=\; \frac{1}{\Delta} \quad \text{for} \quad e_q \in \left[-\frac{\Delta}{2},\, \frac{\Delta}{2}\right]
$$

### Step 3 вҖ” Compute the mean-square quantization noise (variance)

$$
\overline{e_{q}^{2}} \;=\; \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_{q}^{2} \cdot p(e_q) \, de_q
$$

Substituting $p(e_q) = 1/\Delta$:

$$
\overline{e_{q}^{2}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\Delta}\int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_{q}^{2}\, de_q
$$

### Step 4 вҖ” Evaluate the integral

$$
\int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_{q}^{2}\, de_q \;=\; \left[\frac{e_{q}^{3}}{3}\right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} \;=\; \frac{1}{3}\!\left[\left(\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^{3} - \left(-\frac{\Delta}{2}\right)^{3}\right]
$$

$$
=\; \frac{1}{3} \cdot \frac{2\Delta^{3}}{8} \;=\; \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\overline{e_{q}^{2}} \;=\; \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12}
$$

### Step 5 вҖ” Compute the signal power

For a **full-scale sinusoid** of amplitude $A = V_{REF}/2$ (peak-to-peak = $V_{REF}$), the RMS signal voltage is:

$$
V_{\text{sig,RMS}} \;=\; \frac{A}{\sqrt{2}} \;=\; \frac{V_{REF}}{2\sqrt{2}}
$$

The signal power (assuming a 1 О© reference load for simplicity) is:

$$
P_{\text{sig}} \;=\; V_{\text{sig,RMS}}^{2} \;=\; \frac{V_{REF}^{2}}{8}
$$

### Step 6 вҖ” Express LSB in terms of V_REF

$$
\Delta \;=\; V_{LSB} \;=\; \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{N}}
$$

### Step 7 вҖ” Form the SNR ratio

$$
\text{SNR} \;=\; \frac{P_{\text{sig}}}{\overline{e_{q}^{2}}} \;=\; \frac{V_{REF}^{2}/8}{\Delta^{2}/12} \;=\; \frac{V_{REF}^{2}}{8} \cdot \frac{12}{\Delta^{2}}
$$

$$
=\; \frac{12}{8} \cdot \frac{V_{REF}^{2}}{\Delta^{2}} \;=\; \frac{3}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{V_{REF}}{\Delta}\right)^{2}
$$

### Step 8 вҖ” Substitute О” = V_REF / 2вҒҝ

$$
\text{SNR} \;=\; \frac{3}{2} \cdot \left(2^{N}\right)^{2} \;=\; \frac{3}{2} \cdot 2^{2N} \;=\; 1.5 \cdot 4^{N}
$$

### Step 9 вҖ” Convert to decibels

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} \;=\; 10\log_{10}(1.5) + 10\log_{10}(4^{N})
$$

Using $10\log_{10}(1.5) = 1.7609$ and $10\log_{10}(4) = 6.0206$:

$$
\boxed{\;\text{SNR}_{dB} \;=\; 6.02\,N + 1.76 \quad \blacksquare\;}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 вҖ” ENOB from Measured SNR

### Step 1 вҖ” Start from the ideal-SNR equation

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{ideal}} \;=\; 6.02\,N_{\text{ideal}} + 1.76
$$

### Step 2 вҖ” Replace ideal N with the effective number of bits

For a *real* DAC, the effective resolution is $\text{ENOB}$, and the measured SNR captures all non-idealities:

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{measured}} \;=\; 6.02 \cdot \text{ENOB} + 1.76
$$

### Step 3 вҖ” Solve for ENOB algebraically

$$
6.02 \cdot \text{ENOB} \;=\; \text{SNR}_{\text{measured}} - 1.76
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{ENOB} \;=\; \frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{measured}} - 1.76}{6.02} \quad \blacksquare\;}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 вҖ” Settling Time with Slew-Rate Limitation

Consider a unity-gain output buffer with slew rate $SR$ and dominant output pole at frequency $f_{-3dB} = 1/(2\pi\tau)$, driving a full-scale step $\Delta V$.

### Step 1 вҖ” Slew-limited phase

The output cannot change faster than $SR$. Time required to traverse the linear region:

$$
t_{\text{slew}} \;=\; \frac{\Delta V}{SR}
$$

### Step 2 вҖ” Linear settling phase

After the slew phase, the output enters the small-signal exponential regime. The output evolves as:

$$
V_{OUT}(t) \;=\; V_{\text{final}} - \Delta V \cdot e^{-t/\tau}
$$

### Step 3 вҖ” Impose the ВұОө settling criterion

The error at time $t_{\text{linear}}$ must satisfy:

$$
\Delta V \cdot e^{-t_{\text{linear}}/\tau} \;\leq\; \varepsilon
$$

For an N-bit DAC, $\varepsilon = 0.5 \cdot \text{LSB} = 0.5 \cdot V_{REF}/2^{N}$.

### Step 4 вҖ” Solve for the linear settling time

$$
e^{-t_{\text{linear}}/\tau} \;\leq\; \frac{\varepsilon}{\Delta V}
$$

$$
-\frac{t_{\text{linear}}}{\tau} \;\leq\; \ln\!\left(\frac{\varepsilon}{\Delta V}\right)
$$

$$
t_{\text{linear}} \;\geq\; \tau \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta V}{\varepsilon}\right)
$$

### Step 5 вҖ” Combine both phases

$$
\boxed{\;t_{s} \;=\; \frac{\Delta V}{SR} + \tau \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta V}{\varepsilon}\right) \quad \blacksquare\;}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical вҖ” 8-bit DAC, SNR & ENOB

> [!TIP]
> This is the *most-asked* numerical in KTU Module 4. Practice variations of this.

**Given:** An 8-bit ideal DAC with $V_{REF} = 5.0$ V, driven by a full-scale sinusoid.

### (a) Compute LSB and V_FS

$$
\text{LSB} \;=\; \frac{V_{REF}}{2^{8}} \;=\; \frac{5.0}{256} \;=\; 19.53125 \text{ mV}
$$

$$
V_{FS} \;=\; V_{REF}\!\left(1 - \frac{1}{256}\right) \;=\; 5.0 \times 0.99609 \;=\; 4.98047 \text{ V}
$$

### (b) Compute ideal SNR

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{ideal}} \;=\; 6.02 \times 8 + 1.76 \;=\; 48.16 + 1.76 \;=\; 49.92 \text{ dB}
$$

### (c) A measured SNR of 46 dB вҶ’ find ENOB

$$
\text{ENOB} \;=\; \frac{46 - 1.76}{6.02} \;=\; \frac{44.24}{6.02} \;=\; 7.349 \text{ bits} \;\approx\; 7.35 \text{ bits}
$$

### (d) Lost bits due to non-ideality

$$
\Delta N \;=\; 8 - 7.35 \;=\; 0.65 \text{ bits}
$$

This 0.65-bit loss directly indicates the *aggregate penalty* from thermal noise, finite INL, and clock jitter.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical вҖ” Settling Time

**Given:** Output buffer with $SR = 10$ V/Вөs, output pole $\tau = 50$ ns, $\Delta V = 5$ V (full-scale), target $\varepsilon = 0.5$ LSB for a 10-bit DAC.

### Step 1 вҖ” Compute Оө

$$
\varepsilon \;=\; 0.5 \cdot \frac{5}{2^{10}} \;=\; 0.5 \cdot 4.883 \text{ mV} \;=\; 2.441 \text{ mV}
$$

### Step 2 вҖ” Slew phase

$$
t_{\text{slew}} \;=\; \frac{5}{10 \times 10^{6}} \;=\; 0.5\;\mu\text{s}
$$

### Step 3 вҖ” Linear settling phase

$$
t_{\text{linear}} \;=\; 50\,\text{ns} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{5}{0.002441}\right) \;=\; 50\,\text{ns} \cdot \ln(2048)
$$

$$
\ln(2048) \;=\; \ln(2^{11}) \;=\; 11 \cdot \ln 2 \;=\; 11 \times 0.6931 \;=\; 7.624
$$

$$
t_{\text{linear}} \;=\; 50 \times 7.624 \;=\; 381.2 \text{ ns}
$$

### Step 4 вҖ” Total settling time

$$
\boxed{\;t_s \;=\; 500 \text{ ns} + 381.2 \text{ ns} \;=\; 881.2 \text{ ns} \;\approx\; 0.88\;\mu\text{s}\;}
$$

---

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of DAC Specification Influence

The diagram below maps how each specification interacts with the others and with the input signal. Use this for KTU "Discuss the trade-offs" type 7-mark sub-questions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    inputA["Digital Input Code"]
    inputB["Clock f_s"]
    inputC["V_REF"]

    subgraph staticDC["Static DC Domain"]
        res["Resolution N"]
        lsb["LSB Weight"]
        fs["Full Scale V_FS"]
        dnl["DNL"]
        inl["INL"]
        mono["Monotonicity Check"]
        off["Offset Error"]
        gain["Gain Error"]
    end

    subgraph dynamicAC["Dynamic AC Domain"]
        sett["Settling Time t_s"]
        slew["Slew Rate SR"]
        glitch["Glitch Energy"]
        sfdr["SFDR"]
        snr["SNR / SNDR"]
        enob["ENOB"]
        thd["THD"]
        dr["Dynamic Range"]
    end

    inputA --> res
    res --> lsb
    inputC --> lsb
    lsb --> fs
    fs --> sett
    fs --> snr
    fs --> sfdr

    res --> mono
    dnl --> mono
    inl --> mono

    dnl --> inl
    lsb --> dnl

    inputA --> glitch
    inputB --> sett
    inputB --> slew

    slew --> sett
    sett --> enob
    snr --> enob
    thd --> sfdr
    sfdr --> enob
    snr --> dr

    off --> mono
    gain --> fs

    staticDC -. degrades .-> dynamicAC
```

## 4.2 Major-Carry Transition (MSB Glitch Source)

A conceptual functional view of the most problematic code transition вҖ” `0111...1` вҶ’ `1000...1` вҖ” which simultaneously turns *off* many low-order cells and turns *on* the MSB. Timing skew between the switches causes the glitch impulse.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Code 0111...1] -->|clock rising edge| B[All low cells ON]
    A -->|clock rising edge| C[MSB OFF]
    C -->|skew delay tau_d| D[MSB turns ON]
    B -->|simultaneous| E[All low cells turn OFF]
    D --> F[Final state 1000...1]
    E --> F
    E -.->|during skew tau_d| G[Transient Overshoot GLITCH]
    D -.-> G
    G --> H[Settling back to ideal value]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** The glitch in the flow occurs during the *intermediate* time interval when the low-order cells have switched off but the MSB has not yet switched on. The net output momentarily *droops* below the ideal new value вҖ” a classic "negative glitch" at the major carry.

## 4.3 Specification Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    root["DAC Specifications"]
    root --> S1["Static DC"]
    root --> S2["Dynamic AC"]
    root --> S3["Mixed Signal"]

    S1 --> S1a["Resolution / LSB / FS"]
    S1 --> S1b["Offset Error"]
    S1 --> S1c["Gain Error"]
    S1 --> S1d["INL / DNL"]
    S1 --> S1e["Monotonicity"]

    S2 --> S2a["Settling Time"]
    S2 --> S2b["Slew Rate"]
    S2 --> S2c["Glitch Energy"]
    S2 --> S2d["Update Rate"]

    S3 --> S3a["SNR / SNDR"]
    S3 --> S3b["SFDR"]
    S3 --> S3c["THD"]
    S3 --> S3d["ENOB"]
    S3 --> S3e["Dynamic Range"]
```

## 4.4 Trade-off Matrix вҖ” Architecture vs. Specification

A textual block-level representation (since a true Mermaid table of trade-offs is not natively supported) for a 14-mark design question:

| Specification | Current-Steering | R-2R Ladder | Charge-Redistribution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Resolution** | 8вҖ“14 bit (limited by matching) | 8вҖ“12 bit | 8вҖ“10 bit |
| **Speed** | Very high (>100 MS/s) | Medium | Low (switched-cap) |
| **INL/DNL** | Excellent with calibration | Moderate | Good |
| **Glitch** | **High** (MSB switch) | Moderate | Low (intrinsic) |
| **Monotonicity** | Guaranteed if segmented | Not guaranteed | Guaranteed |
| **Power** | High (static current) | Low | Low |

> [!TIP]
> KTU frequently asks: "Why is a **segmented** current-steering DAC used in high-speed video applications?" вҖ” answer combines SFDR + glitch + monotonicity trade-offs.

---

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are modeled on the **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** pattern. Marks indicated are the official KTU weightages (3-mark short answer, 7+7 sub-parts for the 14-mark module question).

---

## Part A вҖ” Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam вҖ” May 2024]`
**Define the term Spurious-Free Dynamic Range (SFDR) of a DAC. Why is it a critical specification in communication transceivers?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

SFDR is defined as the ratio, expressed in dBc, of the **RMS amplitude of the fundamental output tone** to the **RMS amplitude of the largest spurious component** (harmonic, intermodulation product, or clock feedthrough) appearing in the output spectrum of the DAC, when driven by a single-tone sinusoid.

$$
\text{SFDR} \;=\; 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{1}}{V_{\text{spur,max}}}\right) \text{ dBc}
$$

**Why it is critical in communication transceivers (2 marks):**
- The largest spur acts as a *co-channel interferer* and can desensitize the receiver.
- It directly limits the **bit-error rate (BER)** of the transmitted symbol.
- A high SFDR (>80 dBc) is mandatory for multi-carrier systems (e.g., LTE, 5G) to avoid spectral regrowth into adjacent channels.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam вҖ” Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between Integral Non-Linearity (INL) and Differential Non-Linearity (DNL). How are they mathematically related?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | DNL | INL |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Deviation of an individual step from the ideal 1-LSB step | Deviation of the cumulative transfer characteristic from the ideal straight line |
| Local vs. global | **Local** (code-by-code) | **Global** (cumulative) |
| Formula | $\text{DNL}(k) = \dfrac{V(k) - V(k-1) - V_{LSB}}{V_{LSB}}$ | $\text{INL}(k) = \dfrac{V(k) - V_{\text{ideal}}(k)}{V_{LSB}}$ |

**Mathematical relationship (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{INL}(k) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{DNL}(i)\;}
$$

That is, INL at code *k* is the *running sum* of all DNL values from code 1 up to *k*. Conversely, DNL is the *first difference* of INL:

$$
\text{DNL}(k) \;=\; \text{INL}(k) - \text{INL}(k-1)
$$

---

## Part B вҖ” Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> Choose **ONE** of the two alternatives. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

### вң… Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam вҖ” May 2024, Module 4]`

**(a) Derive the theoretical expression for the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) of an N-bit ideal DAC driven by a full-scale sinusoid. Show every step clearly. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution вҖ” Full Working (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the quantization error $e_q(t) = \hat{x}(t) - x(t)$, bounded by $\pm \Delta/2$ where $\Delta = V_{LSB} = V_{REF}/2^N$. Assume $e_q$ is uniformly distributed with PDF $p(e_q) = 1/\Delta$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the mean-square quantization noise:

$$
\overline{e_{q}^{2}} \;=\; \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_{q}^{2}\cdot\frac{1}{\Delta}\,de_{q} \;=\; \frac{\Delta^{2}}{12}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute the full-scale sinusoidal signal power. For a sinusoid of peak amplitude $V_{REF}/2$:

$$
V_{\text{sig,RMS}} \;=\; \frac{V_{REF}}{2\sqrt{2}} \quad\Rightarrow\quad P_{\text{sig}} \;=\; \frac{V_{REF}^{2}}{8}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Form the SNR ratio:

$$
\text{SNR} \;=\; \frac{P_{\text{sig}}}{\overline{e_{q}^{2}}} \;=\; \frac{V_{REF}^{2}/8}{\Delta^{2}/12} \;=\; \frac{3}{2}\left(\frac{V_{REF}}{\Delta}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Substitute $\Delta = V_{REF}/2^N$:

$$
\text{SNR} \;=\; \frac{3}{2}\cdot 4^{N} \;=\; 1.5 \cdot 4^{N}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Convert to decibels:

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} \;=\; 10\log_{10}(1.5) + 10 N \log_{10}(4)
$$

$$
=\; 1.76 + 6.02\,N
$$

**Final Result (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{\;\text{SNR}_{\text{ideal}} \;=\; 6.02\,N + 1.76 \;\text{dB}\;}
$$

---

**(b) For a 12-bit DAC with $V_{REF} = 4.096$ V, calculate the LSB, full-scale output, ideal SNR, and ENOB if the measured SNR is 70 dB. Comment on the bit-loss. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** LSB:

$$
\text{LSB} \;=\; \frac{4.096}{2^{12}} \;=\; \frac{4.096}{4096} \;=\; 1.0 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Full-scale output:

$$
V_{FS} \;=\; 4.096 \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{4096}\right) \;=\; 4.096 - 0.001 \;=\; 4.095 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Ideal SNR:

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{ideal}} \;=\; 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 \;=\; 72.24 + 1.76 \;=\; 74.0 \text{ dB}
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** ENOB from measured SNR:

$$
\text{ENOB} \;=\; \frac{70 - 1.76}{6.02} \;=\; \frac{68.24}{6.02} \;=\; 11.336 \text{ bits} \;\approx\; 11.34 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Bit loss:

$$
\Delta N \;=\; 12 - 11.34 \;=\; 0.66 \text{ bits}
$$

**Comment (included in step 5):** The 0.66-bit loss is due to non-idealities such as INL, finite output buffer bandwidth, thermal noise, clock jitter, and switch charge injection. Each 6 dB of SNR loss corresponds to exactly 1 effective bit lost.

---

### вң… Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam вҖ” Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a) Explain the concept of DAC monotonicity. What is the relationship between DNL and monotonicity? Show with a numerical example how a DNL of вҲ’1.5 LSB at a code breaks monotonicity. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** A DAC is **monotonic** if its analog output never *decreases* as the digital input code increases monotonically from 0 to 2вҒҝ вҲ’ 1. Monotonicity is essential in control loops, audio, and any closed-loop feedback system where a decreasing output for an increasing input would cause *positive feedback* and potential oscillation.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** **Relationship between DNL and monotonicity:**

For monotonicity, every step must be non-negative:

$$
V_{OUT}(k) - V_{OUT}(k-1) \;\geq\; 0
$$

From the DNL definition:

$$
\text{DNL}(k) \;=\; \frac{V_{OUT}(k) - V_{OUT}(k-1) - V_{LSB}}{V_{LSB}}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
V_{OUT}(k) - V_{OUT}(k-1) \;=\; V_{LSB}\cdot\bigl(1 + \text{DNL}(k)\bigr)
$$

For this to be $\geq 0$:

$$
\boxed{\;1 + \text{DNL}(k) \;\geq\; 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{DNL}(k) \;\geq\; -1 \text{ LSB}\;}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** **Numerical example вҖ” DNL = вҲ’1.5 LSB at code k = 64 (in a 10-bit DAC with V_REF = 1 V):**

- Ideal LSB = 1.0 V / 1024 = 0.977 mV
- Ideal step = 1 LSB = 0.977 mV
- Actual step = $V_{LSB} \cdot (1 + \text{DNL}) = 0.977 \times (1 - 1.5) = 0.977 \times (-0.5) = -0.488$ mV

So when the code increases from 63 вҶ’ 64, the analog output **decreases by 0.488 mV**, breaking monotonicity.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** **Consequence in a control loop:**

If this DAC drives the setpoint of a motor controller, then a step-up in command would *briefly slow down* the motor, triggering an error signal that *increases* the command further, leading to overshoot, instability, or even physical damage. This is why monotonicity is a *hard requirement* (often mandated by SIL standards) in industrial applications.

---

**(b) With a neat functional block diagram, describe the source of glitch impulse in a current-steering DAC. Explain two standard glitch-reduction techniques. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Glitch source вҖ” *Major-Carry Transition*:

In a binary-weighted current-steering DAC, when the input code transitions from `0111...1` to `1000...1` (the **major carry**), the following happens *simultaneously*:

- All lower-bit current sources (collectively equal to $V_{REF} \cdot (1 - 1/2^{N-1})$ worth of current) turn **OFF**.
- The MSB current source (worth $V_{REF}/2$) turns **ON**.

Because of finite, *unequal* switch turn-on and turn-off times, there is a small time window $\Delta t$ during which:

$$
\text{net current} \;=\; I_{MSB} - I_{\text{low bits (still partially ON)}} \;<\; I_{\text{ideal}}
$$

This causes a *transient dip* (or spike, depending on the asymmetry) in the output вҖ” the **glitch impulse**.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** *Functional Block Diagram (described verbally for transcription):*

```
       в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
       в”Ӯ  Digital Input Latch         в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ  (synchronizes all bits)     в”Ӯ
       в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                    в”Ӯ
       в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
       в”Ӯ  Current-Source Array       в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ  (thermometer or binary)    в”Ӯ
       в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                    в”Ӯ
       в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
       в”Ӯ  Differential Switches      в”Ӯ  вҶҗ **GLITCH ORIGIN**
       в”Ӯ  (asymmetric switching)     в”Ӯ
       в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
                    в”Ӯ
       в”Ңв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв–јв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”җ
       в”Ӯ  Output Summation Node      в”Ӯ
       в”Ӯ  + Reconstruction Filter    в”Ӯ
       в””в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”ҳ
```

**Step 3 (2.5 marks):** **Glitch-reduction technique #1 вҖ” Segmentation (Thermometer + Binary Hybrid):**

Replace the upper M bits with a *thermometer-coded* array (2бҙ№ unit cells, only the lower M bits required to be active), and the lower (NвҲ’M) bits with binary-weighted cells.

- The thermometer MSBs switch in *sequence* (one-at-a-time), so the major carry no longer toggles a single large current source.
- Glitch energy is reduced *exponentially* with M.
- Trade-off: increased layout area and decoding logic.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** **Glitch-reduction technique #2 вҖ” Switch-Output Impedance Matching & Return-to-Zero (RZ):**

- **Impedance matching:** Ensure the drain impedance of the OFF and ON switches is *identical*, so the output common-mode is undisturbed. Achieved via cascoded switches and matched layout.
- **Return-to-Zero:** After each conversion, briefly drive the output back to zero before the next sample. This *de-correlates* the glitch from the signal, allowing the reconstruction filter to remove it.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout вҖ” Where students lose marks on DAC questions:**
> 1. **Confusing FSR with V_FS:** FSR is the *range* ($V_{REF}$); V_FS is the *max output* ($V_{REF} - 1$ LSB). Examiners explicitly test this.
> 2. **Using the wrong SNR constant:** 6.02 N + 1.76 is for *sinusoidal full-scale* input; for a *triangular* input it is 6.02 N вҲ’ 0.9 (a common KTU trick question).
> 3. **Forgetting units in DNL/INL:** Always state "in LSB" вҖ” bare numbers lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping derivation steps:** KTU awards marks for *intermediate* algebraic transitions. A final answer with no working gets вүӨ40% credit.
> 5. **Monotonicity criterion reversed:** Some students write DNL вүӨ 1 (upper bound) вҖ” that is for *no missing codes*, not monotonicity. The correct condition is **DNL вүҘ вҲ’1 LSB**.

---

## рҹ“Ң Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (for KTU ESE):**

- **LSB:** $V_{REF}/2^{N}$ вҖ” the *atomic* step of a DAC.
- **V_FS:** $V_{REF}(1 - 2^{-N})$ вҖ” note: *not* $V_{REF}$ itself.
- **Ideal SNR:** $6.02\,N + 1.76$ dB (sinusoid) or $6.02\,N - 0.9$ dB (triangle).
- **ENOB:** $(SNR - 1.76)/6.02$ вҖ” always express to **2 decimal places** in KTU answers.
- **DNL:** step-by-step deviation; **DNL вүҘ вҲ’1 LSB** вҮ’ monotonic.
- **INL:** $\sum DNL$ вҖ” global linearity drift; INL вүӨ Вұ0.5 LSB is the typical 12-bit acceptance criterion.
- **Monotonicity:** output never decreases for increasing code вҖ” *must be* guaranteed in control applications.
- **Settling time:** $t_s = \Delta V / SR + \tau \ln(\Delta V / \varepsilon)$ вҖ” has two distinct physical phases.
- **Glitch:** occurs at major-carry transition; **segmentation** is the gold-standard fix.
- **SFDR:** $20\log(V_1 / V_{\text{spur,max}})$ in dBc; dominant in RF/IF applications.
- **THD:** ratio of harmonic RMS to fundamental RMS; multiply by 100% for percentage form.
- **Dynamic Range:** $20\log(V_{FS}/V_{n,RMS})$ вҖ” sets the *smallest representable signal*.
- **Static vs. Dynamic:** static specs use slow ramped codes; dynamic specs use sampled or sinusoidal codes.
- **Architecture trade-off:** current-steering = fast but glitcy; R-2R = low-power but slow; charge-redist = inherently monotonic but bandwidth-limited.
- **Bit-loss math:** every 6 dB SNR loss = 1 effective bit lost.
- **Worst-case settling criterion:** Вұ0.5 LSB of the *target* code, not Вұ0.5 LSB of FSR.

> **Final Advice:** In any 14-mark DAC specification question, *always* include a labelled transfer characteristic, a numerical worked example, and a one-line engineering application. KTU examiners allocate **2 implicit marks** for diagram + numerical + application, even when not explicitly asked.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: ADC Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT755 - MIXED SIGNAL CIRCUIT DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ADC Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ADC Specifications** are the standardized set of static (DC) and dynamic (AC) performance parameters used to quantitatively characterize how faithfully an **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** translates a continuous-time, continuous-amplitude analog input signal into a discrete-time, discrete-amplitude digital output code.

These specifications are the **"Figure of Merit"** vocabulary that allows design engineers, system integrators, and examiners to compare two completely different ADC architectures (Flash, SAR, Pipeline, Sigma-Delta, Dual-Slope) on a common numerical scale.

The complete specification set is conventionally split into two domains:

| Domain | Evaluated When Input Is | Time Frame |
|---|---|---|
| **Static Specifications** | Constant (DC) or very slowly varying | Time-invariant behavior |
| **Dynamic Specifications** | Sinusoidal or wide-band | Time-varying / frequency-domain behavior |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy вҖ” "The Ruler and the Stopwatch"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 вҖ” The Ruler (Static Specs):**
> Imagine you are measuring the length of a table with two different rulers. Ruler A is marked in **millimeters (high resolution)**, but one of its millimeter marks is **missing or duplicated (DNL/INL error)**. Ruler B has fewer marks (lower resolution) but is **perfectly linear (low INL)**. Static specifications tell you exactly *how good your ruler is at every point along its length* вҖ” independently of whether the table is moving or not.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 вҖ” The Stopwatch (Dynamic Specs):**
> Now imagine measuring a *running* sprinter. Even if the ruler is perfect, if the stopwatch is sluggish (high **aperture delay**), if the trigger finger is shaky (**aperture jitter**), or if the display lags behind reality (**acquisition/settling time**), your measurement will be wrong. Dynamic specifications tell you how well the converter can keep up with a *changing* signal.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Full-Scale Sine Wave Input:** Amplitude = $V_{FS}$, RMS = $V_{FS}/\sqrt{2}$.
- **Quantization Step (LSB size):** $q = \Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^N}$ where $N$ is the number of bits.
- **Universal Reference:** For a pure sine input, the **ideal theoretical SNR = 6.02 N + 1.76 dB**. This is the **"Shannon-Hartley for ADCs"** benchmark.
- **Standard Loading:** Specifications are usually reported with input near full-scale, sampling frequency $f_S$, and input frequency $f_{in}$.

## 1.4 Why These Specs Matter in KTU Examinations

> [!WARNING]
> KTU frequently frames the same physical question in *three* different ways вҖ” asking for **ENOB** given measured SNDR, asking for **SNDR** given a list of distortion harmonics, or asking for **minimum N** to meet a system SNR budget. Mastering the *inter-conversion* between specifications is the single highest-weightage skill in this module.
